items.title: Gravitational Potential and Potential Energy
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one source mass fixed at the origin, a movable positive probe in a bounded two-dimensional gravity well, and optional overlays for the potential map, equal-potential contour circles, equal-distance rings, the local field arrow, the force arrow on the probe, and the horizontal scan line used by the graphs.

Dragging the probe changes the sampled location directly on the stage. The same location is used by the readout and the graph cursor, so the map, the arrows, and the graphs always describe the same point.

Very near the source mass, the display uses a minimum sampling radius so the well depth, arrows, and graphs stay finite and readable. This keeps the visualization bounded while preserving the correct trend that potential becomes more negative and the field grows rapidly near the source.

The potential-energy-scan graph plots gravitational potential and the chosen probe's potential energy along the current horizontal scan line. Hovering the graph previews the same x-location on the stage.

The field-link graph plots the horizontal gravitational field component and the matching negative slope of the potential graph along that same scan line. Changing the probe mass rescales the energy curve, but the field-link graph remains a source-mass and geometry readout.

equations.meaning: This distance sets both the well depth and the field strength.

equations.label: Potential from one source mass
equations.meaning: With zero defined at infinity, the potential from one source mass is negative and becomes more negative as r gets smaller.
notes: This bounded lab fixes G = 1 in the displayed units so the readouts emphasize sign, scaling, and geometry.

equations.label: Potential energy of the probe mass
equations.meaning: Probe mass turns the source-set potential into potential energy without changing the potential itself.

equations.label: Field from potential
equations.meaning: The field points downhill on the potential landscape, from higher φ toward lower φ.

equations.label: Horizontal scan-line link
equations.meaning: On the current horizontal slice, the x-component of the field is the negative slope of φ(x).

equations.label: Inverse-square field trend
equations.meaning: Potential changes like 1/r, but field strength changes faster like 1/r².

variableLinks.description: Deepens or shallows the whole well, so φ, U, and |g| all scale from the same source mass at a fixed point.
Moves the probe left or right along the current scan line, so the stage and graphs sample the same x-location.
Moves the probe to a different horizontal slice of the same well, changing both the readout and the graphs.
Changes only the probe response, so U and the force scale while φ and g stay fixed at the same point.

controls.description: Changes the source mass creating the well, so both phi and |g| scale from the same cause.
Moves the probe left or right across both the stage and the linked scan graphs.
Moves the probe to a different horizontal scan line so the graphs sample a different slice through the well.
Changes the potential energy and probe force without changing the source-set potential or field.

presets.label: Axis 1 m
presets.description: A 1 m on-axis probe makes the potential and field ratios easy to compare against later distance changes.

presets.label: Axis 2 m
presets.description: At 2 m on the same axis, phi and U should halve in magnitude while |g| falls to one quarter of the 1 m case.

presets.label: Heavier source
presets.description: Doubling the source mass deepens the well and strengthens the field at the same probe point.

presets.label: Heavier probe
presets.description: A larger probe mass changes U and the force while the potential well stays fixed.

presets.label: Overhead well
presets.description: A point directly above the source makes the contour circles and downhill field direction especially easy to read.

overlays.label: Potential map
overlays.shortDescription: Shows φ as a gravity well: deeper near the source and shallower farther away.
whatToNotice: Farther from the source, φ rises toward zero.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the sign and depth of potential visible on the stage instead of leaving φ as an isolated number.

overlays.label: Potential contours
overlays.shortDescription: Draws equal-potential circles around the source mass for several negative phi values.
whatToNotice: The field arrow crosses the contour circles instead of running along them.
overlays.whyItMatters: Contours help you read potential as one scalar landscape and the field as its downhill direction.

overlays.label: Distance rings
overlays.shortDescription: Marks equal-distance circles around the source so 1/r and 1/r^2 comparisons stay geometric instead of abstract.
whatToNotice: The 1 m, 2 m, and 3 m rings let you compare potential and field changes at matching radial steps.
overlays.whyItMatters: Distance rings make the difference between halving phi and quartering |g| much easier to see before you calculate.

overlays.label: Field arrow
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the local gravitational field as the downhill direction on the potential landscape.
whatToNotice: The field arrow always points toward lower φ and therefore toward the source mass.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps field and potential tied to the same live probe state.

overlays.label: Force arrow
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the force on the chosen positive probe mass without redefining the well itself.
whatToNotice: Changing only the probe mass rescales this arrow while the potential map and field arrow stay fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the source-generated well from the response of the chosen probe.

overlays.label: Graph scan line
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the horizontal slice sampled by both graphs.
whatToNotice: Changing the probe height changes the graph because the graph samples a new horizontal slice through the same well.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the graph-stage link honest so the potential and slope curves clearly come from one sampled slice on the stage.

graphs.label: Potential and energy on the scan line
graphs.description: Shows how φ and the chosen probe's potential energy change along the current horizontal slice.

graphs.label: Field-slope link on the scan line
graphs.description: Shows that g_x matches the negative slope of the potential graph along the same slice.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the potential well
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Each one connects the map, the contour circles, and the scan graphs for the same live probe position.

items.text: Moving the probe inward makes φ more negative quickly. The well becomes much deeper near the source.
items.tryThis: Start on Axis 2 m and drag inward to Axis 1 m. Watch the readout, map, and scan curve deepen together.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you read potential as well depth before mixing in force or energy.

items.text: Farther from the source, φ rises toward zero while staying negative because this model defines zero at infinity.
items.tryThis: Use the Axis 2 m preset, then move even farther out and watch the readout approach zero from below.
items.whyItMatters: It explains the negative sign as a reference choice, not as a change of force direction.

items.text: On this horizontal slice, the g_x curve matches the negative slope of φ(x).
items.tryThis: Find a place where φ increases as x increases. There the slope is positive, so g_x must be negative.
items.whyItMatters: This is the bridge from scalar potential to vector field.

items.text: Changing only m_test leaves φ and g alone but rescales U and the force on the probe immediately.
items.tryThis: Keep the probe fixed, then drag the probe-mass slider upward. Watch U change while the potential map and field-link graph stay put.
items.whyItMatters: It cleanly separates the source-generated well from the response of one chosen object placed in that well.
It makes the difference between geometry changes and probe-response changes visible without leaving the same compact model.

predictionMode.title: Predict the well and the response
predictionMode.intro: Predict what changes before you move the sliders. Each scenario tests one cause at a time: source mass, distance, or probe mass.

items.prompt: Starting from Axis 1 m, what should happen if the probe moves to 2 m on the same horizontal line while the masses stay fixed?

choices.label: The magnitudes of phi and U fall to about one half, while the field magnitude falls to about one quarter.
The magnitudes of phi, U, and |g| all fall to one half because the distance doubled.
Only the field changes because potential depends on source mass but not on distance.

items.explanation: For one source mass, φ scales like -1/r, so doubling r halves the well depth. With the same probe mass, U halves too. But |g| follows 1/r², so it falls to one quarter.
items.observationHint: Use the distance rings and compare the potential scan with the field-link graph together.

items.prompt: At one fixed probe point, what must happen if the source mass doubles while the probe mass and geometry stay fixed?

choices.label: The well gets deeper, so phi and U become twice as negative in magnitude and the field magnitude doubles.
Only the field doubles because potential ignores source mass.
The sign of phi flips because the source mass got larger.

items.explanation: Source mass sets the scale of the whole one-mass model. Doubling M doubles φ, U, and |g| in magnitude at the same point while keeping the same negative-sign and inward-direction meanings.
items.observationHint: The map deepens, the readout becomes more negative, and the field arrow grows without changing its inward direction.

items.prompt: If only the probe mass doubles at the same point, which statement is most defensible?

choices.label: The potential and field stay the same, but U and the force on the probe double in magnitude.
The potential well deepens because there is now more total mass in the interaction.
The field direction flips because the probe is now heavier.

items.explanation: The source mass and distance define φ and g. Changing only m_test multiplies the existing potential to make U and multiplies the existing field to make force, but it does not rewrite the source-generated well.
items.observationHint: The potential map and field-link graph stay fixed while the U curve and force readout grow.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Tune the same one-mass gravity well into compact targets. The checks read the live potential, energy, and field state instead of a detached answer sheet.

items.title: Half the potential, quarter the field
items.prompt: Starting from Axis 1 m, move the probe to the doubled-distance case on the same horizontal line so phi is about half as deep and the field magnitude is about one quarter as large.
items.successMessage: You kept the one-mass model honest: doubling the distance halved the depth of the potential well while the inverse-square field fell to about one quarter.

setup.note: Keep the source mass and probe mass fixed. Use the rings, the potential scan, and the field-link graph together.

hints: Stay on the horizontal axis so the comparison is only about distance, not about a new probe direction.
Potential follows 1/r, but the field follows 1/r^2. Do not aim for the same ratio on both readouts.

targets.label: Keep probex between 1.95 m and 2.05 m.
Keep probey between -0.05 m and 0.05 m.
Keep potential between -1.05 and -0.95.
Keep potentialenergy between -1.05 and -0.95.
Keep fieldmagnitude between 0.48 and 0.52.

items.title: Double the energy, not the potential
items.successMessage: You changed the probe response without rewriting the well: U became more negative in proportion to m_test while phi and g stayed fixed.

setup.note: Leave the source mass and probe position alone. Only the probe mass should move.

hints: If phi should stay fixed, do not touch the source mass or the probe coordinates.
Use U = m_test phi. Doubling m_test doubles the energy magnitude at the same point.

targets.label: Keep testmass between 1.95 kg and 2.05 kg.
Keep potential between -1.01 and -0.99.
Keep potentialenergy between -2.02 and -1.98.
Keep fieldmagnitude between 0.49 and 0.51.
Keep forcemagnitude between 0.99 and 1.01.

items.title: Escape Velocity
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

equations.label: Specific total energy
equations.meaning: The sign of this quantity decides the outcome: negative is bound, zero is threshold, and positive escapes.

equations.label: Threshold condition
equations.meaning: This is the boundary between bound and escaping launches at the chosen starting radius.

equations.label: Escape speed
equations.meaning: A deeper well needs a larger threshold speed, while a shallower starting point needs a smaller one.

equations.label: Circular-speed comparison
equations.meaning: Local circular speed is a useful benchmark, but exceeding it alone does not guarantee escape.

equations.label: Finite turnaround radius
equations.meaning: When $E < 0$, this gives the maximum radius before the bound launch must turn around.

variableLinks.description: Deepens the gravity well, so both the local escape speed and the local circular-speed comparison rise.

variableLinks.label: Launch radius
variableLinks.description: Starting farther out means the potential is less negative, so less launch speed is needed to reach the threshold.
Changes only the launch kinetic term relative to the escape threshold, so it directly controls whether the start is bound, threshold, or escaping.

controls.description: Changes the depth of the gravity well and therefore the local escape threshold.

controls.label: Launch radius
controls.description: Sets how far from the source the outward launch begins. A larger starting radius means a shallower well at launch.
1.00 means the launch speed exactly matches the escape threshold for the current source mass and launch radius. Below 1 is bound; above 1 escapes.

presets.label: Threshold launch
presets.description: The launch starts exactly at escape speed, so the total specific energy sits on zero.

presets.label: High but bound
presets.description: This launch goes far above the start, but it still stays below escape speed and keeps a finite turnaround radius.

presets.label: Clear escape
presets.description: A launch above threshold keeps positive total specific energy, so it never needs a finite turnaround radius.

presets.label: Heavier source threshold
presets.description: At the same launch radius, a heavier source raises the threshold speed while the total-energy line still sits on zero.

presets.label: Wider launch threshold
presets.description: Launching farther from the source lowers the threshold speed because the well is shallower there.

overlays.label: Launch marker
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the starting radius used by the threshold equations and the trail.
whatToNotice: Moving the launch point outward lowers the threshold speed immediately because the starting potential is less negative.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps $r_0$ visible as the launch condition.

overlays.label: Turnaround marker
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the predicted finite turnaround radius for bound launches.
whatToNotice: This marker appears only when the total specific energy is negative.
At threshold or above, there is no finite turnaround radius to mark.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates a long outward trip from a true escape.

overlays.label: Velocity vector
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current radial motion of the launched mass.
whatToNotice: A large outward speed at one instant is not enough to prove escape.
The energy sign still decides whether the launch must reverse later.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the speed comparison tied to the same moving state.

overlays.label: Gravity vector
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the inward gravitational pull that keeps slowing the launch even in escaping cases.
whatToNotice: Gravity keeps pointing inward even for threshold and escape launches.
overlays.whyItMatters: It corrects the idea that escape means gravity disappears.

overlays.label: Trajectory trail
overlays.shortDescription: Leaves the visited radial path so the difference between continuing outward and turning back stays visible over time.
whatToNotice: A long outward trail is not proof of escape. Bound launches can travel far before turning back.
overlays.whyItMatters: It lets the motion and the energy graph tell the same story.

graphs.label: Radius and launch radius
graphs.description: Shows whether the launch keeps moving outward or approaches a finite maximum radius before turning back.

graphs.label: Actual speed and threshold speeds
graphs.description: Compares the live speed with the local escape-speed and circular-speed benchmarks at the same radius.

graphs.label: Specific energy balance
graphs.description: Shows whether the total specific energy stays below zero, reaches zero, or becomes positive.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice about escape
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to separate three ideas that are easy to mix up: going far from the source, being above circular speed, and actually escaping.

items.text: The total-energy line gives the verdict: below zero is bound, zero is threshold, and above zero escapes.
items.tryThis: Switch between Threshold launch, High but bound, and Clear escape with the specific-energy graph open.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps escape tied to the energy sign instead of to distance alone.

items.text: A launch can be above the local circular-speed curve and still remain bound if it stays below escape speed and keeps negative total specific energy.
items.tryThis: Apply High but bound and compare the speed-threshold graph with the specific-energy graph before deciding whether the launch truly escapes.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the local circular benchmark from the actual escape threshold.

items.text: At the same launch radius, increasing source mass deepens the well and raises the threshold speed.
items.tryThis: Use Heavier source threshold and watch the launch-speed readout rise with the escape-speed curve while the total-energy line stays on zero.
items.whyItMatters: It ties the threshold directly to the depth of the gravity well.

items.text: Launching farther from the source lowers the threshold speed because the starting point sits higher in the gravitational well.
items.tryThis: Use Wider launch threshold and compare the new launch marker with the lower threshold speed and the less-negative starting potential energy.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the radius dependence into an energy-depth story instead of a memorized formula.

predictionMode.title: Predict the escape outcome
predictionMode.intro: Predict the energy verdict before you apply the preset. Then use the speed-threshold and specific-energy graphs to check the result.

items.prompt: At the same launch radius, what must happen if the source mass increases while the speed factor stays at 1.00?

choices.label: The threshold speed rises, but the total-energy line stays near zero because the actual launch speed scales with the escape-speed target.
Only the gravity arrow changes because escape speed does not depend on source mass.
The launch becomes bound immediately because a heavier source must make the same speed factor too small.

items.explanation: With speed factor fixed at 1.00, the actual launch speed updates with the new escape threshold. A heavier source makes the well deeper and raises $v_{	ext{esc}}$, but the total specific energy still stays at zero.
items.observationHint: The launch-speed readout rises with the escape-speed curve, while the total-energy line stays on zero.

items.prompt: At the same source mass, what should happen if the launch radius increases while the speed factor stays at 1.00?

choices.label: The threshold speed decreases because the launch begins in a shallower part of the gravity well, but the total-energy line still stays near zero.
The threshold speed increases because larger radii always need more speed to keep moving outward.
Nothing important changes because the same source mass is still present.

items.explanation: Increasing $r_0$ makes the starting potential less negative, so less launch speed is needed to reach the zero-energy threshold. At speed factor 1.00 the total specific energy still stays at zero.
items.observationHint: The launch marker moves outward, the threshold speed drops, and the total-energy line stays near zero.

items.prompt: A launch starts faster than the local circular-speed comparison but still below the escape speed. Which statement is most defensible?
items.scenarioLabel: Fast enough to climb, not enough to escape

choices.label: It can rise far from the source and still remain bound, because negative total specific energy guarantees a finite turnaround radius.
It must escape because any speed above circular speed is automatically above escape speed.
It should settle into a circular orbit because circular speed is the main orbit threshold.

items.explanation: Escape depends on the sign of $E/m$, not on whether the launch exceeds the local circular-speed comparison. In this page the motion is radial, not a sideways orbit.
items.observationHint: The speed-threshold graph can show the launch above circular speed while the specific-energy graph still stays below zero.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same one-source launch bench to build bound, threshold, and escaping cases from the live energy logic.

items.title: Remove the finite turnaround
items.prompt: Starting from High but bound, raise the launch just to the threshold case at the same source mass and launch radius so the total specific energy is about zero and the finite turnaround disappears.
items.successMessage: You reached the threshold case: the total-energy line climbed to zero without changing the source mass or launch radius, so the finite turnaround is gone.

setup.note: Keep the same source mass and launch radius. Only the launch speed should move.

hints: At fixed source mass and launch radius, the threshold case is the place where the total-energy line lands on zero.
Do not change the source mass or launch radius.

targets.label: Keep sourcemass between 3.95 kg and 4.05 kg.
Keep launchradius between 1.55 m and 1.65 m.
Keep speedfactor between 0.99 and 1.01.
Keep totalenergy between -0.02 and 0.02.
Keep launchspeed between 2.22 m/s and 2.25 m/s.

items.title: High climb, still bound
items.prompt: Starting from Threshold launch, tune a launch that still begins from 1.6 m around a 4 kg source, climbs high, but remains bound with a finite turnaround near 10.4 m.
items.successMessage: You built the high-but-bound case honestly: the outward trip is large, but the negative total-energy line still predicts a finite turnaround.

setup.note: Keep the same source mass and launch radius, then lower the launch speed just enough to restore a finite turnaround.

hints: A high bound trip still needs negative total specific energy.
Aim below the escape threshold but not so low that the turnaround collapses back toward the launch point.

targets.label: Keep sourcemass between 3.95 kg and 4.05 kg.
Keep launchradius between 1.55 m and 1.65 m.
Keep speedfactor between 0.91 and 0.93.
Keep totalenergy between -0.4 and -0.36.
Keep turnaroundradius between 10.2 m and 10.6 m.

items.title: Circular Orbits and Orbital Speed
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one source mass at the center, one moving satellite launched from the right side of the chosen reference circle, and optional overlays for the dashed reference orbit, the current radius line, the tangent velocity vector, the inward gravity vector, and the trajectory trail.

Changing source mass, reference radius, or speed factor updates the same orbit path, readouts, and graphs together. The main comparison is whether the live path stays on the reference circle or peels inside or outside it because gravity and the turning requirement no longer match.

controls.description: Changes the mass creating the inward gravity.
Sets the dashed reference circle used for the circular-orbit target.
1.00 means the actual speed equals the circular-orbit speed for the chosen mass and radius.

presets.label: Reference orbit
presets.description: A balanced circular-orbit case with the speed factor set exactly to 1.

presets.label: Inner faster circle
presets.description: A smaller circular orbit around the same source mass needs a larger circular speed.

presets.label: Outer slower circle
presets.description: A wider circular orbit lowers the circular speed and lengthens the circular period.

presets.label: Too slow
presets.description: Dropping below the circular speed makes gravity stronger than the turning requirement, so the path bends inward.

presets.label: Too fast
presets.description: A speed above the circular value makes the path open outward because gravity cannot bend it tightly enough.

presets.label: Heavier source
presets.description: At the same radius, a heavier source mass demands a larger circular speed and a shorter circular period.

overlays.label: Reference orbit
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the dashed circle for the chosen circular-orbit target.
whatToNotice: Only the right speed keeps the live path riding on this dashed circle.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the chosen circular target from the actual path taken when the speed factor is too low or too high.

overlays.label: Radius line
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current source-to-satellite radius.
whatToNotice: The same current radius appears in the live readout and in the linked balance relations.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the geometric meaning of r visible while the orbit drifts away from the reference circle.

overlays.label: Velocity vector
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current tangent direction and speed of the orbiting body.
whatToNotice: At the circular condition, the speed matches the needed turn. Away from that condition, the same tangent direction leads to inward or outward drift.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the sideways-motion part of orbital balance visible instead of treating speed as a hidden number.

overlays.label: Gravity vector
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the inward gravitational acceleration that bends the path.
whatToNotice: This is the centripetal acceleration source in the orbit problem, not an extra force added on top.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the link between gravity and centripetal acceleration explicit.

overlays.label: Trajectory trail
overlays.shortDescription: Leaves the actual path behind the moving body over the current time window.
whatToNotice: A correct circular speed keeps the trail on the dashed circle, while too little or too much speed makes the trail peel away.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the orbit consequence visible over time instead of hiding it in one instant.

graphs.label: Radius over time
graphs.description: Shows whether the live path stays on the chosen circular radius or drifts inward or outward over time.

graphs.label: Actual speed and circular speed
graphs.description: Compares the actual speed with the circular speed required at the current radius.

graphs.label: Gravity and turning requirement
graphs.description: Shows directly whether gravity matches, exceeds, or falls short of the turning requirement $v^2/r$.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to compare the live path with the dashed reference circle. The central question is always the same: does gravity match the turning requirement at the current radius?

items.text: Notice that the trail stays on the dashed reference circle only when the speed factor is very close to 1.00.
items.tryThis: Leave the speed factor at 1.00, then scrub the radius graph. The live radius keeps returning to the same value because gravity and the turning requirement stay matched.
items.whyItMatters: This is the stable circular-orbit case the later formulas are describing.

items.text: Notice that lowering the speed factor below 1 makes gravity stronger than the turning requirement, so the path bends inside the dashed circle.
items.tryThis: Apply Too slow, then compare the trail with the acceleration-balance graph. The teal gravity curve sits above the coral v^2/r curve.
items.whyItMatters: It shows exactly why a circular orbit needs the right sideways speed instead of just any fast motion.

items.text: Notice that raising the speed factor above 1 makes the turning requirement exceed gravity, so the path opens outward from the dashed circle.
items.tryThis: Apply Too fast and compare the live speed curve with the circular-speed curve. The actual speed starts above the circular value, so the path cannot stay on the same radius.
items.whyItMatters: A faster orbit is not automatically more stable. It must still match the radius and the gravity at that location.

items.text: Changing the reference radius changes the needed circular speed and period together: wider circles move more slowly but take longer to complete.
items.tryThis: Switch between Inner faster circle and Outer slower circle while keeping the speed factor at 1. The dashed circle widens, the speed graph drops, and the circular period grows.
items.whyItMatters: This keeps the radius dependence from collapsing into a memorized formula.

items.text: The acceleration-balance graph is the core orbit check: a circular orbit happens when the gravity curve and the v^2/r curve sit on top of each other.
items.tryThis: Stay on Reference orbit first, then move the speed factor away from 1. The separation between the curves predicts whether the trail moves inward or outward.
items.whyItMatters: It makes the phrase "gravity supplies the centripetal acceleration" visible and testable.

items.text: Compare mode is strongest when both setups keep speed factor near 1 while only source mass or reference radius changes. Then the speed and period differences are honest consequences of the same orbit rule.
items.tryThis: Keep both setups circular, then make Setup B the smaller-radius orbit. The same source mass now produces the shorter-period, faster orbit in B.
items.whyItMatters: It isolates the mass and radius dependence without mixing in a separate "too slow" or "too fast" mistake.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the path will do before you run the case. Then use the stage and the balance graph to check whether gravity matches the turning requirement.

items.prompt: At the same reference radius, what must happen when the source mass increases while the speed factor stays at 1.00?

choices.label: The circular speed increases, the circular period shortens, and the trail can still stay on the dashed circle because the speed factor stayed at 1.
The orbit must bend inward immediately because heavier source mass always means the speed is now too low.

items.explanation: At fixed radius, stronger gravity raises the circular speed and shortens the circular period. Because the control here keeps the speed factor at 1, the actual speed rises with the new circular value and the path can remain circular.
items.observationHint: The dashed circle stays the same size, but the live speed and gravity values both rise while the circular period drops.

items.prompt: At the same source mass, what changes most clearly when the chosen circular orbit radius increases while the speed factor stays at 1.00?

choices.label: The circular speed decreases while the circular period increases, so the path stays circular but takes longer to go around.
The circular speed increases because larger circles always need more speed.
The period stays fixed because the speed factor did not change.

items.explanation: For circular orbits, v_c = sqrt(M/r) drops as radius grows, while T_c = 2pi sqrt(r^3/M) grows strongly with radius. Keeping the speed factor at 1 preserves the circular match at the new radius.
items.observationHint: The dashed circle gets wider, the speed graph shifts downward, and the circular period readout rises.

items.prompt: If the speed factor drops below 1.00 at one chosen reference radius, what should happen first?

choices.label: Gravity becomes stronger than the turning requirement, so the trail bends inside the dashed circle.
The path stays perfectly circular because only mass and radius matter.

items.explanation: Lowering the speed lowers v^2/r at that radius. Gravity is then larger than the inward acceleration needed for a circular turn, so the path bends inward.
items.observationHint: The teal gravity curve sits above the coral v^2/r curve, and the trail falls inside the dashed reference circle.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Tune one bounded gravity source into orbit-balance targets. The checks read the same live orbit state, overlays, and graphs instead of a detached answer sheet.

setup.note: Raise the source mass, then keep the speed factor near 1 so the trail stays on the dashed circle.

hints: At the same radius, a heavier source raises the circular speed rather than lowering it.
A circular orbit still needs the actual speed curve to match the circular-speed curve.

targets.label: Keep sourcemass between 5.1 kg and 5.3 kg.
Keep orbitradius between 1.55 m and 1.65 m.
Keep speedfactor between 0.99 and 1.01.
Keep actualspeed between 1.76 m/s and 1.84 m/s.
Keep period between 5.45 s and 5.7 s.

items.title: Inner orbit, faster compare
items.prompt: Open compare mode and keep both setups circular with the same source mass, but make Setup B the smaller-radius orbit so it moves faster and finishes sooner.
items.successMessage: You isolated the radius dependence honestly: the smaller circular orbit kept the same source mass but needed the faster speed and shorter period.

setup.note: Keep both setups near speed factor 1. The comparison should be about radius, not about being too slow or too fast.

hints: Leave the source mass matched in both setups so the comparison stays about radius alone.
The smaller-radius circular orbit should have the larger speed and the shorter period.

targets.label: Keep Setup A sourcemass between 3.95 kg and 4.05 kg.
Keep Setup B sourcemass between 3.95 kg and 4.05 kg.
Keep Setup A orbitradius between 1.95 m and 2.05 m.
Keep Setup B orbitradius between 1.05 m and 1.15 m.
Keep Setup A speedfactor between 0.99 and 1.01.
Keep Setup B speedfactor between 0.99 and 1.01.
Keep Setup B actualspeed between 1.85 m/s and 1.97 m/s.
Keep Setup B period between 3.5 s and 3.75 s.

items.title: Too slow falls inward
items.prompt: Starting from Reference orbit, lower the speed just enough that gravity is clearly stronger than the turning requirement and the path bends inside the dashed circle.
items.successMessage: You built the honest too-slow case: the gravity curve sits above the turning-requirement curve, so the trail falls inside the reference orbit.

setup.note: Keep the same source and radius first, then lower only the speed factor.

hints: A smaller speed lowers v^2/r at the same radius.
This target is about making gravity larger than the turning requirement, not about changing the source mass.

targets.label: Keep orbitradius between 1.55 m and 1.65 m.
Keep speedfactor between 0.84 and 0.86.
Keep actualspeed between 1.32 m/s and 1.37 m/s.
Keep gravityacceleration between 1.52 m/s^2 and 1.6 m/s^2.
Keep requiredcentripetalacceleration between 1.1 m/s^2 and 1.17 m/s^2.

items.title: Rolling Motion
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Change the downhill gravity component without changing the same no-slip rolling model.
Change the roller size. In this bounded model it mostly changes the angular-speed requirement, not the center-of-mass acceleration for a fixed shape.

controls.label: Mass distribution
controls.description: Slide from a center-loaded sphere-like response toward a rim-loaded hoop-like response, or land exactly on the named shape presets.

presets.label: Solid sphere
presets.description: Center-loaded enough to roll quickly while still splitting some energy into rotation.

presets.label: Solid cylinder
presets.description: The benchmark middle case for rolling without slipping on this incline.

presets.label: Hoop
presets.description: Rim-heavy mass distribution makes the center-of-mass acceleration noticeably smaller on the same incline.

presets.label: Steeper sphere
presets.description: A steeper angle raises the shared downhill pull, but the same sphere inertia factor still sets the translation-rotation split.

presets.label: Small fast-spin sphere
presets.description: The center keeps rolling with the same sphere response, but the smaller radius demands a larger angular speed.

presets.label: Custom rim-loaded
presets.description: A custom distribution sits between the cylinder and hoop while staying on the same incline.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the live relation $v_{\mathrm{cm}} = r\omega$ beside the rolling object.
whatToNotice: The center speed and angular speed are not independent once the object rolls without slipping.
For the same center speed, smaller radius requires larger angular speed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps translation and rotation tied to the same physical state.

overlays.label: Mass layout cue
overlays.shortDescription: Shows how sphere, cylinder, hoop, or custom mass distribution changes the inertia factor $k$.
whatToNotice: The slope and total mass can stay the same while only the distribution about the axis changes.
More rim-loaded layouts mean larger inertia factor and slower center-of-mass acceleration.
overlays.whyItMatters: Different shapes roll differently because rotational inertia changes, not because gravity acts differently on them.

overlays.label: Energy split bar
overlays.shortDescription: Shows how the same drop in gravitational potential energy is split between translation and rotation.
whatToNotice: All of the energy comes from the same drop in height.
Larger inertia factor keeps a larger fraction of that energy in rotation.
overlays.whyItMatters: It explains the race outcome in energy language, not just as a kinematics rule.

overlays.label: Static-friction torque
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the uphill static-friction force and the torque it supplies at the contact point.
whatToNotice: Static friction points uphill for an object accelerating down the ramp in this no-slip model.
That same friction force supplies the torque needed to build angular acceleration.
overlays.whyItMatters: It corrects the common mistake that friction must always oppose the motion of the center of mass.

graphs.label: Distance down ramp vs time
graphs.description: Distance curves upward because the center of mass accelerates as the object rolls down the incline.

graphs.label: No-slip speed link
graphs.description: The center-of-mass speed and $r\omega$ stay on top of each other whenever the no-slip condition is satisfied.

graphs.label: Kinetic-energy split
graphs.description: The loss of height feeds both translational and rotational kinetic energy, and the split depends on shape.

graphs.label: Acceleration vs inertia factor
graphs.description: For the current slope angle, larger inertia factor means smaller center-of-mass acceleration.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice while it rolls
noticePrompts.intro: Keep one question in view at a time: what the no-slip rule locks together, and what changing the shape actually changes.

items.text: Sphere, cylinder, and hoop feel the same incline, but the one with smaller inertia factor still reaches the bottom sooner.
items.tryThis: Switch between the solid-sphere, solid-cylinder, and hoop presets without changing the slope angle.
items.whyItMatters: This isolates rotational inertia as the reason the race order changes.

items.text: On the speed-link graph, the two curves lie on top of each other because no-slip rolling ties the linear and angular motions together.
items.tryThis: Turn on the no-slip overlay, then reduce the radius and watch the same center speed require a larger angular speed.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that spin is not a separate extra story. It is locked to the translation.

items.text: The energy split changes with shape even though the same drop in height supplies the whole budget in every case.
items.tryThis: Compare the solid sphere and the hoop on the same slope. The hoop keeps a larger share of the energy in rotation.
items.whyItMatters: This explains why equal gravity does not mean equal race times.

items.text: In compare mode, changing only the inertia factor is enough to separate the finish times on the same ramp.
items.tryThis: Clone the solid-sphere preset into compare mode, then turn only Setup B into a hoop.
items.whyItMatters: It makes the key idea explicit: shape matters because it changes rotational inertia.

predictionMode.title: Predict the rolling response
predictionMode.intro: Predict what changes before you touch the controls. Then test the same prediction on the stage, readouts, and graphs.

items.prompt: If the slope angle and radius stay the same but you switch from a hoop to a solid sphere, what should change?

choices.label: The center-of-mass acceleration should rise and the travel time should drop.
Nothing important should change because the no-slip condition is still true.
The sphere should roll more slowly because more of its motion is translational.

items.explanation: Both still obey rolling without slipping, but the sphere has smaller inertia factor, so less of the same downhill pull is used to spin it up and more is available for center-of-mass acceleration.
items.observationHint: Watch the acceleration map and distance graph after the switch. The sphere should build center speed more quickly.

items.prompt: If you keep the same solid sphere but increase the slope angle, what should change?

choices.label: The acceleration and center speed should rise because the downhill component of gravity is larger.
The acceleration should stay the same because the shape did not change.
The no-slip relation should fail automatically on a steeper incline.

items.explanation: Shape still matters through $k$, but a steeper incline makes $g\sin\theta$ larger for every setup. The no-slip link still holds, so both center speed and spin build faster.
items.observationHint: Compare the distance curve and the speed-link graph after applying the steeper sphere.

items.prompt: If you keep the same solid sphere and the same slope but make the radius smaller, what should happen to the center acceleration and angular speed?

choices.label: The center acceleration should stay on the same sphere curve, but the angular speed should become larger.
Both the center acceleration and angular speed should become smaller.
The center acceleration should become much larger because the object is smaller.

items.explanation: For the same shape, the acceleration formula is unchanged, but the no-slip link requires $\omega = v/r$. Smaller radius means larger angular speed for the same center speed.
items.observationHint: Keep the no-slip overlay visible and compare the live center speed with the larger spin rate.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use one compact incline to prove that you can reason about rolling response instead of memorizing which shape wins.

items.title: Fast run, same ramp
items.prompt: Keep the ramp near its baseline angle and tune the roller so it reaches the bottom in under about $1.85\,\mathrm{s}$.
items.successMessage: You kept the same incline and built a faster roller by lowering the inertia factor instead of pretending gravity changed.

setup.note: Use the acceleration map to stay on the same incline while you move toward a more center-loaded shape.

hints: Do not solve this by steepening the incline too much. Keep the slope near baseline and change the mass distribution first.
A sphere-like response should beat a hoop-like response on the same ramp.

targets.label: Keep the slope angle between $11.5^\circ$ and $12.5^\circ$.
Bring the inertia factor between $0.4$ and $0.43$.
Make the full-ramp travel time land between $1.78$ and $1.86\,\mathrm{s}$.

items.title: Same ramp, different finish
items.prompt: Open compare mode and make Setup B finish much later than Setup A while keeping both setups on the same slope and radius.
items.successMessage: You used shape alone to separate the finish times. The ramp stayed the same, but the larger inertia factor in Setup B slowed the center-of-mass acceleration.

setup.note: Clone the sphere, then turn only Setup B into a hoop while keeping the same slope and radius.

hints: Keep both radii matched so the race comparison stays about shape and inertia factor.
Setup A should stay sphere-like. Setup B should move toward the hoop limit.

targets.label: Keep Setup A slope angle between $11.5^\circ$ and $12.5^\circ$.
Keep Setup B slope angle between $11.5^\circ$ and $12.5^\circ$.
Keep Setup A radius between $0.21$ and $0.23\,\mathrm{m}$.
Keep Setup B radius between $0.21$ and $0.23\,\mathrm{m}$.
Keep Setup A sphere-like between $0.4$ and $0.43$.
Move Setup B toward the hoop between $0.98$ and $1.0$.
Make Setup B travel time land between $2.12$ and $2.22\,\mathrm{s}$.

items.title: Same roll, faster spin
items.prompt: Inspect the run near the end and tune a small sphere that keeps the same rolling logic but reaches a high angular speed.
items.successMessage: You kept the sphere-like rolling response while using a smaller radius to push the angular speed higher under the same no-slip link.

setup.note: Keep the inspection cursor near the end of the run and use the no-slip link to tune the angular speed.

hints: Do not turn this into a hoop. Keep the shape sphere-like and use radius to drive the angular speed.
The center speed stays on the same sphere rolling curve, but the smaller radius raises $\omega$.

targets.label: Keep the inertia factor between $0.4$ and $0.43$.
Keep the radius between $0.16$ and $0.18\,\mathrm{m}$.
Make the late-run angular speed land between $15.5$ and $17.5\,\mathrm{rad/s}$.

items.title: Series and Parallel Circuits
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

equations.meaning: One series loop makes the resistances add directly.
Two parallel branches lower the one-number load seen by the battery.
Battery current depends on the battery voltage and the equivalent resistance of the full arrangement.
A steady series loop carries one current everywhere.

equations.label: Current split in parallel
equations.meaning: Branch currents add back together at the junction.

equations.label: Load voltage relation
equations.meaning: Voltage across one load depends on the current through that load and the load resistance.

equations.label: Load power
equations.meaning: Power sets the rate of energy transfer and explains relative brightness in this bounded model.
At one fixed current, the amount of charge that crosses a branch grows linearly with time.

variableLinks.description: Changes the push from the battery without changing the load arrangement itself.

variableLinks.label: Load A resistance
variableLinks.description: Changes the amber load, the total load seen by the battery, and the way current or voltage is shared.

variableLinks.label: Load B resistance
variableLinks.description: Changes the blue load and makes the contrast between series sharing and parallel splitting easy to read.

controls.description: Raises or lowers the battery push for both arrangements.

controls.label: Load A resistance
controls.description: Sets the amber load in either the single series path or the upper parallel branch.

controls.label: Load B resistance
controls.description: Sets the blue load and makes branch-current and brightness differences visible.

controls.label: Use parallel branches
controls.description: Off keeps one series loop. On gives the same battery two separate branches.

presets.label: Equal series pair
presets.description: Two equal loads in one loop make the shared-current logic easy to see.

presets.label: Equal parallel pair
presets.description: The same two equal loads now span the same battery directly.

presets.label: Unequal series loads
presets.description: The same current crosses both loads, so the larger resistance takes the larger voltage drop.

presets.label: Unequal parallel pair
presets.description: Both branches keep the same battery voltage, but the lower-resistance branch pulls more current and more power.

presets.label: Low-voltage parallel
presets.description: The same equal branches stay in parallel but dim because the battery voltage is smaller.

overlays.label: Current flow
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the live current arrows and charge counters on the stage.
whatToNotice: Series keeps one current everywhere, while parallel splits the current into two rates that add back together.
overlays.whyItMatters: Current becomes a visible flow rate rather than a disconnected number.

overlays.label: Voltage labels
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the battery voltage and the live voltage across each load.
whatToNotice: Series makes the load voltages add up to the battery voltage, while parallel keeps both load voltages pinned to the battery.
overlays.whyItMatters: Voltage stops feeling like a detached rule and becomes a stage-visible difference across each load.

overlays.label: Node guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the shared nodes that explain why parallel branches keep the same voltage.
whatToNotice: Both parallel branches connect across the same top and bottom nodes, so they must share the same branch voltage.
overlays.whyItMatters: Node language makes equal branch voltage in parallel feel inevitable instead of mysterious.

overlays.label: Brightness guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows live power badges so the load brightness story stays tied to the same circuit state.
whatToNotice: Brightness changes with power, not with resistance alone.
overlays.whyItMatters: This keeps the bulb intuition honest by connecting it directly to power in each load.

graphs.label: Branch current vs load B
graphs.description: Sweep only load B while the battery voltage, load A, and arrangement stay fixed. In series all three current lines move together because one loop current passes through both loads. In parallel, load A stays fixed while load B and the total current respond to the changing blue branch resistance.

graphs.label: Load voltage vs load B
graphs.description: Sweep only load B while the battery voltage and load A stay fixed. In series the two load voltages trade off and still add to the battery voltage, while in parallel both branch voltages stay pinned to the battery.

graphs.label: Load power vs load B
graphs.description: Brightness follows power in this bounded model. Sweep load B to see how changing one resistance changes which load transfers energy faster in series and in parallel.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use each prompt as a small investigation. Change one thing, then connect what the stage shows to what the graphs show.

items.text: Switching from series to parallel does not make the battery stronger. It changes the path structure and therefore the equivalent resistance seen by the battery.
items.tryThis: Toggle only the arrangement while the equal pair stays at 12 V and 6 ohm plus 6 ohm. Watch R_eq and I_total jump even though the battery label does not move.
items.whyItMatters: This keeps the cause of brighter parallel bulbs honest: the battery is unchanged, but the total load is smaller.

items.text: On the branch-current graph, series keeps one current everywhere, while parallel lets load B change on its own and makes the total current follow the branch sum.
items.tryThis: Open the branch-current graph, stay at 12 V, then drag only load B. In parallel, the amber branch line stays fixed while the blue and teal lines move.
items.whyItMatters: Current splitting becomes something you can read directly instead of memorizing as a detached rule.

items.text: On the branch-voltage graph, parallel branches stay at the battery value because both loads connect across the same two nodes. In series, the two load voltages trade off and still add to the battery.
items.tryThis: Compare the unequal series and unequal parallel presets. The series lines split the battery by resistance, while the parallel lines sit at the same height.
items.whyItMatters: Equal branch voltage in parallel stops feeling mysterious once the shared-node picture and the graph agree.

items.text: The load-power graph is the brightness story. The arrangement decides whether the loads share the same current or the same voltage, so resistance alone does not predict brightness.
items.tryThis: Stay with load A at 4 ohm and sweep load B. In parallel, the blue load dims as R_B rises. In series, the blue load can become brighter because the same loop current crosses both loads.
items.whyItMatters: This keeps bulb intuition tied to power rather than to one variable taken out of context.

items.text: Charge counters show the time consequence of current. In series both counters rise together; in parallel the branch with the larger current accumulates charge faster.
items.tryThis: Pause at a later time in unequal parallel mode and compare Q_A and Q_B. Then switch to unequal series mode and watch those counters lock together.
items.whyItMatters: It connects a current rule to a time-based outcome without introducing a separate hidden model.
Topology is easier to trust when it is the only thing that changed.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict before you change the arrangement or a load. Each scenario changes one idea at a time so you can track what must happen to current, voltage, and brightness.

items.prompt: The circuit starts as the equal series pair: 12 V with 6 ohm and 6 ohm in one loop. If you change only to parallel, which statement must be true?

choices.label: Equivalent resistance drops, total current rises, and each load keeps the full 12 V.
Equivalent resistance stays at 12 ohm, but each branch gets half the battery voltage.
Total current falls because the charge now has more components to cross.

items.explanation: Putting the same two loads in parallel reduces the equivalent resistance seen by the battery. The battery is unchanged, so the total current rises and each branch still spans the full battery voltage.
items.observationHint: The battery label stays fixed while both branch-voltage labels sit at the full battery value and the total current badge jumps.

items.prompt: In series mode with V = 12 V and R_A = 4 ohm, what happens if R_B increases from 4 ohm to 12 ohm?

choices.label: The loop current drops, and load B takes a larger share of the battery voltage.
The loop current stays fixed, but load A takes the larger voltage share because it is first.
The loop current rises because the larger resistor now stores more charge.

items.explanation: Raising one series resistance raises the equivalent resistance, so the same battery drives less loop current. With the same current through both loads, the larger resistance gets the larger voltage drop.
items.observationHint: The current badge shrinks, the blue voltage label grows, and the branch-voltage graph tilts toward load B.

items.prompt: In parallel mode with V = 12 V and R_A = 4 ohm, what happens if R_B increases from 4 ohm to 12 ohm?

choices.label: Load B current and power fall, while load A stays on the same branch voltage and current.
Both branch currents rise because the blue branch is now harder to cross.
Load B voltage falls below 12 V because the branches no longer share the same nodes.

items.explanation: In parallel, each branch keeps the full battery voltage. Raising only R_B lowers only the blue branch current and power, while the amber branch remains set by its own unchanged resistance.
items.observationHint: The amber branch stays unchanged while the blue current, power, and charge rate all shrink.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same two-load circuit to hit branch-current and branch-voltage targets. The checks come from the live stage, so the topology rule stays tied to what you can actually see.

items.title: Give Load B full battery voltage
items.prompt: Starting from Unequal series loads, rewire the circuit so Load B keeps the full battery voltage while its branch current stays around 1 A.
items.successMessage: You rebuilt the unequal pair into a true parallel case. The blue branch now keeps the full source voltage while its own larger resistance still limits its current.

setup.note: Use the branch-voltage graph and the node guide together while you rewire the unequal pair.

hints: Keep the 4 ohm and 12 ohm loads unchanged. The target comes from changing the arrangement, not the resistances.
If Load B really spans the same two nodes as the battery, its voltage should stay at the full battery value.

targets.label: Keep resistancea between 3.8 ohm and 4.2 ohm.
Keep resistanceb between 11.8 ohm and 12.2 ohm.
Keep branchbvoltage between 11.8 V and 12.2 V.
Keep branchbcurrent between 0.95 A and 1.05 A.
Keep totalcurrent between 3.9 A and 4.1 A.

items.title: Total Internal Reflection
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one boundary between two media with a dashed normal at the contact point. One ray approaches from medium 1. Below threshold, a transmitted ray enters medium 2; above threshold, only a reflected ray remains in medium 1.
Optional overlays show the angle markers, the critical-angle threshold, the equal-angle reflection cue, and speed information linked to the current refractive indices.

The threshold transition graph plots outgoing angle against incident angle for the current media pair. The refracted branch reaches 90° at $\theta_c$, and beyond that only the reflected branch remains.
The transmitted-angle graph isolates the ordinary-refraction branch below threshold, so hovering it previews different boundary setups rather than later times.

equations.meaning: This is the same refraction law used below threshold, and it is the law that stops giving a real $\theta_2$ above the critical angle.
This is the largest incident angle that still allows a real transmitted ray when light goes from higher $n$ to lower $n$.

equations.label: Reflected angle
equations.meaning: In total internal reflection, the ray remains in medium 1 and the reflected angle still equals the incident angle.

variableLinks.description: Sets how steeply the incoming ray approaches the normal. Raising it drives the live transition from ordinary refraction toward and then beyond the critical angle.

variableLinks.label: Incident-medium index
variableLinks.description: Controls the medium the ray starts in. Raising n_1 at fixed n_2 lowers the critical angle and makes total internal reflection easier to reach.

variableLinks.label: Transmitted-medium index
variableLinks.description: Controls the second medium. Lowering n_2 relative to n_1 increases the index contrast, lowers theta_c, and pushes the threshold to smaller incident angles.

controls.description: Measured from the normal. Increasing it pushes the setup toward, and then possibly beyond, the critical angle.

controls.label: Incident-medium index n1
controls.description: Changing $n_1$ changes the speed contrast. Higher $n_1$ relative to $n_2$ lowers the critical angle.

controls.label: Transmitted-medium index n2
controls.description: Changing $n_2$ moves the threshold. Raising $n_2$ toward $n_1$ raises $\theta_c$; lowering it makes TIR easier to reach.

presets.label: Below critical
presets.description: Glass to air with a real transmitted ray still present.

presets.label: Near critical
presets.description: The transmitted ray is still real but almost parallel to the boundary.

presets.label: Above critical
presets.description: The incident angle is above $\theta_c$, so no real transmitted ray remains.

presets.label: Glass to water
presets.description: The weaker contrast raises $\theta_c$, so the same 50° ray refracts again.

presets.label: Water to air
presets.description: The milder contrast still allows TIR, but at a larger critical angle.

overlays.label: Normal and angle guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the normal and the angle markers used on this page.
whatToNotice: The incident and transmitted angles are measured from the normal, not from the surface.
overlays.whyItMatters: Many TIR mistakes come from measuring the wrong angle.

overlays.label: Critical-angle guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the threshold where the transmitted ray would just run along the boundary.
whatToNotice: The guide only exists when light starts in the higher-index medium.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the change from refraction to TIR visible on the same boundary.

overlays.label: Reflected-angle guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the equal-angle reflection that remains after the threshold is crossed.
whatToNotice: Above $\theta_c$, there is no real transmitted ray, so the reflected ray is the only outgoing branch in this model.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links TIR to the ordinary law of reflection without pretending refraction still survives.

overlays.label: Speed guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the relative light speeds implied by $n_1$ and $n_2$.
whatToNotice: Changing the index contrast changes the speed contrast, and that moves the critical angle.
overlays.whyItMatters: TIR comes from how the two media differ, not from a separate bounce rule.

graphs.label: Threshold transition
graphs.description: Below $\theta_c$, the refracted-angle branch climbs toward 90°. Above $\theta_c$, no real transmitted branch remains, so only the reflected-angle branch continues.

graphs.label: Transmitted angle below threshold
graphs.description: Shows only the refracted branch that exists below threshold. As $\theta_1$ approaches $\theta_c$ from below, $\theta_2$ approaches 90°.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Change one thing at a time. Use the stage and graphs together to see where the refracted branch ends and total internal reflection begins.

items.text: Notice that TIR only appears when the ray starts in the higher-index medium. If $n_1$ is not larger than $n_2$, the critical-angle threshold disappears.
items.tryThis: Start with Glass to air, then raise $n_2$ toward $n_1$ and watch the threshold move upward or disappear.
items.whyItMatters: A large angle alone is never enough. The travel direction across the index contrast matters first.

items.text: Notice that the threshold transition graph is not a time plot. Each point is a different incident-angle setup for the same two media.
items.tryThis: Hover from left to right and watch the stage jump through different incident angles instead of advancing later in time.
items.whyItMatters: That is why the graph can show where the refracted branch ends and the reflected branch takes over.

items.text: Notice that just below $\theta_c$ the transmitted ray is still real, but it is nearly parallel to the boundary as $\theta_2$ approaches 90°.
items.tryThis: Use Near critical and nudge theta_1 upward one degree at a time until the transmitted ray nearly skims the boundary.
items.whyItMatters: That is the geometric limit you cross before TIR begins.

items.text: Notice that above theta_c the stage shows no real transmitted ray. The only outgoing branch left in the model is the reflected one in medium 1.
items.tryThis: Switch from Near critical to Above critical without changing the media pair and watch the reflected branch replace the transmitted one.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents the mistaken idea that the transmitted ray just keeps bending forever.

items.text: Notice how index contrast moves theta_c. A stronger contrast lowers the threshold, so the same incident angle can flip from refraction into TIR.
items.tryThis: Clone Water to air into both setups, then raise only n_1 in Setup B and read the critical-margin row plus the transition graph.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode separates a bigger incident angle from a bigger index contrast without leaving the live page.

items.text: Notice that repeated TIR at successive boundaries is the same local event used in fiber-optic guidance.
items.tryThis: Stay above theta_c and imagine the ray meeting another lower-index wall farther ahead. The same threshold event can repeat again and again.
items.whyItMatters: It gives one concrete application of the boundary event without leaving the current concept.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the threshold outcome first, then apply the change and compare the stage, the critical-angle readout, and the graphs.

items.prompt: Starting from Glass to air near critical, what happens if you increase theta_1 a few degrees without changing the media pair?

choices.label: The transmitted branch ends, and the ray stays in medium 1 as a totally internally reflected ray.
The transmitted ray keeps opening away from the normal until it points straight down into medium 2.
Nothing important changes because theta_c only depends on the indices.

items.explanation: At $\theta_c$, the refracted ray reaches 90°. Above it, Snell's law no longer gives a real transmitted angle, so the ray stays in medium 1.
items.observationHint: Watch the critical margin turn positive as the reflected ray becomes the only outgoing branch.

items.prompt: Starting from Glass to air above critical, what happens if you raise n_2 closer to n_1 by switching to Glass to water at the same incident angle?

choices.label: The critical angle rises, so the same 50 degree ray drops back into ordinary refraction.
The critical angle falls even more, so the reflected branch becomes stronger.
The outcome cannot change because theta_1 stayed fixed.

items.explanation: Raising $n_2$ toward $n_1$ weakens the contrast, raises $\theta_c$, and can restore a real transmitted ray at the same $\theta_1$.
items.observationHint: Compare the positive critical margin in Glass to air with the negative margin in Glass to water.

items.prompt: Starting from Glass to water at 50 degrees, what happens if you keep theta_1 fixed but lower n_2 to air?

choices.label: The critical angle drops, so the same ray can cross into total internal reflection.
The critical angle rises, so the transmitted branch becomes even easier to keep.
Only the speed ratio changes. The threshold angle stays the same.

items.explanation: Lowering $n_2$ strengthens the contrast, lowers $\theta_c$, and can move the same $\theta_1$ from refraction into TIR.
items.observationHint: Apply the air case and watch theta_c shift left on the graph while the reflected branch appears on the stage.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the live threshold model rather than a detached puzzle. The checks read the same incident angle, index pair, and graph state you see on the stage.

items.title: Land just below the threshold
items.prompt: Starting from Glass to air near critical, tune the setup until the incident angle stays just below theta_c: keep theta_1 - theta_c between -2 and -0.4 degrees while theta_2 remains between 74 and 89 degrees.
items.successMessage: You held the ray in ordinary refraction while staying visibly close to the threshold.

setup.note: Use the critical-margin row and the transition map together.

hints: You want a small negative critical margin, not a positive one.
If theta_2 is already gone, you crossed too far and need to come back below theta_c.

checks.label: Open the threshold transition graph.
Keep the critical-angle guide visible.
Keep theta_1 - theta_c slightly negative.
Keep the transmitted angle steep but real.

items.title: Cross into TIR cleanly
items.prompt: Starting from Glass to air below critical, raise the setup until the boundary is clearly above threshold while staying on the same media pair.
items.successMessage: You crossed the critical-angle limit and turned the same boundary into total internal reflection.

setup.note: Keep the glass-to-air pair and push the critical margin positive.

hints: The easiest move is to raise theta_1 while leaving n_1 and n_2 near the glass-to-air values.
A positive critical margin means you are above theta_c.

checks.label: Open the threshold transition graph.
Keep the reflected-angle guide visible.
Keep the incident medium near glass.
Keep the transmitted medium near air.
Make theta_1 - theta_c clearly positive.

items.title: Pressure and Hydrostatic Pressure
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

keyIdeas: Surface pressure from the piston is $P_s = F/A$, so the same force gives less pressure when it is spread over a larger area.
In a resting connected fluid, pressure at one point acts equally in all directions.
At the same depth in the same resting fluid, the hydrostatic pressure is the same.
Hydrostatic pressure increases linearly with depth because each extra meter adds the same pressure increment.
Density and gravity set the slope of the pressure-depth graph, so denser fluids or stronger gravity make pressure rise faster with depth.

controls.description: Changes the piston load at the fluid surface.
Spreads the same force across a wider or narrower surface.
Sets how much pressure each meter of depth adds.
Changes the hydrostatic pressure gradient without changing $F/A$.
Moves the probe deeper or shallower in the same fluid.

presets.label: Water baseline
presets.description: A water-like fluid with a moderate piston load and a 1 m probe depth.

presets.label: Wide piston
presets.description: The same force is spread over a much larger surface area.

presets.label: Deep probe
presets.description: The same surface loading, but the probe is much deeper in the fluid.

presets.label: Dense brine
presets.description: A denser fluid raises the pressure gradient without changing the depth.

presets.label: Low gravity tank
presets.description: The same fluid and depth under weaker gravity.

overlays.label: Surface load
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the piston area and the downward surface force that creates $F/A$.
whatToNotice: Making the piston wider spreads the same force over more area, so the surface pressure falls.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps pressure tied to force per area instead of making it feel like a mysterious extra quantity.

overlays.label: Depth guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the probe depth from the fluid surface.
whatToNotice: Every extra meter adds the same amount of hydrostatic pressure when density and gravity stay fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the $h$ in $\rho g h$ concrete.

overlays.label: Pressure arrows
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the same probe pressure acting upward, downward, and sideways.
whatToNotice: At one point in a resting fluid, the arrows stay equal because pressure acts equally in all directions.
overlays.whyItMatters: It prevents the common mistake of treating hydrostatic pressure as only a downward push.

overlays.label: Same-depth line
overlays.shortDescription: Shows a horizontal line through the probe to emphasize equal depth.
whatToNotice: Points on the same horizontal line have the same hydrostatic pressure in one connected resting fluid.
overlays.whyItMatters: It sets up buoyancy by showing that deeper points feel more pressure than shallower ones.

graphs.label: Pressure vs depth
graphs.description: Change only the probe depth. The surface-pressure line stays flat, while the hydrostatic and total pressures rise linearly.
Change only fluid density. The surface-pressure line stays fixed, while the hydrostatic and total pressures increase with density.
Change only piston force. The hydrostatic part stays fixed, while the surface and total pressures rise together.
Change only piston area. Spreading the same force over more area lowers the surface and total pressures.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Ask one question at a time: what comes from $F/A$, what comes from depth, and what stays unchanged?

items.text: Move the probe deeper. The surface pressure from $F/A$ stays the same, but the total probe pressure rises because the hydrostatic part $ho g h$ is larger.
Turn on the same-depth line. A point near the wall and a point near the middle have the same hydrostatic pressure if they are at the same depth in one connected fluid.
Increase the piston area while keeping the same force. The piston gets wider, the surface pressure falls, and the total pressure curve drops on the area graph.
Density and gravity do not change the surface-pressure line. They change the slope of the hydrostatic and total depth graphs because they change how much pressure each extra meter adds.
Use compare mode to match the same total pressure with a smaller surface-pressure part but a denser fluid. The same total pressure can come from different combinations of causes.
items.tryThis: Start from Water baseline, open compare mode, leave Setup A unchanged, and tune Setup B until the total pressures match while Setup B uses a smaller $F/A$ and a larger hydrostatic part.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict which part changes first: the surface-pressure part, the hydrostatic part, or both.

items.prompt: Starting from Water baseline, you move the probe deeper while changing nothing else. What should happen?

choices.label: The hydrostatic and total pressures rise, while the surface-pressure part stays the same.
Only the surface pressure rises because there is more fluid touching the piston.
All pressure stays the same because the fluid is connected.

items.explanation: Moving the probe deeper changes only the $ho g h$ part. The piston force and area have not changed, so the surface-pressure term stays fixed.
items.observationHint: The depth guide grows, the pressure arrows lengthen, and only the hydrostatic and total curves move on the depth graph.

items.prompt: Starting from Water baseline, you keep the same force but double the piston area. Which statement is best?

choices.label: Surface pressure falls, so the total probe pressure also falls.
Surface pressure rises because a larger area lets the fluid push harder.
Only the hydrostatic pressure changes because the fluid depth is the same.

items.explanation: With the same force spread over a larger area, $F/A$ becomes smaller. The hydrostatic part stays the same, so the total pressure falls by the same amount.
items.observationHint: The piston gets wider, the surface-pressure readout drops, and the area graph slopes downward.

items.prompt: Starting from Water baseline, you swap in a denser fluid at the same depth and surface loading. What changes next?

choices.label: The hydrostatic and total pressures rise because each meter of depth adds more pressure.
Only the surface pressure rises because the denser fluid pushes back on the piston more.
Pressure falls because denser fluid spreads the piston force better.

items.explanation: Density appears in the hydrostatic term on this page. A denser fluid makes pressure increase faster with depth without changing $F/A$ at the surface.
items.observationHint: The density changes, but the surface-pressure line stays put while the hydrostatic and total curves rise together.

items.prompt: Starting from Water baseline, you lower gravity while keeping the same piston load, area, fluid, and depth. What happens?

choices.label: The hydrostatic part drops, so the total pressure drops too.
The surface pressure drops but the hydrostatic part stays the same.
Nothing changes because the fluid and depth are unchanged.

items.explanation: Gravity changes the hydrostatic gradient $ho g$. The surface-pressure part depends only on force and area, so it stays fixed.
items.observationHint: The pressure arrows shorten and the hydrostatic part decreases even though the piston load has not changed.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same tank to hit pressure targets and compare different ways of making the same total pressure.

items.title: Reach 24 kPa by changing depth only
items.prompt: Start from Water baseline and adjust only the probe depth until the total pressure is about 24 kPa while force, area, density, and gravity stay near baseline.
items.successMessage: You reached the target by changing depth only. The surface pressure stayed fixed while the hydrostatic part increased linearly.

setup.note: Keep the surface-loading controls and fluid properties near baseline. Move only the probe depth.

hints: Read the flat surface-pressure line first, then use the total-pressure line to estimate the needed depth.
If force or area changes, you are no longer isolating the hydrostatic part.

checks.label: Open the pressure-depth graph.
Keep the depth guide visible.
Keep the force near the baseline.
Keep the area near the baseline.
Keep the fluid near water density.
Keep gravity near Earth gravity.
Move the probe to about 1.96 m depth.
Reach about 24 kPa total pressure.

items.title: Match the total with less surface pressure
items.prompt: Start from Water baseline, switch to compare mode, leave Setup A unchanged, and tune only Setup B until it reaches the same total pressure with a smaller surface-pressure part and a denser fluid.
items.successMessage: You matched the same total pressure with different causes. Setup B used less surface pressure and more hydrostatic pressure.

setup.note: Keep Setup A fixed as the baseline. Edit only Setup B.

hints: Keep Setup B at about the same depth first, then lower its surface-pressure part and let density make up the difference.
Match the total pressure before worrying about matching the separate parts.

checks.label: Stay in compare mode while editing Setup B.
Open the pressure-density graph.
Keep the pressure arrows visible.
Keep Setup A at the baseline total pressure.
Keep Setup B near the same depth.
Lower Setup B's force.
Spread Setup B over a wider piston.
Use a denser fluid in Setup B.
Keep Setup B's surface pressure lower than Setup A.
Let Setup B's hydrostatic part carry more of the total.
Match Setup B to the same total pressure.

items.title: Resonance in Air Columns / Open and Closed Pipes
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
