Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Changes the resonating length of the air column and therefore the wavelengths that fit.

controls.label: Close the left end
controls.description: Switch between open-open and closed-open boundary conditions on the same tube.
Selects which allowed mode is shown. In a closed-open tube, the order steps through the odd harmonic family.
Moves the live parcel probe along the tube so you can test nodes, antinodes, and boundary behavior.
Scales the size of the parcel-motion and pressure cues without changing the allowed resonance family.

presets.label: Open tube fundamental
presets.description: Both ends breathe, so the tube fits half a wavelength.

presets.label: Open tube second harmonic
presets.description: A center displacement node appears while both ends still breathe.

presets.label: Closed pipe fundamental
presets.description: The wall stays still while the open mouth becomes the moving end.

presets.label: Closed pipe third harmonic
presets.description: The next closed-pipe resonance jumps to the odd third harmonic.

presets.label: Probe at the wall
presets.description: The probe sits almost at the closed wall where pressure is strongest and parcel motion is smallest.

overlays.label: Boundary rules
overlays.shortDescription: Marks how the current tube ends constrain parcel motion and pressure variation.
whatToNotice: At an open end, parcel motion is large and pressure variation is small. At a closed wall, parcel motion is zero and the pressure swing is strongest.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the allowed harmonic family tied to a physical end condition instead of a memorized formula.

overlays.label: Parcel nodes and antinodes
overlays.shortDescription: Shows where parcels stay still and where they swing most in the current standing-wave pattern.
whatToNotice: Nodes and antinodes stay at fixed positions in the standing-wave pattern, even though the actual parcel displacement changes through time.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the spatial resonance pattern from one frozen instant of motion.

overlays.label: Pressure complements
overlays.shortDescription: Adds the complementary pressure-node and pressure-antinode pattern to the same tube.
whatToNotice: Pressure and parcel motion swap roles: where one is smallest, the other is often strongest.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps displacement and pressure connected without pretending they peak at the same places.

graphs.label: Displacement shape
graphs.description: Shows the standing-wave shape along the tube, so you can see where parcel motion must be zero or largest for the current boundary condition.

graphs.label: Probe motion
graphs.description: Tracks one chosen parcel in time together with its local envelope, so a node gives a flat trace while an antinode gives the largest motion.

graphs.label: Allowed harmonics
graphs.description: Shows the allowed resonance frequencies for the current tube, making the missing even harmonics of a closed-open pipe easy to see.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time. Each one points to a real boundary-condition effect already visible on the tube or graph.

items.text: Notice that an open end is where the air moves most freely, so it is a displacement antinode, not a stuck point.
items.tryThis: Leave the boundary overlay on and compare the open mouth with an interior node before you move the probe.
items.whyItMatters: It ties the mouth of the tube to the correct displacement rule before you think about harmonics.

items.text: Notice that closing one end changes the whole harmonic family, not just the label on the same pattern.
items.tryThis: Keep the tube length fixed, toggle the wall on, and watch the harmonic ladder drop into the odd-only closed-pipe pattern.
items.whyItMatters: Boundary conditions decide which wavelengths can fit in the first place.

items.text: Notice that hovering the displacement-shape graph moves the live probe to that same position in the tube.
items.tryThis: Hover near the closed wall, then hover near the open mouth, and compare the probe-motion graph.
items.whyItMatters: The graph and the tube are two views of the same standing-wave pattern.

items.text: Notice that the pressure guides swap roles with the parcel-motion guides: pressure is strongest where parcel motion is smallest, and weakest where parcel motion is largest.
items.tryThis: Turn on pressure guides and compare the closed wall or the open mouth with the parcel-motion markers.
items.whyItMatters: The displacement picture and the pressure picture describe the same resonance from complementary angles.

items.text: Notice that the harmonic ladder skips the even harmonics in a closed-open tube.
items.tryThis: Keep the length fixed and compare open-open against closed-open while the ladder graph stays open.
items.whyItMatters: The missing even modes are a direct consequence of the end conditions.

items.text: Notice that the time rail changes the instantaneous parcel motion, but the shape graph stays spatial because it represents the allowed standing pattern, not one frozen frame.
items.tryThis: Pause, step once, and compare what changes in the tube with what stays fixed on the shape graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the resonance pattern from the moment-by-moment oscillation inside that pattern.

items.text: Notice which setup has the lower allowed frequency at the same length. That setup is the one with the longer allowed wavelength family.
items.tryThis: Clone the open tube into A and B, then close one end in Setup B and compare the harmonic ladder before reading the equations.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode makes the open-vs-closed resonance shift visible without hiding the shared tube length.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the boundary-condition consequence first, then test it on the same tube.

items.prompt: The tube length stays fixed, and you close one end of an open tube while staying on the fundamental resonance. What must happen?

choices.label: The allowed wavelength doubles and the fundamental frequency halves.
The allowed wavelength stays the same because the tube length did not change.
Only the pressure cue changes. The parcel-motion pattern should stay identical.

items.explanation: An open-open fundamental fits half a wavelength across the tube, but a closed-open fundamental fits only a quarter wavelength. That makes the allowed wavelength longer and the fundamental frequency lower for the same tube length.
items.observationHint: Watch the ladder drop to a lower fundamental while the closed wall becomes a motion node.

items.prompt: In a closed-open tube, you slide the probe to the closed wall. What should happen to the probe-motion graph and the pressure cue there?

choices.label: The probe-motion graph should collapse near zero while the pressure cue becomes strongest.
Both motion and pressure should vanish because the wall blocks the air.
The probe-motion graph should double because reflections stack at the wall.

items.explanation: The closed wall is a displacement node and a pressure antinode. Parcels cannot move through the wall, but the compressions and rarefactions pile up there most strongly.
items.observationHint: Use the pressure-guides overlay to confirm that the pressure cue peaks where the parcel motion collapses.

items.prompt: A closed-open tube is already on its fundamental. You raise the resonance order from 1 to 2 without changing the tube length. What must the next allowed pattern be?

choices.label: The tube should jump to the third harmonic, with a shorter wavelength and a higher frequency.
The tube should move to the second harmonic because every resonance order adds the next integer harmonic.
The wavelength should get longer because there are now more nodes.

items.explanation: Closed-open tubes skip the even harmonics. After the fundamental, the next allowed pattern is the third harmonic, so the wavelength shortens and the frequency rises.
items.observationHint: The harmonic ladder skips straight from the first to the third harmonic family.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the tube, overlays, and graphs to turn open-vs-closed boundary rules into compact resonance tasks.

items.title: Closed-end stillness
items.prompt: Starting from the closed-pipe third harmonic, move the probe onto the closed wall so parcel motion nearly disappears while the pressure cue stays strong.
items.successMessage: You found the closed-wall displacement node. The parcels stay still there even while the pressure swing remains large.

setup.note: Use the boundary and pressure overlays together while you move the probe.

hints: A closed wall is a displacement node, not a pressure node.
This task is about the envelope, not one lucky instant. Keep the probe where the motion stays tiny across the full cycle.

checks.label: Open the displacement-shape graph.
Keep the boundary overlay visible.
Turn on the pressure guides.
Make the parcel-motion envelope smaller than $0.015\,\mathrm{m}$.
Keep the pressure envelope above $0.09$.

items.title: Same slider, different harmonic family
items.prompt: Enter compare mode, keep both setups at the same tube length and the same resonance-order slider setting of 2, then make Setup B the closed-open tube so Setup A lands on the 2nd harmonic while Setup B lands on the 3rd harmonic at a lower frequency.
items.successMessage: You matched the same slider value across two different boundary conditions. The open tube keeps the 2nd harmonic, while the closed-open tube shifts to the odd 3rd harmonic and a lower allowed frequency.

setup.note: Clone the open-tube second harmonic into A and B, then edit only Setup B.

hints: Do not change the tube length or the resonance-order slider between A and B.
The closed-open tube keeps the same slider setting but changes the harmonic multiple because the even harmonics are missing.

checks.label: Switch into compare mode and edit Setup B.
Keep the harmonic ladder graph open.
Keep Setup A on the reference tube length.
Keep Setup B on the same tube length.
Keep Setup A on resonance-order setting 2.
Keep Setup B on the same resonance-order setting.
Setup A should stay on the 2nd harmonic family.
Setup B should shift to the odd 3rd harmonic family.
Setup A should stay near $28.3\,\mathrm{Hz}$.
Setup B should drop near $21.3\,\mathrm{Hz}$.

items.title: Angular Momentum
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Slide the same six equal masses inward or outward while total mass stays fixed. This changes the moment of inertia even before you change the spin rate.
Set the spin rate so you can compare same-speed changes with same-angular-momentum changes.

presets.label: Lab baseline
presets.description: A mid-radius rotor with a moderate spin makes both ingredients of angular momentum easy to read.

presets.label: Compact, same L
presets.description: A compact layout can carry nearly the same angular momentum as the baseline only by spinning much faster.

presets.label: Mid-radius, same L
presets.description: This middle layout keeps the angular momentum close to the same-L family without being extremely compact.

presets.label: Wide, same L
presets.description: The wide layout keeps nearly the same angular momentum as the baseline, but it can do that with a much slower spin.

presets.label: Wide, same speed
presets.description: Keeping the same spin while moving the mass outward produces a much larger angular momentum instead of conserving the old one.

overlays.label: Radius guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current distance of the equal masses from the axis.
whatToNotice: The total mass is unchanged while the radius changes.
That changing radius is why compact and wide same-L states can need very different spin rates.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the inertia change visible instead of leaving it as an abstract variable.

overlays.label: Tangential-speed arrow
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the straight-line speed of one mass at the rim.
whatToNotice: Rim speed depends on both radius and angular speed.
A faster spin in angular terms does not automatically mean a larger rim speed or a larger angular momentum.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps straight-line speed from being confused with angular momentum.

overlays.label: Equal mass markers
overlays.shortDescription: Marks that every puck carries the same mass, so only distribution and spin are changing.
whatToNotice: The rotor is not gaining extra mass when the pucks move outward.
A change in angular momentum can come from changing the moment of inertia, not only from changing angular speed.
overlays.whyItMatters: This keeps the cause tied to mass distribution instead of extra mass.

overlays.label: Same-L compact reference
overlays.shortDescription: Adds a compact reference ring and shows the spin rate that would carry the same angular momentum there.
whatToNotice: The same angular momentum can require a very different spin rate at a different radius.
The compact reference is not a second simulation. It is the same $L$ translated to a different moment of inertia.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns conservation of angular momentum into a live comparison you can read directly.

graphs.label: Rotation angle vs time
graphs.description: For a fixed setup on this bench, angle grows linearly because the rotor keeps one constant angular speed through the clip.

graphs.label: Angular momentum vs mass radius
graphs.description: At the current angular speed, moving the same masses outward increases the moment of inertia and therefore increases angular momentum.

graphs.label: Same-L angular speed vs mass radius
graphs.description: For the current angular momentum, this curve shows how angular speed would have to change if mass radius changed while angular momentum stayed fixed.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice about angular momentum
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to separate two different stories: holding angular speed fixed, and holding angular momentum fixed.

items.text: In compare mode, two rotors can carry nearly the same angular momentum even while the compact one spins much faster.
items.tryThis: Start from the compact same-L preset, clone it, then move only Setup B outward until the angular-momentum badges still match but the angular-speed badges separate sharply.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest same-L comparison on the page: same angular momentum, different moment of inertia, different spin.

items.text: Holding angular speed fixed while moving the masses outward creates a larger angular momentum. That is not conservation.
items.tryThis: Apply the wide same-speed preset and compare it with the baseline. The angular speed readout stays the same, but the angular-momentum map jumps upward.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that changing radius changes the moment of inertia and therefore changes $L$ even when $\omega$ is unchanged.

items.text: On the same-L map, angular speed falls as radius grows because the same angular momentum is being carried by a larger moment of inertia.
items.tryThis: Scrub across the response curve from left to right. The previewed stage should widen while the previewed spin rate drops.
items.whyItMatters: This is the conservation relationship in graph form.

items.text: On the angular-momentum map, a wider rotor at the same angular speed carries more angular momentum.
items.tryThis: Keep the angular speed slider fixed and drag across the momentum map. The current marker should climb as radius increases.
items.whyItMatters: It separates same-speed comparisons from same-angular-momentum comparisons.

items.text: Rim speed and angular momentum are different ideas. Both depend on radius and angular speed, but only angular momentum includes the full inertia story.
items.tryThis: Compare the compact same-L and wide same-L presets with the tangential-speed arrow visible. The faster spinner does not automatically make every other rotational quantity larger.
items.whyItMatters: It stops straight-line speed cues from replacing the actual $L = I\omega$ idea.

items.text: The same-L compact-reference label updates immediately from the current live state.
items.tryThis: Turn on the same-L reference overlay and move the radius slider slowly. The compact-reference speed changes right away because it is being computed from the same live angular momentum.
items.whyItMatters: It makes conservation a live comparison instead of a separate imagined example.

predictionMode.title: Predict the same-L tradeoff
predictionMode.intro: Predict whether a change is a same-speed change or a same-angular-momentum change before you apply it. Then compare the stage, readouts, and maps.

items.prompt: If the same angular momentum is carried from a compact layout to a much wider one, what should happen?

choices.label: Angular speed should drop because the larger moment of inertia is carrying the same angular momentum.
Angular speed should stay the same because angular momentum is already fixed.
Angular speed should increase because the masses are farther from the axis.

items.explanation: If $L$ stays fixed while $I$ increases, $\omega$ must decrease. A wide same-L rotor therefore spins more slowly than a compact same-L rotor.
items.observationHint: Watch the same-L response map and the angular-speed badges together. The wide same-L state should trade larger inertia for slower spin.

items.prompt: If you move the same masses outward but deliberately keep the angular speed unchanged, what should the simulation show?

choices.label: Angular momentum should increase because the moment of inertia increased while angular speed stayed fixed.
Angular momentum should stay the same because the rotor is still spinning.
Angular momentum should decrease because the wide rotor looks slower.

items.explanation: Holding $\omega$ fixed does not conserve the old angular momentum. It creates a new, larger $L$ because the wider layout has a larger moment of inertia.
items.observationHint: Watch the angular-momentum map and the same-L map together. One shows the new larger $L$, while the other shows what conserving the old $L$ would have required.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same rotor to prove that you can reason about angular momentum as a product story instead of treating conservation like a slogan.

items.title: Wide and still same-L
items.prompt: Start from the lab baseline, then build a wide layout that still carries nearly the same angular momentum.
items.successMessage: You kept the angular momentum nearly matched while widening the rotor and slowing the spin, which is exactly the conservation tradeoff this concept needs.

setup.note: Use the same-L response map to keep the wide-state target tied to the current angular momentum.

hints: Do not keep the old angular speed. A wide same-L rotor should spin much more slowly.
Use the radius slider first, then tune the angular speed until the angular-momentum readout comes back to the baseline range.

targets.label: Move the mass radius between $0.9$ and $1.0\,\mathrm{m}$.
Keep angular momentum between $5.3$ and $5.6\,\mathrm{kg\,m^2/s}$.
Bring angular speed between $0.85$ and $1.05\,\mathrm{rad/s}$.

items.prompt: Open compare mode and make Setup A compact and Setup B wide while keeping their angular momenta nearly matched.
items.successMessage: You built the core conservation comparison: same angular momentum, very different spin rates, and very different rotation angles by the end of the clip.

setup.note: Clone the compact same-L state, then widen only Setup B until the angular-momentum badges still match but the spin-rate badges separate.

hints: Setup A can stay near the compact same-L preset.
The main job is to slow Setup B enough that its larger inertia still lands in the same angular-momentum range.

targets.label: Keep Setup A compact between $0.26$ and $0.3\,\mathrm{m}$.
Keep Setup A angular momentum between $5.3$ and $5.6\,\mathrm{kg\,m^2/s}$.
Move Setup B outward between $0.9$ and $1.0\,\mathrm{m}$.
Keep Setup B angular momentum between $5.3$ and $5.6\,\mathrm{kg\,m^2/s}$.
Keep Setup A spinning between $5.7$ and $6.1\,\mathrm{rad/s}$.
Bring Setup B between $0.85$ and $1.05\,\mathrm{rad/s}$.

items.title: Read the end-of-clip consequence
items.prompt: Pause at the end of the clip and tune a wide same-L rotor whose slow spin makes the accumulated angle stay small.
items.successMessage: You tied the conservation story back to the visible motion: the wide same-L rotor really does sweep out less angle over the same time because it is spinning more slowly.

setup.note: Keep the cursor near the end of the clip so the angle target stays tied to one honest time window.

hints: Do not solve this by making the rotor compact again. The layout should stay wide.
The end angle is the consequence of the slow wide-layout spin, so tune angular speed gently around the same-L target.

targets.label: Keep the mass radius between $0.9$ and $1.0\,\mathrm{m}$.
Keep angular momentum between $5.3$ and $5.6\,\mathrm{kg\,m^2/s}$.
Make the end angle land between $2.1$ and $2.5\,\mathrm{rad}$.

items.title: Static Equilibrium / Centre of Mass
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Increase or decrease the cargo mass to see how it changes both total weight and centre-of-mass position.
Slide the cargo left or right to move the combined centre of mass.
Move the support region under the plank without changing the mass distribution.
Widen or narrow the support region to change only the stability margin.

presets.label: Balanced shifted support
presets.description: The support is moved under the combined centre of mass, so the reactions are close to matched even though the cargo sits right of center.

presets.label: Bare plank
presets.description: With no added cargo, the plank's own mass stays centered and the stability question is controlled entirely by the support region.

presets.label: Support under load
presets.description: A heavier cargo is placed right of center, but the support is shifted directly under the combined centre of mass.

presets.label: Barely stable
presets.description: The same heavy right load is still stable, but the centre-of-mass line is only just inside the right support edge.

presets.label: Tips right
presets.description: The combined centre of mass has crossed the right support edge, so the plank can no longer stay flat.

presets.label: Heavier but closer in
presets.description: A heavier cargo can still create the same combined centre of mass if it sits closer to the middle.

overlays.label: Weight lines
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the plank weight and cargo weight separately before combining them into one effective weight line.
whatToNotice: The separate weight arrows explain why mass placement matters before you collapse them into one combined centre of mass.
Heavier cargo does two things at once: it raises the total weight and pulls the combined weight line toward itself.
overlays.whyItMatters: Static-equilibrium reasoning is cleaner when you can see where the total weight came from instead of treating the centre of mass like magic.

overlays.shortDescription: Draws the single vertical line where the total weight acts.
whatToNotice: Changing cargo mass or cargo position moves this line because the mass distribution changes.
Changing support center or support width does not move this line. It only changes how the support compares with it.
overlays.whyItMatters: This line is the bridge between centre-of-mass calculations and torque balance.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the two support edges and shows how much room the combined centre-of-mass line still has before tipping.
whatToNotice: The plank is stable only while the combined centre-of-mass line falls between the two edges.
If the line reaches an edge, the margin is zero. If it crosses the edge, tipping begins.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the abstract stability rule into one visible geometric test.

overlays.label: Support reactions
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the upward reactions at the support edges and whether one side would need to go negative.
whatToNotice: When the combined centre-of-mass line sits over the support center, the two reactions are most balanced.
If the line crosses an edge, one required reaction would have to become negative, so that side can no longer support the plank.
overlays.whyItMatters: This connects the support-region rule back to force balance.

overlays.label: Torque arm
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the horizontal distance from the support center to the combined centre-of-mass line.
whatToNotice: This offset is the lever arm of the total weight about the support center.
When the offset is zero, the torque of the total weight about that point is zero.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the centre-of-mass story tied to the torque idea instead of turning it into a separate rule.

graphs.label: Torque about the support center
graphs.description: Shows how the torque from the total weight changes as you move the support center. The zero crossing marks the support position directly under the combined centre of mass.

graphs.label: Required support reactions
graphs.description: Shows the left and right reactions required for equilibrium. A negative value means that side would have to pull downward, so the plank tips instead.

graphs.label: Stability margin vs cargo position
graphs.description: Shows the distance from the combined centre-of-mass line to the nearest support edge for the current support geometry. Positive values are stable, zero is the edge case, and negative values mean tipping.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the balance setup
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to connect four views of the same situation: centre of mass, torque, reactions, and support-region geometry.

items.text: Notice that stability is a geometry test: the combined centre-of-mass line must land inside the support region.
items.tryThis: Keep the combined centre-of-mass and support-region overlays on while you slide the cargo block.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the tipping question into a visible condition instead of a memorized phrase.

items.text: Notice that the support-torque graph crosses zero when the support center sits directly under the combined centre of mass.
items.tryThis: Drag the support center until the graph marker lands on zero, then compare the support center with the combined centre-of-mass line.
items.whyItMatters: This is the same torque idea from a pivot, now applied to the total weight.

items.text: Changing support width changes the margin before tipping, but it does not move the combined centre of mass.
items.tryThis: Keep cargo and support center fixed, then narrow the support width. The same centre-of-mass line now has less room inside the region.
items.whyItMatters: It separates mass distribution from support geometry.

items.text: If one required reaction becomes negative, the plank tips instead of remaining in static equilibrium.
items.tryThis: Move the support center away from the combined centre-of-mass line and watch one reaction curve drop below zero.
items.whyItMatters: This is the force-balance version of the same support-region rule.

items.text: In compare mode, a heavier cargo placed closer in can share the same combined centre of mass as a lighter cargo placed farther out.
items.tryThis: Keep Setup A on Support under load, then tune Setup B to Heavier but closer in and compare the centre-of-mass lines.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that centre of mass is a weighted average, not just a visual midpoint.

predictionMode.title: Predict the balance
predictionMode.intro: Predict the balance result before you change the setup. Then check the same idea on the stage, the reaction arrows, and the graphs.

items.prompt: Starting from the tipping-right setup, what should happen if you move the support center directly under the combined centre-of-mass line while keeping the cargo fixed?
items.scenarioLabel: Support moved under the weight line

choices.label: The torque about the support center should drop toward zero and the plank should become statically stable.
The torque should stay the same because the cargo mass and cargo position did not change.
Only the total weight changes because the support has moved.

items.explanation: Moving the support changes the torque arm, not the mass distribution. When the support center lines up with the combined centre of mass, the torque from the total weight about that point becomes zero.
items.observationHint: Watch the torque marker reach zero and both reactions become non-negative.

items.prompt: Starting from the support-under-load setup, what should happen if you narrow the support region while leaving the cargo and support center fixed?
items.scenarioLabel: Same centre of mass, narrower support

choices.label: The centre of mass and total weight stay the same, but the stability margin shrinks.
The centre of mass shifts outward because the support region is narrower.
Both reactions must become equal because the support is narrower.

items.explanation: Narrowing the support region does not change the total weight or the combined centre of mass. It only brings the support edges closer to the same weight line, so the margin shrinks.
items.observationHint: The centre-of-mass line stays put while the support region closes in around it.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same bench to show that you can coordinate centre of mass, support region, torque, and reactions instead of treating them as separate rules.

items.title: Recover equilibrium
items.prompt: Starting from Tips right, move the support center until the plank is stable again.
items.successMessage: You put the support center under the combined centre of mass. That made the weight torque about the support vanish and made both reactions physically possible.

setup.note: Use the torque zero crossing together with the support-region overlay.

hints: Do not move the cargo. Fix the problem by changing only the support geometry.
The target position is where the support center lines up with the combined centre-of-mass line.

targets.label: Place the support center under the combined centre-of-mass line.
Make the torque about the support center essentially zero.
Restore a clear positive margin inside the support region.

items.title: Edge of stability
items.prompt: Starting from Support under load, shift the support region left until the plank is only just stable.
items.successMessage: You kept the combined centre-of-mass line inside the support region, but only by a very small margin.

setup.note: Keep the heavy cargo fixed. Use support center and the stability-margin graph together.

hints: A small positive margin is the goal. Zero is the exact tipping edge.
Keep the support width fixed so you are testing support position, not support size.

targets.label: Keep the support width at 1.0 m.
Shift the support region left, but not past the tipping point.
Leave only a small positive margin to the nearest edge.

items.title: Match the same centre of mass
items.prompt: Open compare mode. Keep Setup A on Support under load, then tune Setup B so a heavier cargo placed closer in gives the same combined centre of mass.
items.successMessage: You matched the combined centre of mass in two different mass distributions, so the balance point stayed the same.

setup.note: Clone Setup A, then make Setup B heavier but move the cargo closer to the middle.

hints: Heavier cargo does not need to sit as far out to produce the same weighted average.
You are matching $x_{\mathrm{CM}}$, not just making the two cargo blocks look visually similar.

targets.label: Keep Setup A at the reference combined centre-of-mass position.
Make Setup B clearly heavier than Setup A.
Move the heavier cargo closer to the middle.
Match Setup B to the same combined centre of mass.

items.title: Ideal Gas Law and Kinetic Theory
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Changes how many gas particles share the same box.
Changes the average particle-speed scale.
Changes how much room the same gas has to move.

presets.label: Room baseline
presets.description: A mid-temperature gas in a moderate box.

presets.label: Compressed box
presets.description: Same particle number and temperature, but less room.

presets.label: Hotter same box
presets.description: Same particle number and volume, but a hotter gas.

presets.label: More particles same box
presets.description: Same volume and temperature, but more particles.

overlays.label: Speed cue
overlays.shortDescription: Shows motion streaks and the average-speed chip.
whatToNotice: Raising temperature lengthens the motion streaks before you even look at the graphs.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the temperature-pressure link visible on the stage.

overlays.label: Density cue
overlays.shortDescription: Shows how packed the box is with density shading and a density chip.
whatToNotice: Reducing volume or adding particles increases density even if the average speed stays the same.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the $N/V$ part of the law visible.

overlays.label: Wall-hit cue
overlays.shortDescription: Shows wall flashes and the wall collision rate.
whatToNotice: Pressure rises when wall hits become more frequent, not only when particles move faster.
overlays.whyItMatters: It connects the pressure gauge to particle-wall collisions.

overlays.label: Pressure gauge
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current pressure next to the live wall-hit rate.
whatToNotice: Two setups can have similar pressure even when one is hotter and the other is more crowded.
overlays.whyItMatters: It reminds you that pressure is an outcome, not the whole microscopic story.

graphs.description: Change only volume. This is the cleanest graph for seeing how compression raises pressure.

graphs.label: Pressure vs temperature
graphs.description: Change only temperature. Use this graph to check that hotter gas gives higher pressure at fixed $N$ and $V$.
Change only particle number. This shows that more particles raise pressure even when the average speed stays about the same.

graphs.label: Wall collision rate vs temperature
graphs.description: Change only temperature. This graph shows how faster particles reach the walls more often.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Change one variable, then check the stage, the pressure gauge, and the matching graph.

items.text: Reducing only the volume raises pressure even though the speed cue stays about the same. In the smaller box, the same particles reach the walls more often.
items.tryThis: Switch between Room baseline and Compressed box. Compare the motion streaks, wall-hit flashes, and the pressure-volume graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the volume effect from the temperature effect.

items.text: Raising only the temperature lengthens the motion streaks and increases wall-hit rate, so pressure rises even though box size and particle count stay fixed.
items.tryThis: Hover a cool point and a warm point on the collision-temperature graph, then watch the preview values update on the stage.
items.whyItMatters: It links the pressure-temperature law to particle speed and collisions.

items.text: Adding particles at fixed temperature raises pressure without changing the average-speed cue much. More particles means more wall hits each second.
items.tryThis: Switch between Room baseline and More particles same box, then compare the density cue and the pressure-particle-count graph.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents the shortcut that every pressure change must come from temperature.

items.text: Hotter same box and More particles same box can reach similar pressures. The gauge can match while the speed cue and density cue tell different microscopic stories.
items.tryThis: In compare mode, keep one setup hotter and the other more crowded until the two pressure readings are close.
items.whyItMatters: It shows why pressure alone does not determine temperature or particle number.

items.text: Read the ideal gas law as one summary of speed, crowding, and box size, not as four unrelated symbols.
items.tryThis: Hold two variables fixed, move one slider, and use the matching response graph to explain the pressure change.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the law into a causal model instead of a formula to memorize.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict before you move the slider. Each scenario asks which microscopic part of the pressure story is changing.

items.prompt: Starting from Room baseline, you reduce only the volume while keeping the same particle count and temperature. Which statement best matches the bench?

choices.label: Pressure rises because the same particles strike the walls more often in the smaller box.
Pressure stays the same because the particles are not individually faster.
Pressure falls because the particles have less distance to travel.

items.explanation: At fixed particle number and temperature, compression increases density and shortens the distance to the walls, so wall hits become more frequent and pressure rises.
items.observationHint: The motion streaks stay similar, but the wall-hit cue and pressure gauge both increase.

items.prompt: Starting from Room baseline, you raise only the temperature. What should happen to the pressure?

choices.label: Pressure rises because particles move faster, hit the walls more often, and transfer more momentum on each hit.
Pressure stays the same because the number of particles and the box size have not changed.
Pressure falls because faster particles spend less time near the walls.

items.explanation: At fixed $N$ and $V$, hotter gas means faster particles. That raises both the collision rate and the momentum change per hit, so pressure rises.
items.observationHint: The motion streaks lengthen, wall hits become more frequent, and the pressure-temperature graph rises.

items.prompt: Starting from Room baseline, you increase only particle count. Which statement best matches the bench?

choices.label: Pressure rises even though the average particle speed stays about the same.
Pressure stays the same because temperature sets particle speed and temperature did not change.
Pressure falls because a crowded box leaves each particle less room to accelerate.

items.explanation: At fixed temperature and volume, average speed stays tied to temperature, but more particles mean more wall hits, so pressure still rises.
items.observationHint: The speed cue changes little, but density and pressure both rise.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the live gas box to hit pressure targets. The checks read the current state, so you have to create the right pressure for the right reason.

items.title: Double the pressure by compression
items.prompt: Start from Room baseline and lower only the volume until the pressure is about double while temperature and particle count stay near the baseline values.
items.successMessage: You doubled the pressure by compression alone. The particles were not individually faster; they were just reaching the walls more often in the smaller box.

setup.note: Do not use temperature or particle count to help. The point is to raise pressure by compression alone.

hints: Use the pressure-volume graph first. Halving the baseline volume is the cleanest route.
If temperature drifts away from the baseline, you are changing two causes at once.

checks.label: Open the pressure-volume graph.
Keep the pressure gauge visible.
Keep the particle count near the baseline.
Keep the temperature near the baseline.
Compress the gas to about half the original volume.
Reach about double the original pressure.

items.title: Match the pressure with a different cause
items.prompt: Start from Hotter same box, switch to compare mode, and edit only Setup B until it reaches about the same pressure while staying cooler and using more particles instead of more temperature.
items.successMessage: You matched the pressure with a different microscopic cause. Setup A used faster particles, while Setup B used more particles in the same box.

setup.note: Leave Setup A hot. Make Setup B cooler and more crowded.

hints: Keep the box size close to the same in both setups, then let particle count do the compensating work in Setup B.
The pressure badges should match before the speed cue and density cue do.

checks.label: Stay in compare mode while editing Setup B.
Open the pressure-particle-count graph.
Keep the pressure gauge visible.
Keep Setup A hot.
Keep Setup A near the target pressure.
Keep Setup B at nearly the same box size.
Keep Setup B cooler than Setup A.
Raise Setup B's particle count instead.
Match Setup B to about the same pressure.

items.title: Heat Transfer
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Sets the block's starting temperature, so it sets the initial temperature gap.
Changes the room and bench temperature around the block.
Changes how strong the solid-conduction path is.
Changes how well the block actually touches the bench.
Changes the size of the boundary through which energy can leave.

controls.label: Airflow
controls.description: Makes the surrounding air more still or more strongly moving.

presets.label: Metal on cool bench
presets.description: Strong conductivity and good contact make conduction dominate early.

presets.label: Loose contact
presets.description: Weak contact reduces conduction, so convection and radiation matter more.

presets.label: Windy room
presets.description: Strong airflow makes convection the largest pathway even though the conduction path still exists.

presets.label: Radiation-dominant
presets.description: A very hot block with weak contact and still air can make radiation the largest pathway.

overlays.label: Temperature-gap bridge
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the shared temperature difference and the net flow direction from hotter toward cooler.
whatToNotice: As the two temperature chips move closer together, all three pathway rates shrink because they share the same driving temperature difference.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps heat tied to a temperature difference instead of treating heat as something stored in the block.

overlays.label: Pathway split
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the separate conduction, convection, and radiation channels plus the total built from them.
whatToNotice: The total rate is just the sum of the three visible pathways, not a fourth hidden process.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you see which pathway is actually setting the total at any moment.

overlays.label: Area cue
overlays.shortDescription: Highlights the contact patch and exposed outline so the surface-area control has a visible meaning.
whatToNotice: Increasing area raises all three rates because it gives energy more boundary to cross.
overlays.whyItMatters: It gives the area control a visible physical meaning.

overlays.label: Environment cue
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the room sink and airflow arrows so convection stays attached to the surrounding fluid.
whatToNotice: Changing airflow leaves the solid-contact path nearly unchanged while the convection arrows and convection rate respond right away.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates convection through moving air from conduction through the bench.

graphs.label: Block and room temperatures
graphs.description: Track the hot block against the room. As the temperature gap shrinks, the pathway rates below should shrink too.

graphs.label: Pathway rates over time
graphs.description: Compare conduction, convection, radiation, and the total on one axis. The total line should match the sum of the three pathway lines.

graphs.label: Contact response
graphs.description: Change only contact quality. This isolates how strongly the solid-contact path can affect the total rate.

graphs.label: Rate vs temperature contrast
graphs.description: Change only the starting temperature difference. Radiation curves upward more strongly here than conduction and convection.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Each one links one control change to the temperature gap and the pathway split.

items.text: Changing material conductivity or contact quality changes only the conduction path directly. That is the clearest sign that conduction depends on the solid connection, not on the airflow.
items.tryThis: Switch between Loose contact and Metal on cool bench, then compare the conduction line and the contact-response graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the contact path from the air path before later topics ask what the transferred energy does.

items.text: Changing airflow leaves the solid-contact path nearly unchanged while the convection arrows and convection curve respond immediately. That is the cleanest way to separate convection from conduction.
items.tryThis: Start from Windy room, then lower only the airflow and watch the blue convection curve drop while the conduction line barely changes.
items.whyItMatters: Convection is about the moving fluid carrying energy away, not about a hidden solid bridge.

items.text: A larger area raises all three pathways because it gives energy more boundary to cross. Area is not just a conduction idea.
items.tryThis: Raise only the surface area and compare the pathway bar before and after. The whole bar grows, not just one segment.
items.whyItMatters: Area will matter again when later thermal problems compare small and large objects that cool or heat at different rates.

items.text: On the contrast graph, radiation bends upward more strongly than conduction and convection. That is the sign that radiation becomes much more important at large temperature differences.
items.tryThis: Hover a small delta T and a large delta T on the contrast graph, then compare how much the amber radiation curve catches up to the others.
items.whyItMatters: This gives you the right early intuition for why very hot objects can radiate strongly.
It separates transfer-rate causes now, before later concepts ask how that transferred energy changes temperature or supports phase change.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the pathway split before you move the control. Change one cause, then check which part of the rate bar responds.

items.prompt: Starting from Loose contact, you improve only the contact quality while keeping the same temperatures. What should change first?

choices.label: Conduction rises sharply and the total rate rises, while convection and radiation change much less at that instant.
Only radiation rises because better contact makes the block glow more strongly.
Nothing changes until the block has already cooled for a while.

items.explanation: Better contact strengthens the solid-conduction path immediately. Because the temperature gap is the same at the start, conduction is the part that jumps first.
items.observationHint: Watch the conduction bar grow first while the two temperature chips are still almost unchanged.

items.prompt: From Radiation-dominant, you turn up only the airflow while keeping the block just as hot. Which statement is strongest?

choices.label: Convection rises and may overtake radiation, while the contact path stays almost the same.
Conduction rises most because moving air improves the bench contact.
All three pathways stay fixed because the temperatures were not changed.

items.explanation: Airflow belongs to convection, not to the solid-contact path. With the same temperatures, changing only airflow mainly changes the convection share.
items.observationHint: Watch the convection arrows and blue curve first.

items.prompt: Starting from Windy room, you raise only the hot-block temperature. Which change is most important to expect at large temperature contrast?

choices.label: All three rates rise, but radiation grows more strongly at the large-contrast end of the graph.
Only conduction rises because temperature contrast does not affect convection or radiation.
The total rate must stay the same because the room temperature did not move.

items.explanation: A larger temperature difference increases all three pathways, but radiation increases most strongly at high contrast because of the fourth-power dependence.
items.observationHint: Open the contrast-response graph and compare the amber curve with the nearly straight conduction and convection lines.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the live bench to create target heat-flow situations. The checks read the current temperature gap and pathway rates, so the physics stays tied to one visible state.

hints: Keep the hot-block and room temperatures matched so the delta-T badges stay close.
Use contact quality or material conductivity to change the conduction path without changing the temperature gap.

targets.label: Keep Setup A temperaturecontrast between 123 degC and 127 degC.
Keep Setup B temperaturecontrast between 123 degC and 127 degC.
Keep Setup B contactquality between 0.15 and 0.3.
Keep Setup B totalrate between 18 u/s and 30 u/s.

items.title: Make radiation the biggest path
items.prompt: Tune the live setup until radiation is the largest pathway while the block is still clearly hotter than the room.
items.successMessage: You created a case where weak contact and still air keep conduction and convection small enough for radiation to dominate.

setup.note: Keep the pathway-rates graph open so you can see the amber radiation line overtake the others.

hints: Radiation likes a large temperature contrast.
To keep radiation largest, do not accidentally strengthen the contact path or the airflow too much.

targets.label: Keep hottemperature between 165 degC and 18 degC.
Keep contactquality between 0 and 0.12.
Keep airflow between 0.3 and 0.5.
Keep radiationrate between 8 u/s and 13 u/s.
Keep conductionrate between 0 u/s and 3 u/s.
Keep convectionrate between 0 u/s and 7 u/s.

items.title: Torque
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Set how hard the push is without changing its angle or position.
Rotate the force relative to the bar. This changes the perpendicular component and can flip the torque sign.

controls.label: Distance from pivot
controls.description: Move the push point closer to or farther from the pivot. The same force gives a different torque when the lever arm changes.

presets.label: Handle right-angle push
presets.description: Reference case: a perpendicular push at the handle.

presets.label: Near-hinge same push
presets.description: Same force and angle, half the distance from the pivot, so the torque is halved.

presets.label: Shallow but stronger
presets.description: A bigger force at $30^\circ$ keeps the perpendicular component and torque matched to the reference case.

presets.label: Line through pivot
presets.description: Large force, but almost zero torque because the line of action passes through the pivot.

presets.label: Clockwise push
presets.description: A perpendicular push in the opposite direction produces clockwise torque.

overlays.label: Perpendicular component
overlays.shortDescription: Separates the force into the twisting part and the part along the bar.
whatToNotice: The perpendicular part is what sets the torque.
A large component along the bar can look dramatic while adding little or no turning effect.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns $\tau = rF\sin\phi = rF_\perp$ into something you can see.

overlays.label: Line of action
overlays.shortDescription: Extends the force direction so you can see whether it misses the pivot or passes through it.
whatToNotice: When this line passes through the pivot, the moment arm is zero and the torque vanishes.
Changing only the angle can move the same push from a strong-torque case to a near-zero-torque case.
overlays.whyItMatters: Torque depends on the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the force line, not just on arrow length.

overlays.label: Moment arm
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the shortest distance from the pivot to the line of action.
whatToNotice: The same force produces more torque when this distance is larger.
The moment arm can shrink to nearly zero even when the hand stays far from the pivot.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the geometry behind $r\sin\phi$ visible instead of leaving it as a memorized formula.

graphs.label: Torque vs time
graphs.description: For one fixed setup, torque stays constant because distance from the pivot, force size, and angle relative to the bar stay fixed.

graphs.label: Angular speed vs time
graphs.description: With constant angular acceleration from rest, angular speed changes linearly.

graphs.label: Rotation angle vs time
graphs.description: Rotation angle curves because the bar keeps gaining angular speed as it turns.

graphs.label: Torque vs force angle
graphs.description: Shows how torque changes with force angle for the current force size and distance. The turning effect is largest near $\pm 90^\circ$ and near zero when the line of action passes through the pivot.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice about torque
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts while changing one control at a time. Match what you see on the bar to the moment arm, line of action, and graphs.

items.text: Notice that the same perpendicular push twists much more at the handle than near the pivot.
items.tryThis: Switch between the handle right-angle push and the near-hinge version. The force arrow stays the same, but the torque and final rotation should drop by about half.
items.whyItMatters: This isolates distance from every other factor.

items.text: On the angle map, torque is largest near $\pm 90^\circ$ and near zero at $0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$.
items.tryThis: Scrub across the sine-shaped response curve. Positive and negative angles flip the torque sign, while line-through-pivot angles sit near zero.
items.whyItMatters: It makes the $\sin\phi$ factor visible instead of leaving it as a rule to memorize.

items.text: A large force can still make almost no torque when its line of action passes through the pivot.
items.tryThis: Turn on the line of action, then aim the force back along the bar. The moment arm should collapse even though the force readout stays large.
items.whyItMatters: It breaks the mistake that a bigger force automatically means a bigger turning effect.

items.text: Notice in compare mode that different geometries can still land on the same torque if $rF_\perp$ matches.
items.tryThis: Keep Setup A at the handle, shorten Setup B toward the pivot, then raise the perpendicular part of the push until the torque graph lines up again.
items.whyItMatters: Torque is a product, so there are multiple honest ways to build the same turning effect.

items.text: Changing only the force angle can weaken, remove, or reverse the torque without moving the hand.
items.tryThis: Slide the angle from $90^\circ$ through $0^\circ$ to $-90^\circ$. Watch the perpendicular component cross through zero and then flip sign.
items.whyItMatters: Direction decides both the size and the sign of the turning effect.

items.text: Changing only the application point changes torque immediately, even if the force arrow looks the same.
items.tryThis: Keep the same right-angle push and slide the application point inward. The moment arm, torque graph, and final angle should shrink together.
items.whyItMatters: This is why a door opens more easily at the handle than near the hinge.

predictionMode.title: Predict the turning effect
predictionMode.intro: Predict the torque first, then test it on the bar and graphs. The aim is to connect distance, force direction, and rotational response.

items.prompt: If the same $2\,\mathrm{N}$ perpendicular push moves from the handle to the middle of the bar, what should happen?

choices.label: Torque and the end-of-clip rotation should both drop because the lever arm is shorter.
Torque should stay the same because the force direction is still perpendicular.
The bar should rotate more because the push is now closer to the pivot.

items.explanation: Keeping the same perpendicular force while cutting $r$ in half cuts the torque in half, so angular speed and rotation build more slowly.
items.observationHint: Half the distance should halve the torque, so the bar gains angular speed more slowly and turns less by the end of the clip.

items.prompt: If you replace the handle right-angle push with a stronger but shallower push that keeps $F_\perp$ the same, what should stay almost unchanged?

choices.label: The torque and the end-of-clip turning should stay nearly the same even though the force arrow changes.
The torque must get much larger because the total force is larger.
The torque must vanish because the force is no longer perpendicular.

items.explanation: Torque depends on the perpendicular component, not on total force alone. If $F_\perp$ and the distance from the pivot stay matched, the turning effect stays matched too.
items.observationHint: Compare the torque graph and end-of-clip angle with the reference case. Matching $F_\perp$ should match the turning effect.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same live bench to prove that you can reason about lever arm distance, force direction, and matched turning effects without hiding behind memorized formulas.

setup.note: Turn on the line of action and use the torque-angle map together.

hints: Do not solve this by sliding all the way to the pivot. Keep the distance long and change the direction instead.
The target is not a small force. It is a small moment arm.

targets.label: Keep the application point between $1.35$ and $1.6\,\mathrm{m}$.
Make the torque land between $-0.12$ and $0.12\,\mathrm{N\,m}$.

items.title: Same torque, shorter lever arm
items.prompt: Open compare mode and make Setup B twist just as hard as Setup A even though Setup B pushes much closer to the pivot.
items.successMessage: You matched the turning effect across two different geometries, which means you compensated through the perpendicular part of the force instead of relying on distance alone.

setup.note: Clone the handle push, then shorten only Setup B while keeping the torque graph aligned.

hints: Setup A can stay at the handle. Shorten only Setup B, then rebuild the same torque by changing the perpendicular part of the push.
You do not need identical arrows. You need the same value of $rF_\perp$.

targets.label: Keep Setup A near the handle between $1.55$ and $1.6\,\mathrm{m}$.
Keep Setup A torque between $3.15$ and $3.25\,\mathrm{N\,m}$.
Move Setup B closer to the pivot between $0.75$ and $0.85\,\mathrm{m}$.
Bring Setup B torque back between $3.15$ and $3.25\,\mathrm{N\,m}$.

items.title: Clockwise finish
items.prompt: Pause at the end of the clip and tune a clean clockwise twist.
items.successMessage: You built a steady clockwise torque and let it accumulate into the matching end-of-clip speed and angle.

setup.note: Use the end-of-clip inspect state so the speed and angle readouts refer to the same moment.

hints: A clockwise twist needs negative torque, so the perpendicular component must point the opposite way.
Once torque is in range, angular speed and angle should fall into place because the bench inertia stays fixed.

targets.label: Keep torque between $-2.55$ and $-2.25\,\mathrm{N\,m}$.
Make angular speed land between $-0.9$ and $-0.76\,\mathrm{rad/s}$.
Make rotation angle land between $-1.08$ and $-0.9\,\mathrm{rad}$.

items.title: Equivalent Resistance
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Changes the source push for the whole reduced circuit.

controls.label: Outer resistor R1
controls.description: This resistor always stays in series with the reduced group.

controls.label: Grouped resistor R2
controls.description: Changes the top or first resistor inside the highlighted group.

controls.label: Grouped resistor R3
controls.description: Changes the bottom or second resistor in the highlighted group and drives the response-graph sweep.

controls.label: Use a parallel grouped pair
controls.description: Off keeps the highlighted pair in series. On makes the highlighted pair parallel before it is combined with R1.

presets.label: Balanced series group
presets.description: A symmetric starting point where the highlighted pair adds directly before R1 is included.

presets.label: Balanced parallel group
presets.description: The same resistor values now reduce through the parallel rule first, giving a much smaller grouped equivalent before R1 is added.

presets.label: Unequal series group
presets.description: The grouped series resistor with larger resistance takes a larger share of the group drop.

presets.label: Unequal parallel group
presets.description: Both grouped branches share the same group voltage, but the lower-resistance branch draws more current and charge per second.

presets.label: Low-voltage parallel group
presets.description: The reduction order stays the same while the whole circuit runs more gently.

overlays.label: Current flow
overlays.shortDescription: Shows live current arrows and charge counters through the grouped circuit.
whatToNotice: R1 always carries the total current. The highlighted pair only splits that current when the pair is parallel.
overlays.whyItMatters: The simplified circuit should still agree with the live charge flow you can see.

overlays.label: Voltage labels
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the source voltage, the outer-resistor drop, the grouped-block drop, and the live R3 drop.
whatToNotice: The grouped block takes one shared voltage drop before you decide how that drop is distributed inside the highlighted pair.
overlays.whyItMatters: This keeps the reduction honest by tying the grouped block back to the full circuit voltage story.

overlays.label: Reduction guide
overlays.shortDescription: Highlights the only pair you should reduce first and shows its live grouped equivalent.
whatToNotice: The highlighted pair is reduced first. R1 is not combined until that group has already become one resistor.
overlays.whyItMatters: This makes reduction order visible instead of turning it into a symbol trick.

overlays.label: Node guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the shared group nodes that justify the parallel reduction.
whatToNotice: When the highlighted pair is parallel, both grouped resistors connect across the same two group nodes and therefore share the same group voltage.
overlays.whyItMatters: The shared-node picture explains both the parallel reduction rule and the live branch behavior inside the group.

graphs.label: Reduction sweep vs R3
graphs.description: Sweep only R3 while keeping the battery, R1, R2, and the group mode fixed. First read the grouped-equivalent curve, then add the fixed outer resistor R1 to understand the total-equivalent curve.

graphs.label: Current sweep vs R3
graphs.description: Sweep only R3 while keeping the rest of the circuit fixed. In a series group, all currents stay locked together. In a parallel group, R2 and R3 split the grouped current while R1 still carries the total current.

graphs.label: Voltage share vs R3
graphs.description: Sweep only R3 while keeping the source, R1, R2, and the group mode fixed. The outer-resistor drop and the grouped-block drop always add to the battery voltage. In a parallel group, R3 gets the full group voltage; in a series group, it gets only part of that drop.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep one eye on the highlighted pair and one on the full circuit. The important question is always: what changes when that group is reduced honestly?

items.text: Start with the highlighted pair, not the whole circuit. Reduce only a group that truly shares one current path or one pair of nodes.
items.tryThis: Toggle the grouped pair between series and parallel without touching R1. Watch the reduction card change before the total current changes.
items.whyItMatters: Reduction order is the main new skill in this concept.

items.text: The total-equivalent curve stays a fixed amount above the grouped curve because R1 is added only after the highlighted pair has been reduced.
items.tryThis: Hold R1 at 4 ohm and compare the vertical gap between the two curves as you preview different R3 values.
items.whyItMatters: The graph turns a two-step symbolic process into something you can see.

items.text: When the highlighted pair is parallel, the node guide explains why both grouped branches keep the same group voltage even though their currents differ.
items.tryThis: Turn on the node guide in parallel-group mode and compare it with the voltage-share graph.
items.whyItMatters: The same two shared nodes justify both the reduction rule and the live branch behavior.

items.text: The current graph is the honesty check. In a series group, all currents match. In a parallel group, only the branch currents split while R1 still carries the total.
items.tryThis: Use the unequal grouped preset and preview larger R3 values. The blue branch current falls, but the outer-resistor current is still the total of both grouped branches.
items.whyItMatters: Equivalent resistance should agree with the visible current flow, not replace it.

items.text: Charge counters show the time consequence of the group rule. In a series group, Q2 and Q3 rise together. In a parallel group, they separate because the branch currents differ.
items.tryThis: Pause later in time with the unequal parallel-group preset, then switch to the unequal series-group preset and compare Q2 and Q3.
items.whyItMatters: The reduction step changes a real time-based outcome, not just a static number.

items.text: Compare mode is strongest here when both setups keep the same resistor values but change only the grouped-pair mode. That isolates reduction order as the only structural change.
items.tryThis: Clone the balanced series-group preset into Setup B, switch only Setup B to a parallel grouped pair, and compare the ghost circuit, reduction card, and current graph.
items.whyItMatters: You can trust the causal change because only the grouped relationship changed.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the honest reduction before you switch the circuit. Each scenario changes one group feature at a time so the cause stays visible.

items.prompt: The circuit starts with V = 12 V, R1 = 4 ohm, and a balanced highlighted pair of R2 = 6 ohm and R3 = 6 ohm in series. If you switch only the highlighted pair to parallel, which statement is most defensible?

choices.label: The grouped equivalent drops from 12 ohm to 3 ohm, the total equivalent drops from 16 ohm to 7 ohm, and the total current rises.
The grouped equivalent stays 12 ohm because R1 is still in series with the whole circuit.
The total current falls because the highlighted pair now contains two separate branches.

items.explanation: Only the highlighted pair changes. Switching it from series to parallel makes the grouped equivalent much smaller, so the final total equivalent drops and the battery current rises.
items.observationHint: Watch the grouped equivalent change first, then the total-current badge and graph respond.

items.prompt: In series-group mode with V = 12 V, R1 = 4 ohm, and R2 = 4 ohm, what happens if R3 rises from 4 ohm to 12 ohm?
items.scenarioLabel: Increase R3 in a series group

choices.label: The grouped equivalent rises, the total current falls, and R3 takes a larger share of the grouped voltage.
The total current stays fixed because R1 still carries the same loop current as before.
R3 voltage must fall because a larger resistor always gets less voltage.

items.explanation: In a series group, increasing R3 increases the grouped equivalent and the final total equivalent. The same current still flows through both grouped resistors, so the larger R3 also gets a larger share of the group voltage.
items.observationHint: The current falls everywhere, while the blue share of the grouped voltage grows.

items.prompt: In parallel-group mode with V = 12 V, R1 = 4 ohm, and R2 = 4 ohm, what happens if R3 rises from 4 ohm to 12 ohm?
items.scenarioLabel: Increase R3 in a parallel group

choices.label: The grouped equivalent rises, the total current falls, and the R3 branch current falls while R2 still keeps the same group voltage.
The grouped equivalent falls because the larger R3 gives the current another path.
The R3 branch current rises because the grouped block now stores more charge.

items.explanation: In a parallel group, increasing R3 makes that branch weaker and increases the grouped equivalent. The whole circuit draws less total current, and the R3 branch current falls even though R3 still sees the group voltage.
items.observationHint: The blue branch current and blue charge counter shrink, even while the branch still shares the group's voltage.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same grouped circuit to hit reduction targets. The checks come from the live reduction card, current response, and voltage behavior, not from a separate answer sheet.

items.title: Build the 3 ohm parallel group
items.prompt: Starting from the balanced series group, change only the grouped connection until the highlighted pair is about 3 ohm and the full circuit is about 7 ohm.
items.successMessage: You reduced the right pair first and landed on the honest mixed-circuit answer. The grouped equivalent and the total equivalent now agree with the live current response.

setup.note: Use the reduction guide and the equivalent sweep together while you change only the grouped-pair mode.

hints: Keep the resistor values unchanged. The target comes from changing the grouped relationship, not the resistor sizes.
The grouped equivalent should drop below either grouped resistor before R1 is added back in series.

targets.label: Keep resistance1 between 3.8 ohm and 4.2 ohm.
Keep resistance2 between 5.8 ohm and 6.2 ohm.
Keep resistance3 between 5.8 ohm and 6.2 ohm.
Keep groupequivalentresistance between 2.9 ohm and 3.1 ohm.
Keep equivalentresistance between 6.9 ohm and 7.1 ohm.
Keep totalcurrent between 1.67 A and 1.76 A.

items.title: Radioactivity and Half-Life
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Set how many nuclei start in the tray so you can compare small-sample noise with large-sample regularity.
Set the half-life that defines the equal-interval halving law on the time axis.

presets.label: Single nucleus
presets.description: One nucleus shows pure yes-or-no survival while the expected curve still reads 50% after one half-life.

presets.label: Small noisy sample
presets.description: A dozen nuclei make the sample curve noticeably jumpy around the smooth expectation.

presets.label: Class-lab sample
presets.description: A medium sample makes the expectation readable while sample noise is still visible.

presets.label: Large smooth sample
presets.description: A larger tray makes the step curve hug the smooth expectation more closely.

presets.label: Short half-life
presets.description: The same sample now decays over a shorter time scale, so the checkpoints arrive quickly.

presets.label: Long half-life
presets.description: The same sample now decays over a longer time scale, spreading the same checkpoint story outward.

overlays.label: Recent decays
overlays.shortDescription: Highlights nuclei that decayed recently so the tray does not look as if it fades smoothly.
whatToNotice: The tray changes one nucleus at a time even when the expected curve is smooth.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the one-by-one nature of decay visible.

overlays.label: Sample versus expectation
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the stepped actual count and the smooth expectation together.
whatToNotice: The actual tray can sit above or below the smooth curve while still following the same decay law overall.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates random single events from predictable large-sample behavior.

overlays.label: Half-life markers
overlays.shortDescription: Marks equal half-life checkpoints so repeated halving stays visible.
whatToNotice: Each equal checkpoint halves the expectation again instead of subtracting a fixed number.
overlays.whyItMatters: It prevents half-life from being misread as a linear drop.

overlays.label: Single versus sample
overlays.shortDescription: Keeps one-nucleus language beside the sample curve.
whatToNotice: One nucleus is either still present or gone, while the sample curve is an average expectation.
overlays.whyItMatters: It prevents 50 percent survival from being misread as half a nucleus or a countdown timer.

graphs.label: Remaining nuclei vs time
graphs.description: Shows the stepped actual count and the smooth expected count together so one-by-one decays can be compared with the exponential trend.

graphs.label: Remaining fraction vs time
graphs.description: Shows the same decay story as a fraction of the start, so samples of different sizes can be compared without changing the law.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time so the tray, readout card, and graphs stay tied to the same decay story.

items.text: At one half-life, a single nucleus is not 'half there'. It is either still present or gone. The 50 percent value is a survival probability for one nucleus and an expectation for a sample.
items.tryThis: Open Single nucleus and scrub to one half-life.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps probability honest at the single-nucleus level.

items.text: Equal half-life intervals repeatedly halve the expected sample. They do not remove a fixed number each second, so the curve bends instead of staying straight.
items.tryThis: Start from Class-lab sample and scrub from one half-life to two half-lives.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you read half-life as repeated multiplication, not linear loss.

items.text: Small samples can land noticeably above or below the expectation, but larger samples hug the same curve more closely because many independent decays are being averaged.
items.tryThis: Compare Small noisy sample with Large smooth sample at the same half-life.
items.whyItMatters: It links randomness in single events to regularity in large samples.

items.text: Compare mode works best when the half-life stays the same but the sample size changes. That separates randomness in the tray from the shared decay law in the graphs.
items.tryThis: Keep Setup A on Small noisy sample and Setup B on Large smooth sample.
items.whyItMatters: Side by side, you can see the same decay law with different amounts of noise.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict what should happen before you scrub time or change the sample.

items.prompt: Start from Class-lab sample and scrub to one half-life. Which statement should stay honest?

choices.label: The expected sample is cut in half, but the actual tray can land near that value rather than exactly on it.
Exactly half of the nuclei must disappear at that instant because half-life is a schedule.
The curve should stay straight because each second removes the same number of nuclei.

items.explanation: After one half-life, the expectation is 50 percent of the start, but the actual tray still changes by whole nuclei at random times.
items.observationHint: Use the half-life markers, then compare the stepped count with the smooth expectation.

items.prompt: Keep the same half-life but raise the sample size from Small noisy sample to Large smooth sample. What should the page do?

choices.label: The actual fraction should wobble less around the same expectation curve because more independent decays are being averaged.
The half-life itself should become longer because larger samples decay more slowly.
The expected fraction curve should tilt upward because there are more nuclei to start with.

items.explanation: Changing sample size changes how noisy the actual result looks, not the half-life law itself. The fraction expectation stays the same, but the larger sample looks steadier.
items.observationHint: Jump between the presets or use compare mode, then focus on the fraction graph.

items.prompt: From the same starting sample, scrub from one half-life to two half-lives. Which statement is correct about the expectation?

choices.label: The expected sample falls to one quarter of the start because the halving repeats over equal intervals.
The expected sample falls to zero because two half-lives means nothing can remain.
The expected sample loses the same number of nuclei as it did during the first half-life.

items.explanation: After a second equal interval, the expectation is halved again, so it becomes one quarter of the starting sample.
items.observationHint: Watch the half-life markers and the fraction graph together.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same bounded decay bench for compact half-life and sample-noise targets instead of widening into a full nuclear-physics system.

items.title: Land on the one-half-life checkpoint
items.prompt: Starting from Class-lab sample, scrub to about one half-life so the expectation is halved while the live tray stays slightly below it.
items.successMessage: You landed on the one-half-life checkpoint and kept the actual tray and smooth expectation aligned honestly.

setup.note: Use the half-life markers and the dashed expectation together, then scrub the time rail.

hints: Keep the sample on the 64-nucleus class-lab setting.
At one half-life, the expectation should be about 32 nuclei while the live tray can sit a little lower.

targets.label: Keep samplesize between 64 nuclei and 64 nuclei.
Keep halflifeseconds between 2.35 s and 2.45 s.
Keep elapsedhalflives between 0.98 and 1.02.
Keep expectedremainingcount between 31.6 nuclei and 32.4 nuclei.
Keep actualremainingcount between 26.5 nuclei and 28.5 nuclei.

items.title: Catch a noisy small sample
items.prompt: Starting from Small noisy sample, scrub to about one half-life so the live tray sits well below the smooth expectation and the spread is obvious.
items.successMessage: You caught a genuinely noisy small sample without changing the underlying half-life law.

setup.note: Use the fraction graph so the gap between the tray and the expectation stays easy to read.

hints: Keep the sample small so the same half-life law looks visibly noisier.
