items.text: Notice that the refraction map is not a time trace. Each point is a different incident angle for the same media pair.
items.tryThis: Hover farther to the right on the graph and watch the stage jump to the matching incident angle instead of advancing in time.
items.whyItMatters: That is why hovering changes the setup immediately instead of advancing the scene in time.

items.text: Notice the sign of theta_1 - theta_2: positive means the transmitted ray is closer to the normal, while negative means it is farther away.
items.tryThis: Switch between Air to glass and Glass to air while staying on the bend graph so you can see the sign flip.
items.whyItMatters: One graph now tells you both the size and the direction of the bend.

items.text: Notice that normal incidence is not a no-effect case. The speed can change even when the ray keeps the same direction.
items.tryThis: Use the Normal incidence preset, then compare the speed readout with the fact that both angles stay at zero.
items.whyItMatters: It separates speed change from bending.

items.text: Notice how compare mode isolates the medium contrast. Keep one setup fixed, then change only n2 or the incident angle in the variant.
items.tryThis: Clone Air to glass into Setup A and Setup B, then raise only n2 in Setup B before you read the bend graph.
items.whyItMatters: It lets you separate a stronger index contrast from a steeper incident angle without leaving the live page.

items.text: Notice that as the incident angle approaches the critical angle from below, the transmitted ray flattens along the boundary and the transmitted angle approaches 90 degrees.
items.tryThis: Turn on the critical-angle guide, use Glass to air, and slide the incident angle upward until the transmitted ray nearly runs along the interface.
items.whyItMatters: That is the clean limit between ordinary refraction and total internal reflection.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the bend first, then test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time so you can connect refractive index, speed, and angle.

items.prompt: Starting from Air to glass, what happens if you keep the same incident angle but make the lower medium even denser by raising n2?

choices.label: The transmitted ray bends closer to the normal and the lower-medium speed drops.
The transmitted ray bends farther from the normal because the boundary pushes harder.
Only the speed changes. The transmitted angle stays fixed because theta_1 stayed fixed.

items.explanation: Raising n2 lowers v2 and increases the contrast between the two media. Snell's law then requires a smaller transmitted angle, so the ray bends more toward the normal.
items.observationHint: Watch theta_2 shrink on the stage and on the angle map while the speed ratio v2/v1 drops below the air-to-glass baseline.

items.prompt: If the incident angle drops all the way to zero while the two indices stay different, what should you expect?

choices.label: The speed changes, but the transmitted ray keeps the same direction because both angles are zero.
The ray must still bend because n1 and n2 are different.
The boundary has no effect at all once theta_1 is zero.

items.explanation: At normal incidence, theta_1 = 0 so theta_2 = 0 as well. The speed can still change because the refractive indices are different.
items.observationHint: Apply the normal-incidence preset and compare the speed badges with the unchanged straight-through direction.

items.prompt: Starting from Glass to air, what happens if you increase the incident angle far enough above the critical angle?

choices.label: There is no real transmitted angle because Snell's law would require sin(theta_2) > 1.
The transmitted ray keeps opening away from the normal until it points straight down.
The transmitted ray flips to the other side of the normal in the lower medium.

items.explanation: When n1 is greater than n2, raising the incident angle can make the required transmitted sine exceed 1. Then no real transmitted angle exists, which is the threshold for total internal reflection.
items.observationHint: Apply the above-critical preset and look for the missing transmitted ray plus the critical-angle note in the readout.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the real ray diagram, not a separate puzzle state. The checks read the live angles, indices, and overlays from the same boundary you are editing.

items.title: Hit the transmitted-angle target
items.prompt: Starting from Air to glass, tune the setup until the refracted angle lands between 25 and 28 degrees while the speed ratio v2/v1 stays between 0.62 and 0.69.
items.successMessage: You matched the target by coordinating the speed ratio and Snell-angle response on the same boundary.

setup.note: Use the angle map and the stage together.

hints: A slightly denser lower medium makes the transmitted angle smaller.
The target speed ratio is below one, so the lower medium must stay slower than the top medium.

checks.label: Open the incident-to-refracted angle graph.
Keep the normal and angle guide visible.
Make theta_2 land between 25 and 28 degrees.
Keep v2/v1 between 0.62 and 0.69.

items.title: Compare a denser variant
items.prompt: Open compare mode from Air to glass. Leave Setup A near the baseline, then edit Setup B so the lower medium is noticeably denser and the incident angle is steeper.
items.successMessage: You used compare mode to isolate a stronger index contrast instead of eyeballing two unrelated rays.

setup.note: Keep A as the baseline while you edit B.

hints: Setup A should stay close to n2 = 1.5 so it remains the readable baseline.
Make Setup B both steeper and denser so its ray bends more strongly toward the normal.

checks.label: Open the bend graph.
Edit Setup B while compare mode is active.
Keep Setup A near the air-to-glass baseline.
Make Setup B use a denser lower medium.
Make Setup B use a steeper incident angle.

items.title: Electric Potential
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Positive values raise nearby potential and negative values lower it.
Changing Source B lets the second potential contribution reinforce, weaken, or reverse the net value.
Moves both sources symmetrically, changing distances and reshaping the potential landscape.
Moves the probe left or right across both the stage and the linked scan graphs.
Moves the probe to a different horizontal scan line, so the graphs sample a new y-level.
Changes only the potential energy U = qV, not the potential map or the field graph.

presets.label: Dipole reference
presets.description: Equal and opposite charges create a strong sign change across the map.

presets.label: Like-charge midpoint
presets.description: Equal positive charges show that the field can be zero at the midpoint while the potential stays positive.

presets.label: Zero-potential midpoint
presets.description: Equal and opposite charges make the midpoint a clean check that zero potential does not imply zero field.

presets.label: Single positive source
presets.description: Turning off Source B isolates one positive-source potential hill.

presets.label: Negative test charge
presets.description: The source-generated potential stays fixed while the test charge makes the potential energy change sign.

overlays.label: Potential map
overlays.shortDescription: Shows signed potential across the stage, with warm colors for positive V and cool colors for negative V.
whatToNotice: Each point on the map already includes both source contributions.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns potential difference into a visible change across one shared landscape instead of a detached number.

overlays.label: Equipotential contours
overlays.shortDescription: Draws lines of equal potential across the stage.
whatToNotice: The field arrow crosses these contours instead of pointing along them.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you see that the field comes from the slope of the potential, not from a separate unrelated rule.

overlays.label: Field arrow
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the net electric field at the probe as the downhill direction on the potential map.
whatToNotice: At a balance point, the field arrow can vanish even while the potential readout stays nonzero.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the link E = -grad V tied to the same live probe state.

overlays.label: Scan line
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the horizontal slice used by the linked graphs.
whatToNotice: Graph hover and stage preview refer to the same x-location on this line.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the graph tied to one real spatial slice through the map.

graphs.label: Potential along the scan line
graphs.description: Source A, Source B, and the net potential are sampled along the same horizontal scan line. The vertical gap between two x-positions is a potential difference.

graphs.label: Field-slope link
graphs.description: Along the current horizontal slice, the net horizontal field matches the negative slope of the potential graph. Hovering previews that same x-location on the stage.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Treat each prompt as a small investigation. Make the named change, then connect what you see on the map to what changes in the readout and graphs.

items.text: Source sign decides whether a nearby region is positive or negative potential before distance decides how large the value is.
items.tryThis: Turn Source B off, then drag Source A from positive to negative and watch the warm hill switch to a cool well.
items.whyItMatters: Potential-sign mistakes often come from forgetting that a negative source lowers V instead of making a smaller positive value.

items.text: Potential difference is the vertical gap between two points on the same V(x) curve, not a separate object.
items.tryThis: Hover two x-locations on the potential graph and compare the stage preview, the readout, and the height difference on the curve.
items.whyItMatters: Voltage becomes easier to read when you treat it as a change between two positions on one shared graph.

items.text: The field arrow points downhill on the potential map. Along this horizontal slice, the E_x curve follows the negative slope of V(x).
items.tryThis: Find a place where the potential graph drops sharply to the right. The field-link graph should show a positive E_x there.
items.whyItMatters: This is the key bridge between the scalar potential picture and the vector field picture.

items.text: At the midpoint between equal positive charges, the field can be zero while the potential stays positive.
items.tryThis: Use the like-charge midpoint preset and compare the warm map color, the positive V readout, and the vanished field arrow.
items.whyItMatters: It separates scalar superposition from vector cancellation in one clean setup.

items.text: Changing only q_test flips the sign of potential energy, not the potential map or the field-link graph.
items.tryThis: Keep the sources and probe fixed, then drag q_test through zero and into the negative side.
items.whyItMatters: It separates source-set potential from the energy of one chosen test charge.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict first, then apply the change. Each scenario changes one cause at a time so you can test what the map, contours, and graphs should do.

items.prompt: At one fixed probe point, what changes if Source B flips from positive to negative while Source A stays positive?

choices.label: Source B's contribution changes sign, so the net potential can shift sharply and the nearby map can switch from warm to cool.
Only the field changes because potential does not depend on source sign.
Nothing changes because the source stayed the same distance from the probe.

items.explanation: Potential is signed. Flipping a source charge changes that source's contribution from positive to negative or vice versa, so the net potential can change strongly even when the geometry stays fixed.
items.observationHint: Watch the region near Source B flip sign when the blue source changes sign.

items.prompt: If both sources are equal and positive, then the probe moves to the exact midpoint on the source axis, which statement is best supported?

choices.label: The potential stays positive while the field becomes zero because scalar addition and vector cancellation follow different rules.
Both potential and field must become zero because the probe is equally far from both charges.
The field must point upward because each charge pushes away from itself.

items.explanation: At the midpoint, the two positive-source fields are equal and opposite, so they cancel. But the two potentials are both positive scalars, so they add and leave a positive V.
items.observationHint: This is the cleanest place to see a warm map, a positive V readout, and a vanished field arrow at the same time.

items.prompt: If the sources and probe stay fixed but the test charge flips from positive to negative, what should happen?

choices.label: The potential energy changes sign while the potential map and field graph stay the same.
The potential map reverses because the probe charge now defines the sign of V.
Both potential and field vanish because the probe charge is no longer positive.

items.explanation: The source charges set V and E. The test charge only multiplies that existing potential to produce U = qV, so flipping q_test changes the energy sign without changing the map.
items.observationHint: The potential-energy readout changes sign while the map and graphs stay fixed.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the live controls to build a specific potential-field situation. Success depends on reading both the map and the field link from the same setup.

items.title: Build a positive midpoint with zero field
items.prompt: Starting from Dipole reference, adjust the setup until the midpoint has almost zero field but still clearly positive potential.
items.successMessage: You found the clean midpoint case where vector cancellation makes the field vanish while scalar addition keeps the potential positive.

setup.note: Use the field-link graph, the field arrow, and the potential map together while you tune the midpoint.

hints: You need the midpoint between two equal positive charges, not the midpoint of a dipole.
A nearly zero field means the local slope is almost flat. The map can still remain positive there.

targets.label: Keep sourcechargea between 1.9 q and 2.1 q.
Keep sourcechargeb between 1.9 q and 2.1 q.
Keep sourceseparation between 1.95 m and 2.05 m.
Keep probex between -0.05 m and 0.05 m.
Keep probey between -0.05 m and 0.05 m.
Keep potential between 3.8 and 4.2.
Keep fieldmagnitude between 0 and 0.08.

items.title: Power and Energy in Circuits
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Raises or lowers the source voltage without changing the load itself.
Changes how strongly the load limits current in this one-load ohmic-circuit model.

presets.label: Steady glow
presets.description: A balanced reference run with a moderate source and load.

presets.label: Gentle glow
presets.description: The same 8 ohm load at a lower source voltage shows a smaller current and much lower power.

presets.label: Boosted source
presets.description: The same 8 ohm load is driven by a larger source voltage, so current and power both increase strongly.

presets.label: High-resistance load
presets.description: With the same 12 V source, a larger load resistance gives a smaller current and lower power.

presets.label: Low-resistance heater
presets.description: A smaller load resistance draws more current, takes more power, and makes the energy rise faster each second.

overlays.label: Current arrows
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the one-loop current direction and the live current label on the wire.
whatToNotice: For one fixed load, the current changes immediately when you change the voltage or the resistance.
overlays.whyItMatters: Power formulas only make sense when the current stays tied to the same visible circuit.

overlays.label: Voltage labels
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the source voltage and the matching load drop in this single-load loop.
whatToNotice: The same source voltage appears across the one load in this bounded model.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the power formulas anchored to the actual voltage across the component.

overlays.label: Power glow
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the load response and the instantaneous power-rate bar on the stage.
whatToNotice: For one fixed setup, the power bar stays constant even while the energy meter keeps growing.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the transfer rate right now from the total energy transferred so far.

overlays.label: Energy meter
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the cumulative energy bar and the current elapsed time on the stage.
whatToNotice: At fixed power, the energy meter grows by equal amounts in equal time intervals.
overlays.whyItMatters: Energy is the accumulated total, not a second copy of power.

graphs.label: Energy transferred over time
graphs.description: For one fixed setup, the line is straight and its slope is the power. A steeper line means the circuit is transferring more energy each second.

graphs.label: Current vs source voltage
graphs.description: Sweep the source voltage while keeping the load resistance fixed. The straight line shows the Ohm's-law response for this one load.

graphs.label: Power vs source voltage
graphs.description: Sweep only the source voltage while the load stays fixed. The curve bends upward because power grows like V^2 for one fixed resistance.

graphs.label: Power vs load resistance
graphs.description: Sweep only the load resistance while the source stays fixed. In this model, larger resistance lowers current and therefore lowers power.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use each prompt as a short investigation. Make one change, then connect what the stage shows to what the graphs show.

items.text: On the energy graph, the slope is the power. A steeper line means more energy transferred each second, not just more time passing.
items.tryThis: Pause the run, compare the line slope for the steady-glow and low-resistance-heater setups, then compare the stage power bar.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest way to separate power from energy without leaving the same circuit.

items.text: With one fixed load, the current-voltage graph is a straight line because the resistance stays constant.
items.tryThis: Hover a lower and a higher source voltage and compare how the wire-current label scales on the stage.
items.whyItMatters: Current is the link between the source setting and the power formulas.

items.text: The power-voltage graph bends upward because raising V also raises I in the same load, so power grows faster than linearly.
items.tryThis: Compare the gentle-glow and boosted-source presets. The voltage increase is moderate, but the power increase is much more dramatic.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you see that power is not just another copy of current.

items.text: At one fixed source voltage, increasing the load resistance lowers both current and power in this model.
items.tryThis: Drag only the load resistance upward from the low-resistance-heater preset and watch the current label, power bar, and glow all fall together.
items.whyItMatters: This corrects the common mistake that a larger resistance number must mean the component uses more power.

items.text: Compare mode is most useful here when both setups are inspected at the same time but use different voltage or resistance values.
items.tryThis: Clone the steady-glow setup into Setup B, then lower only Setup B's resistance and compare the dashed ghost circuit, the power badges, and the energy line slopes.
items.whyItMatters: That isolates the causal parameter change instead of mixing time differences with circuit differences.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the circuit response before you apply the change. Each scenario changes one cause at a time so current, power, and energy rate stay linked to the same setup.

items.prompt: From the gentle-glow setup, if the source rises from 6 V to 12 V while the 8 ohm load stays fixed, which statement is most defensible?

choices.label: Current doubles and power becomes four times larger because the same load now gets both more voltage and more current.
Current doubles but power stays the same because resistance did not change.
Power only doubles because voltage doubled once.

items.explanation: At fixed resistance, current is proportional to voltage. Since P = VI, doubling both V and I makes the power four times larger.
items.observationHint: The current point doubles, but the power graph rises much more sharply.

items.prompt: At 12 V, what happens if the load resistance rises from 4 ohm to 16 ohm?

choices.label: Current and power both fall, so the energy line becomes less steep.
Current falls, but power rises because the resistance number is larger.
Current stays the same because the source voltage did not change.

items.explanation: With the source fixed, a larger resistance limits current more strongly. Lower current means lower power, so the circuit transfers energy more slowly each second.
items.observationHint: Watch the current label, power bar, and energy-graph slope all shrink together.

items.prompt: Two setups use the same 12 V source. One load is 16 ohm and the other is 4 ohm. Which setup transfers more energy each second?

choices.label: The 4 ohm load, because the same source can drive more current and therefore more power.
The 16 ohm load, because larger resistance always means larger power.
They transfer energy each second at the same rate because the source voltage is identical.

items.explanation: Energy transferred each second is power. At one fixed source voltage, the lower-resistance load draws the larger current and therefore the larger power.
items.observationHint: The lower-resistance setup has the steeper energy line because its power bar is higher.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same single-load circuit for compact power targets. The checks come from the live current and power, so rate and total stay connected to one honest setup.

items.title: Build a steady 18 W load
items.prompt: Starting from Gentle glow, keep the 8 ohm load and raise the source until the stage power bar is about 18 W.
items.successMessage: You hit the target without changing the load. The stronger source raised current and power together, exactly as the graph and stage should agree.

setup.note: Use the power-voltage graph and the stage power bar together while you raise the source.

hints: Do not change the load. The target comes from raising the voltage while the same 8 ohm resistor stays in place.
At the target, the current should be about 1.5 A at the same time the power is about 18 W.

targets.label: Keep voltage between 11.8 V and 12.2 V.
Keep loadresistance between 7.8 ohm and 8.2 ohm.
Keep current between 1.45 A and 1.55 A.
Keep power between 17.5 W and 18.5 W.

items.title: Gravitational Fields
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Changes the mass creating the field, so both the inward field and the force scale with M at every point.
Moves the probe left or right across both the stage and the linked scan graphs.
Moves the probe to a new horizontal scan line so the graphs sample a different field slice.
Changes only the force on the probe. It does not change the gravitational field created by the source mass.

presets.label: Axis near
presets.description: A 1 m on-axis probe gives a clean inverse-square reference case.

presets.label: Double distance
presets.description: At 2 m on the same axis, the field should drop to about one quarter of the 1 m case.

presets.label: Heavier source
presets.description: Doubling the source mass strengthens both the field and the force at the same probe point.

presets.label: Heavier probe
presets.description: A larger probe mass scales only the force response while the field stays fixed.

presets.label: Vertical overhead
presets.description: A point directly above the source makes the field point straight down.

overlays.label: Field grid
overlays.shortDescription: Shows sample field arrows across the stage.
whatToNotice: Every arrow points inward toward the source, but the arrows shrink quickly as the distance grows.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes field direction a whole-region pattern instead of something you infer from only one probe point.

overlays.label: Field vector
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the live field arrow and the radial line from the source to the probe.
whatToNotice: At every point, the teal arrow points back along the line toward the source mass.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the stage direction directly to the component graph and the field equation.

overlays.label: Force vector
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the force on the chosen probe mass using the same live field.
whatToNotice: Changing only the probe mass rescales the coral force arrow while the teal field arrow stays fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the field itself from the response of the object placed in that field.

overlays.label: Distance rings
overlays.shortDescription: Marks equal-distance circles around the source for inverse-square comparisons.
whatToNotice: The 1 m, 2 m, and 3 m rings make it easier to compare how quickly $|\vec{g}|$ falls as $r$ grows.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns inverse-square reasoning into a geometric comparison you can see before you calculate.

overlays.label: Graph scan line
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the horizontal slice sampled by both graphs.
whatToNotice: Changing the probe's y-position changes the entire graph because the graph is now sampling a different slice of the field.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the graph-to-stage link honest instead of making the graphs feel detached.

graphs.label: Field components on the scan line
graphs.description: Shows how the inward field splits into x- and y-components along the current horizontal slice.

graphs.label: Field and force magnitudes
graphs.description: Separates field strength from force on the chosen probe, so source-mass and probe-mass changes do not get mixed up.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the field
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to connect the stage to the graphs. The main questions are: what sets the field, what changes only the force, and how fast does the field fall with distance?

items.text: Field direction comes first: the gravitational field always points toward the source mass.
items.tryThis: Drag the probe around the source and keep the teal field arrow visible. The direction should keep rotating inward toward the center.
items.whyItMatters: Direction is part of the field, not an afterthought. Read the geometry before the magnitude.

items.text: On the same radial line, moving from 1 m to 2 m cuts $|\vec{g}|$ to about one quarter.
items.tryThis: Switch between Axis near and Double distance while keeping the rings and strength graph visible.
items.whyItMatters: It turns inverse-square into a visual distance comparison.

items.text: Changing only the probe mass moves the force curve and force arrow, not the field curve or field arrow.
items.tryThis: Keep the source mass and probe position fixed, then drag the probe mass slider upward.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the source-generated field from the response of the chosen object.

items.text: Changing source mass scales the whole field pattern while every arrow still points inward.
items.tryThis: Keep the probe fixed and raise the source mass from 2 kg to 4 kg. Both the field and force magnitudes should double at that point.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the source-strength idea from the distance idea before you combine them.
It is the fastest honest contrast between cause of a field and response to a field.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict what changes before you move the sliders. Then check the stage and the graphs against the same live field model.

items.prompt: At one fixed probe point, what must happen if the source mass doubles while the probe position and probe mass stay fixed?

choices.label: Both the field magnitude and the force magnitude double, and the direction stays inward.
The direction flips because a larger source mass points gravity outward.

items.explanation: The source mass creates the field, so increasing M scales the entire field at that point. With the same probe mass, the force scales by the same factor because F = m_test g.
items.observationHint: The teal field arrow and coral force arrow should both grow while keeping the same inward direction.

items.prompt: Starting from the 1 m axis case, what should happen when the probe moves to 2 m on the same axis and the masses stay fixed?

choices.label: The field and the force both fall to about one quarter of the 1 m case.
The field and the force both fall to about one half because the distance doubled.
Only the vertical component changes because the probe stayed on the axis.

items.explanation: For one source mass, |g| follows the inverse-square rule. Doubling the distance multiplies the denominator by four, so the field and the force both drop to about one quarter when the probe mass stays fixed.
items.observationHint: Use the distance rings and the strength graph together. The 2 m point should land near one quarter of the 1 m field.

items.prompt: If only the probe mass doubles at the same point, which statement is most defensible?

choices.label: The force doubles while the field stays the same because only the responding mass changed.
The field doubles because the gravitational interaction now involves more total mass.
The field direction flips because the probe is now heavier than before.

items.explanation: The source mass and the geometry determine the field. Changing only the probe mass changes F = m_test g, so the force rescales while the field itself stays fixed.
items.observationHint: The coral force curve should rise while the teal field curve stays put.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Tune the same one-mass field into compact targets. The checks read the live inverse-square model instead of a detached answer key.

items.title: Quarter the field by distance
items.prompt: Starting from Axis near, move the probe to the doubled-distance case on the same horizontal line so the field magnitude falls to about one quarter of the 1 m reference.
items.successMessage: You used the same one-mass field honestly: doubling the distance on the same radial line cut |g| to about one quarter.

setup.note: Keep the source mass and probe mass fixed. Use the rings and the response graph together.

hints: Stay on the horizontal axis so the comparison is only about distance, not direction.
The target is not half the field. For one mass, doubling r changes the inverse-square denominator by four.

targets.label: Keep probex between 1.95 m and 2.05 m.
Keep probey between -0.05 m and 0.05 m.
Keep fieldmagnitude between 0.48 and 0.52.
Keep forcemagnitude between 0.48 and 0.52.

items.title: Double the force, not the field
items.successMessage: You changed the response without rewriting the field: the coral force doubled while the teal field stayed fixed because only m_test changed.

setup.note: Leave the source mass and probe position alone. Only the probe mass should move.

hints: If the field should stay fixed, do not touch the source mass or the probe position.
Doubling m_test doubles |F| because F = m_test g.

targets.label: Keep testmass between 1.95 kg and 2.05 kg.
Keep fieldmagnitude between 0.49 and 0.51.
Keep forcemagnitude between 0.98 and 1.02.

items.title: Mirrors
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

keyIdeas: A plane mirror gives a virtual upright image the same distance behind the mirror as the object is in front.
For a concave mirror, an object outside F gives a real inverted image in front of the mirror, while an object inside F gives a virtual upright image behind it.
A convex mirror always gives a virtual upright reduced image for a real object in front.
The sign of d_i tells which side of the mirror the image is on, and the sign of m tells whether the image is upright or inverted.

controls.label: Curved mirror
controls.description: Turn this off for a plane mirror. Leave it on and use Concave mirror to switch between concave and convex cases with the same focal-length magnitude.

controls.label: Concave mirror
controls.description: With Curved mirror on, turn this off to switch from concave to convex while keeping the same |f|.

controls.label: Focal-length magnitude
controls.description: Controls where $F$ and $C$ sit for the curved-mirror cases.
Moves the object along the principal axis. For a concave mirror, crossing F changes the image from real to virtual.
Changes only the object size, so the image height changes by the same magnification factor without moving the image point.

presets.label: Plane mirror
presets.description: A same-size virtual image behind the mirror.

presets.label: Concave real image
presets.description: A concave mirror with the object outside F, giving a real inverted image in front of the mirror.

presets.label: Inside focus
presets.description: A concave mirror with the object inside F, giving a virtual upright enlarged image behind the mirror.

presets.label: Convex mirror
presets.description: A convex mirror with a virtual upright reduced image behind the mirror.

overlays.label: Equal-angle cue
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the normal at the pole and reminds you that the incident and reflected angles match there.
whatToNotice: Every mirror on this page still obeys the same equal-angle reflection rule at the surface.
overlays.whyItMatters: The different image behaviors come from how those reflected rays combine, not from a different reflection law.

overlays.label: F and C markers
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the focal point and center of curvature for the curved mirrors.
whatToNotice: For a concave mirror, crossing F changes the image from real to virtual.
overlays.whyItMatters: The focal markers anchor the standard mirror regions directly on the stage.

overlays.label: Principal rays
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the construction rays that locate the image point.
whatToNotice: Real images come from reflected rays that actually meet, while virtual images come from backward extensions.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the sign of d_i to visible ray behavior instead of to a memorized sign rule.

overlays.label: Distance and height guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the signed distances and the object-image height comparison used in magnification.
whatToNotice: The sign of m tells orientation, while |m| tells how much the image size has changed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the stage directly to the magnification relation.

graphs.label: Object to image map
graphs.description: Shows signed image distance against object distance for the current mirror. Positive values are real images in front of the mirror, and negative values are virtual images behind it.
Shows how m changes with object distance. Negative values are inverted images, positive values are upright images, and |m| gives the image-size scale.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use the live prompt to connect the law of reflection, the ray construction, and the graph branch for the current mirror.

items.text: Notice that every mirror on this page still obeys the same equal-angle rule at the surface. The different image behaviors come from where the reflected rays do or do not meet.
items.tryThis: Toggle between plane and concave mirrors, then watch the pole ray keep the same equal-angle rule while the second principal ray changes.
items.whyItMatters: Mirror type changes the geometry built from reflection, not the reflection law itself.

items.text: Notice that the mirror graphs are object-distance based, not time based. Hovering a point previews a different geometry immediately, not a later moment.
items.tryThis: Hover the image map and watch the object jump to the matching position while the reflected-ray geometry updates in one step.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps the graph honest: you are comparing families of setups, not stepping through time.

items.text: Notice that when a concave-mirror object crosses inside F, both d_i and m change sign together.
items.tryThis: Start from the concave real-image preset, then slide the object inward past $F$ and compare the image-map and magnification branches.
items.whyItMatters: That shared sign flip is the clean signal that the image has changed from real inverted to virtual upright.

items.text: Notice that a plane mirror places the image the same distance behind the mirror as the object is in front. The image is not inside the surface; it is set by the backward extensions of the reflected rays.
items.tryThis: Turn Curved mirror off, then use the distance guide to compare $d_o$ and $d_i$ directly.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the geometric image location from the physical mirror material.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the branch or orientation change before you apply it. Then compare the sign of d_i and m with the ray diagram.

items.prompt: Starting from the plane-mirror preset, what happens if you switch to a concave mirror with the same object distance and a focal-length magnitude of 0.8 m?

choices.label: The image becomes real, inverted, and forms in front of the mirror.
The image stays virtual, upright, and exactly the same size.
The image disappears because the reflected rays now curve.

items.explanation: At this object distance, the concave mirror is outside the focal length, so the reflected rays meet in front of the mirror and form a real inverted image.
items.observationHint: Watch the image-map point jump from the plane-mirror line to the real branch.

items.prompt: What changes if the concave-mirror object crosses from outside $F$ to inside $F$?

choices.label: The image becomes virtual, upright, and enlarged behind the mirror.
The image stays real, inverted, and simply moves closer to the mirror.
The mirror stops producing any image point.

items.explanation: Inside F, the reflected rays no longer meet in front of the mirror. Their backward extensions meet behind the mirror instead, so the image becomes virtual and upright.
items.observationHint: The dashed backward extensions replace the real ray intersection, and both d_i and m flip sign.

items.prompt: If you switch from the concave real-image reference to a convex mirror with the same focal-length magnitude, what should happen?

choices.label: The image becomes virtual, upright, and reduced behind the mirror.
The image stays real because curved mirrors always focus reflected rays.
The image must stay the same size because only the sign of $f$ changed.

items.explanation: A convex mirror always sends the image to the virtual side for a real object in front of it and keeps the image upright with magnification below one.
items.observationHint: Compare the virtual branch and the positive magnification that stays below one.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the real mirror controls and signed graphs to hit image targets. The checks read the same d_i and m that the stage is already showing.

items.title: Real-image target
items.prompt: Starting from the concave real-image preset, tune the setup until the image distance lands between 1.0 and 1.2 m and the magnification lands between -1.4 and -1.1.
items.successMessage: You matched a real inverted image by steering the object onto the correct branch instead of guessing by eye.

setup.note: Use the image map and the reflected-ray intersection together.

hints: Keep the mirror concave and the object outside the focal point.
The magnification should stay negative because the image is still inverted.

checks.label: Open the object-image map.
Keep the principal rays visible.
Make $d_i$ land between 1.0 and 1.2 m.
Keep magnification between -1.4 and -1.1.

items.title: Virtual-image target
items.prompt: Starting from the inside-focus preset, make a virtual upright image with $d_i$ between -0.90 and -0.75 m and magnification between 2.2 and 2.6.
items.successMessage: You used the signed graph honestly: the image stayed virtual and upright while the enlarged magnification set the final size.

setup.note: Use the magnification graph and the distance guide together.

hints: The object must stay inside the focal point for the image to remain virtual.
A positive magnification above one means the image is upright and enlarged.

checks.label: Open the magnification graph.
Keep the distance and height guide visible.
Make $d_i$ land between -0.90 and -0.75 m.
Keep magnification between 2.2 and 2.6.

items.title: Wave Interference
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

paragraphs: Keep the Path-difference guide and Result envelope visible, and fix the probe at one screen height. The probe-motion graph shows Source A, Source B, and their resultant at that one point, while the pattern graph shows the long-run brightness at every screen height. A bright band is not one frozen crest in time. It is a position where the combined wave stays large cycle after cycle.

The key quantity is the total phase difference, and the Path-difference guide shows where part of it comes from. When you move the probe, r1 and r2 change, so the extra distance Delta r changes too. If Delta r is 0, one wavelength, two wavelengths, and so on, equal sources reinforce. If Delta r is about half a wavelength, one and a half wavelengths, and so on, equal sources arrive nearly opposite in phase and the screen becomes dark.

The source controls let you test which part of that phase difference changed. Source phase offset can turn the center from bright to dark without changing the geometry at all, because it adds phase directly. Changing Wavelength also leaves the geometry alone, but it changes how much phase each meter of path difference represents, so the fringes squeeze together or spread apart on the pattern graph.

One last check matters before you trust a dark band: destructive phase does not guarantee a perfect zero unless the source amplitudes are matched. If Source A and Source B are unequal, the Result envelope does not fully close. The same idea later reappears in standing waves, noise cancellation, and optical interference. Next, make a dark band by moving Probe height, return to the center and darken it again with Source phase offset alone, then unbalance the amplitudes and watch the null fill back in.

controls.description: Controls how strongly source A contributes to the combined motion.
Controls how strongly source B contributes to the combined motion.
Shorter wavelengths make the same path difference produce a larger phase shift.
Adds a direct phase shift between the two sources without changing the geometry.
Moves the screen point up or down so the path difference changes.

presets.label: Center bright
presets.description: Equal paths and equal phases reinforce at the middle of the screen.

presets.label: Offset dark
presets.description: The probe sits where the path difference is close to half a wavelength.

presets.label: Phase flip
presets.description: A source phase shift of π turns the center from bright to dark.

presets.label: Unequal sources
presets.description: The phase split is destructive, but unequal amplitudes leave a leftover signal.

presets.label: Short wavelength
presets.description: A shorter wavelength packs bright and dark regions closer together on the same screen.

overlays.label: Path-difference guide
overlays.shortDescription: Labels the two travel distances and the extra distance between them.
whatToNotice: Moving the probe changes both path lengths, but interference depends on their difference, not on the two lengths separately.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows how screen position turns into phase difference instead of making bright and dark bands look mysterious.

overlays.label: Result envelope
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the maximum size of the combined motion at the probe.
whatToNotice: The instantaneous displacement can cross zero even at a bright point, but the envelope still shows whether that point is mostly bright or dark over time.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the fast oscillation from the longer-run interference pattern.

overlays.label: Phase wheel
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the two source contributions as arrows that add to the final result.
whatToNotice: As the phase difference changes, the two arrows swing apart or together, and the final arrow shrinks or grows with that angle.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the amplitude formula into a picture of vector addition instead of algebra alone.

graphs.label: Probe motion
graphs.description: Shows Source A, Source B, and their sum at one chosen screen point, so you can see how bright or dark regions still come from time-varying waves.
Shows the average brightness at every screen height, so you can compare one chosen probe point with the full interference pattern.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time. Each prompt should point to something you can actually see change on the stage or graphs.

items.text: Notice that the probe point and the screen band tell the same story: a larger envelope at the probe means a brighter region on the screen.
items.tryThis: Keep the amplitudes equal, move the probe slowly, and watch the screen strip brighten and dim as the resultant envelope changes.
items.whyItMatters: The pattern is the long-run result of the same superposition you are watching at one point.

items.text: Notice that hovering the pattern graph moves the live probe to that same screen height. The graph and stage are linked to the same point.
items.tryThis: Hover a bright peak, then hover a dark valley, and compare the envelope at the probe.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps the graph, geometry, and probe motion synchronized.

items.text: Notice that shortening the wavelength makes the same path difference count for more phase, so the bright and dark bands pack closer together.
items.tryThis: Lower the wavelength while keeping the probe height fixed and compare how far the pattern graph shifts.
items.whyItMatters: Wavelength sets fringe spacing because it converts path difference into phase difference.

items.text: Notice that a destructive phase split does not force the resultant to zero when the source amplitudes are unequal.
items.tryThis: Set the center point dark with a phase flip, then lower Source B amplitude and watch the null fill back in.
items.whyItMatters: A perfect null needs both the right phase difference and equal amplitudes.

items.text: Notice that the time rail changes the instantaneous probe motion, but the pattern graph stays the same because intensity is an average over time, not a time trace.
items.tryThis: Pause on the probe-motion graph, step forward once, and compare what changed at the probe with what stayed fixed in the pattern view.
items.whyItMatters: This separates the instantaneous displacement from the time-averaged interference pattern.

items.text: Notice which setup keeps the bright peaks farther apart on the screen. That setup has the larger wavelength or the smaller phase change per meter of path difference.
items.tryThis: Clone the current state, shorten the wavelength in one setup, and compare the pattern strips row by row before reading the graph.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode makes fringe-spacing changes easy to see without hiding the geometry underneath.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the outcome before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time so you can connect geometry, phase, and brightness.

items.prompt: The probe is centered and the two source amplitudes are equal. If you add a source phase offset of π, what should happen at that same screen point?

choices.label: The center point should collapse toward a dark region because the arrivals are now out of phase.
The center point should stay bright because the path lengths are still equal.
The center point should move upward on the screen but keep the same intensity.

items.explanation: At the center, the path difference is zero, so geometry does not create a phase split. But a source phase offset of pi still makes the two arrivals oppose each other at that same point.
items.observationHint: Watch the center band dim even though the path lengths stay equal.

items.prompt: Keep the probe where it is and shorten the wavelength. What should happen to the bright and dark spacing on the screen?

choices.label: The pattern should tighten because the same path difference now produces more phase change.
The pattern should spread out because shorter waves need more room.
Only the probe-motion graph should change. The screen pattern should stay fixed.

items.explanation: Shorter wavelength means the same extra distance produces a larger phase change, so the fringes crowd closer together.
items.observationHint: The bright and dark peaks on the pattern graph pack inward when λ shrinks.

items.prompt: You already have a destructive phase split. If Source B becomes smaller than Source A, what should happen to the dark point?

choices.label: The dark point should partially fill in because the stronger source leaves a leftover resultant.
The point should stay perfectly dark because phase difference matters more than amplitude.
The whole pattern should disappear because one source is weaker.

items.explanation: Destructive phase alone does not create a full null. If one source is stronger, part of its contribution is left over instead of being completely canceled.
items.observationHint: The phase split is still destructive, but the envelope opens back up because the amplitudes are unmatched.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Turn the screen pattern and probe motion into small interference hunts that update from the same live geometry.

items.title: Find a dark band
items.prompt: Starting from Center bright, move the probe onto a dark region where the screen intensity almost vanishes.
items.successMessage: You found destructive interference by changing the real path difference, not by toggling a cosmetic effect. The pattern graph and the stage now agree on the dark band.

setup.note: Use the screen-pattern graph as the guide while you move the probe off the center line.

hints: You need a place where the path difference is close to half a wavelength.
Keep the path-difference overlay on so the geometry stays visible while you search.

targets.label: Drive the relative intensity below $0.1$.

items.title: Bright zero crossing
items.prompt: From Center bright, pause at a moment when the resultant amplitude is still large but the instantaneous probe displacement has crossed through zero.
items.successMessage: You separated amplitude from instantaneous displacement. Even at a bright point, the probe can pass through zero while the interference remains strongly constructive.

setup.note: Keep the probe at a bright location, then use inspect time to catch a zero crossing.

hints: This is a time challenge, not a pattern challenge. Pause the probe motion instead of moving the geometry first.
You want a large resultant amplitude and a near-zero instantaneous displacement at the same point.

targets.label: Keep resultant amplitude between $1.8$ and $2.05$.
Bring the instantaneous resultant displacement close to zero.

items.title: Standing Waves
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

presets.label: Fundamental
presets.description: One loop, two end nodes, and one middle antinode.

presets.label: Third harmonic
presets.description: Three loops and two extra interior nodes fit on the same string.

presets.label: Fourth harmonic
presets.description: Doubling the second harmonic adds more nodes and shortens the allowed wavelength.

presets.label: Probe on a node
presets.description: The probe sits on an interior node while nearby segments still move.

presets.label: Shorter string
presets.description: The same harmonic on a shorter string needs a shorter wavelength and a higher frequency.

overlays.label: Node guides
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the fixed points that never move in the current mode.
whatToNotice: A node stays on the center line at every instant, even while nearby parts of the string swing above and below it.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes persistent destructive interference visible instead of treating nodes as decorative markers.

overlays.label: Antinode guides
overlays.shortDescription: Shows where the string can reach its largest local oscillation.
whatToNotice: An antinode is the center of a loop where the local envelope is largest, not just a high point in one frozen frame.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates maximum possible displacement from the instantaneous shape at one moment.

overlays.label: Counter-traveling waves
overlays.shortDescription: Adds the two equal traveling waves that superpose to create the standing pattern.
whatToNotice: The two faint component waves keep moving through each other while the node pattern stays fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the interference origin of a standing wave visible instead of treating the pattern as a special separate object.

graphs.label: Mode shape
graphs.description: Shows the spatial factor of the current harmonic. Zero crossings mark nodes, and peaks mark antinodes.

graphs.label: Probe motion
graphs.description: Shows how one chosen point moves in time and how large its local envelope is in the current mode.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time. Each prompt should point to something you can actually see on the string or graph.

items.text: Notice that the node markers never leave the center line, even while the neighboring loops cross it every cycle.
items.tryThis: Leave the node overlay on and watch one full cycle before moving the probe.
items.whyItMatters: A standing wave is not motionless everywhere. What stays fixed are the nodes and the mode pattern.

items.text: Notice that hovering the mode-shape graph moves the same live probe to that position on the string.
items.tryThis: Hover near a node, then hover near an antinode, and compare how the probe trace changes.
items.whyItMatters: The graph and stage are two views of the same standing-wave pattern.

items.text: Notice that raising the mode number adds another half-wavelength segment to the same string, so nodes move closer together and the oscillation gets faster.
items.tryThis: Step from the second harmonic to the third or fourth and compare both the node spacing and the speed of the probe trace.
items.whyItMatters: Changing mode number changes both the spatial pattern and the time behavior.

items.text: Notice that the standing pattern comes from two equal waves passing through each other. The string has not stopped carrying waves.
items.tryThis: Turn on the counter-traveling waves and watch them pass through each other while the standing-wave nodes stay fixed.
items.whyItMatters: This connects standing waves back to interference between traveling waves.

items.text: Notice that the time rail changes the instantaneous string shape and probe displacement, but the mode-shape graph stays the same because it represents the spatial pattern, not one frozen frame.
items.tryThis: Pause, step once, and compare what changes on the string with what stays fixed on the mode-shape graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the standing-wave pattern from the local oscillation within that pattern.

items.text: Notice which setup packs more loops into the same length. That setup is either a higher harmonic or a shorter string.
items.tryThis: Clone the current state, then raise the mode number in one setup and compare the node spacing row by row before reading the graph.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode makes harmonic spacing changes visible without hiding the fixed-end geometry.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the consequence before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time so you can connect the string, graph, and equations.

items.prompt: The string length stays fixed, but the mode number jumps from 2 to 4. What must happen to the standing pattern?

choices.label: The string should show more nodes and a shorter allowed wavelength.
The string should keep the same node spacing because the length did not change.
Only the probe graph should change. The stage pattern should stay the same.

items.explanation: On a fixed string, a higher harmonic fits more half-wavelength segments into the same length. That adds nodes, shortens the allowed wavelength, and raises the oscillation frequency.
items.observationHint: Watch the node spacing shrink and the probe trace cycle faster after the change.

items.prompt: If you move the probe onto a node while keeping the same harmonic, what should happen to the probe-motion graph?

choices.label: The probe trace should collapse near zero because that point does not oscillate.
The probe trace should double because nodes are where two waves meet.
The probe trace should stay unchanged because the harmonic did not change.

items.explanation: A node is a permanent cancellation point. Moving the probe there makes the spatial factor zero, so that point stops oscillating even though the rest of the string keeps moving.
items.observationHint: The string still shows neighboring loops, but the probe line flattens when the point lands on a node.

items.prompt: The same second harmonic is kept, but the string is shortened. What must happen to the allowed wavelength and frequency?

choices.label: The allowed wavelength gets shorter and the frequency rises.
The allowed wavelength stays the same, but the amplitude rises.
The frequency drops because the string has less room to move.

items.explanation: For the same mode number, shortening $L$ shortens the allowed wavelength through $\lambda_n = 2L/n$. With the wave speed fixed, that shorter wavelength means a higher frequency.
items.observationHint: The ruler distance shrinks, the nodes move closer, and the probe trace cycles faster.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the mode-shape graph, overlays, and inspect-time rail to solve short standing-wave tasks on the same live string.

items.title: Probe on a node
items.prompt: Starting from the third harmonic, move the probe onto a node so its local envelope becomes almost zero.
items.successMessage: You placed the probe on a true node. The rest of the string still shows a standing-wave pattern, but that point stays still.

setup.note: Use the node overlay and the mode-shape graph together as you move the probe.

hints: In the third harmonic, the interior nodes divide the string into three equal loop sections.
A tiny probe envelope matters more than one instant of small displacement. A true node stays quiet at all times.

checks.label: Open the mode-shape graph.
Keep the node overlay visible.
Keep the mode number near the third harmonic.
Make the probe envelope smaller than $0.08\,\mathrm{m}$.

items.title: Center zero crossing
items.prompt: From the fundamental mode, keep the probe at the center antinode and pause when that antinode crosses through zero displacement.
items.successMessage: You separated instantaneous displacement from envelope. An antinode can cross zero even though it is the point of largest oscillation.

setup.note: Keep the probe at the center antinode, then pause when the probe-motion trace crosses the axis.

hints: The center of the fundamental is an antinode, so its envelope should stay large even when the instantaneous displacement is zero.
This is an inspect-time task. Watch the probe-motion graph and pause at a zero crossing.

checks.label: Open the probe-motion graph.
Keep the antinode overlay visible.
Pause into inspect mode.
Keep the probe envelope between $0.95$ and $1.1\,\mathrm{m}$.
Bring instantaneous probe displacement close to zero.

items.title: Collisions
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Increase or decrease A's mass and watch how the same collision changes A's velocity jump.
Change B's mass and compare how its post-collision speed changes on the velocity and momentum views.
Set how fast cart A approaches the contact point from the left.
Set how fast cart B approaches from the right. Zero means B starts at rest.
Lower values make the carts separate less after contact. Zero means perfectly inelastic; one means perfectly elastic.

presets.label: Elastic equal masses
presets.description: A classic elastic case where motion transfers from A to B.

presets.label: Sticky collision
presets.description: A perfectly inelastic case where the carts move together after contact.

presets.label: Light into heavy
presets.description: A light cart A hits a heavy cart B and is more likely to rebound.

presets.label: Heavy into light
presets.description: A heavy cart A keeps moving forward while a lighter cart B speeds away.

presets.label: Moving target
presets.description: Both carts are already moving, so you can test momentum conservation without a stationary target.

overlays.label: Collision zone
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the position and instant of contact so you can line up the stage with the graph jumps.
whatToNotice: The jump in velocity and momentum happens at one instant, exactly when the carts meet.
Use this marker to align the stage with the step or jump you see on the graphs.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you connect the physical contact on the track to the sudden changes on the velocity, momentum, and energy graphs.

overlays.label: Center of mass
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the system's center of mass as the carts approach, collide, and separate.
whatToNotice: The center of mass keeps one steady drift through the collision even when the carts' individual velocities jump.
When the total momentum is close to zero, the center of mass stays nearly fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: A steady center-of-mass motion is a compact visual check that the system total momentum has not changed.

overlays.label: Relative speed
overlays.shortDescription: Compares the closing speed before contact with the separation speed after contact.
whatToNotice: Changing elasticity does not alter the closing speed before impact in the same setup, but it does change the separation speed after impact.
At elasticity zero, the separation speed becomes zero because the carts leave together.
overlays.whyItMatters: This makes the restitution equation visible as a before-and-after speed comparison instead of just a symbol.

overlays.label: Momentum bars
overlays.shortDescription: Shows cart A, cart B, and total momentum on one shared scale.
whatToNotice: The two cart bars can change sharply at contact while the total bar stays fixed.
Changing mass changes how the system momentum is split between the carts, even when the total is unchanged.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the system total visible while you compare the two individual outcomes.

graphs.label: Velocities and center-of-mass speed
graphs.description: Shows each cart's velocity changing at contact and the steady center-of-mass speed that reflects the system total momentum.

graphs.label: Object and total momentum
graphs.description: Shows momentum being exchanged between the carts while the system total stays constant.

graphs.label: Kinetic energy through the collision
graphs.description: Shows whether total kinetic energy is preserved or reduced at the collision instant.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the collision
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts while you change one control at a time. Each one should help you connect what the carts do on the track to what the graphs and overlays show.

items.text: Notice that the total-momentum line stays flat while the individual cart momentum lines jump at contact.
items.tryThis: Pause just before and just after contact, or scrub across it. The cart lines jump, but the total line should stay level.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest graph sign that momentum conservation applies to the whole system, not to each cart separately.

items.text: Notice that lowering elasticity can make the energy graph step downward at contact even while the total-momentum line stays flat.
items.tryThis: Lower elasticity, then compare the energy and momentum graphs at the same contact instant.
items.whyItMatters: It separates two ideas students often merge: momentum can be conserved even when kinetic energy is not.

items.text: Notice that a heavier cart changes speed less, even though the same collision still obeys the momentum rule.
items.tryThis: Make cart B much heavier, then compare the size of B's velocity jump with A's.
items.whyItMatters: Momentum change and velocity change are not the same thing. Mass controls how much a given momentum transfer changes speed.

items.text: Notice that elasticity changes the separation speed after contact, not the closing speed before contact.
items.tryThis: Turn on the relative-speed overlay, then lower elasticity from 1 toward 0 and watch only the after-collision value change.
items.whyItMatters: Elasticity is a ratio of before-and-after relative speeds, so the overlay helps you read what the equation means.

items.text: Notice that the center-of-mass marker keeps one smooth drift even though the individual cart velocities jump at contact.
items.tryThis: Scrub through the collision with the center-of-mass overlay on. The marker should keep the same smooth motion.
items.whyItMatters: A smooth center-of-mass motion is another sign that the system total momentum stays constant.

items.text: Notice that two setups can have the same total momentum but very different total kinetic energy after contact.
items.tryThis: Compare the elastic equal-mass case with the sticky collision. The momentum totals match, but the energy graphs do not.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode helps separate what momentum conservation guarantees from what elasticity changes.

predictionMode.title: Predict before you test
predictionMode.intro: Make the prediction first, then test it on the stage and graphs. Use momentum conservation together with mass and elasticity rather than guessing from memory.

items.prompt: Equal-mass carts collide elastically and cart B starts at rest. What should happen immediately after contact?

choices.label: Cart A stops, and cart B leaves with A's original speed.
Both carts move right together with the same speed.
The total momentum becomes zero because one cart started at rest.

items.explanation: With equal masses in an elastic collision, the moving cart transfers its speed to the cart that was at rest. The total momentum stays the same throughout.
items.observationHint: Watch the velocity graph right after contact: A drops to zero while B rises to A's original speed.

items.prompt: If you keep the same equal-mass setup but set elasticity to zero, what should happen after contact?

choices.label: They leave together with one shared velocity while the total kinetic energy drops.
They rebound faster because more energy is stored during contact.
The total momentum disappears because the carts stick.

items.explanation: In a perfectly inelastic collision, the carts share one final velocity. Momentum is still conserved, but total kinetic energy decreases.
items.observationHint: Watch the carts leave together and compare the flat momentum total with the step down in total energy.

items.prompt: A light cart A hits a much heavier cart B in a fairly elastic collision. What outcome is most likely?

choices.label: Cart A rebounds left while cart B gains only a modest rightward speed.
Cart B must rebound left because it has more mass.
Both carts keep exactly the same speeds because momentum is conserved.

items.explanation: A heavier cart needs more momentum change to get the same speed change, so B usually speeds up only a little while the lighter cart can rebound.
items.observationHint: Compare the size and direction of the two post-collision velocity changes.

items.title: Double-Slit Interference
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Longer wavelength widens the fringe spacing for the same slit geometry.
Increasing slit separation tightens the fringes by increasing the path-difference gradient.
Moving the screen farther turns the same interference angles into larger fringe spacing.
Moves the sampled screen point without changing the underlying fringe pattern.

presets.label: Center bright
presets.description: The center line has equal path lengths, so it is a bright fringe.

presets.label: First dark
presets.description: The probe sits close to the first dark fringe where the path difference is about half a wavelength.

presets.label: Wide fringes
presets.description: Longer wavelength, smaller slit separation, and a farther screen spread the bright bands apart.

presets.label: Tight fringes
presets.description: Shorter wavelength, larger slit separation, and a nearer screen pack the bands together.

presets.label: Far screen
presets.description: Moving the screen farther widens the same interference pattern in vertical spacing.

overlays.label: Geometry guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks slit separation and screen distance directly on the stage.
whatToNotice: The same d and L drawn on the stage are the quantities that appear in the fringe-spacing rule.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the visible setup to the fringe-spacing equation.

overlays.label: Path-difference guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the two slit-to-probe paths and their current difference.
whatToNotice: Both slit-to-probe distances change as you move the probe, but the interference condition depends on their difference.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the geometry-to-phase step visible on the same bench.

overlays.label: Fringe-spacing guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the approximate bright-to-bright spacing and the first dark position for the current geometry.
whatToNotice: Changing wavelength, screen distance, or slit separation moves these markers together because all three live in the same spacing rule.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns fringe spacing from a formula into a screen measurement.

overlays.label: Phase wheel
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the relative phase angle between the two slit contributions at the current probe.
whatToNotice: When the phase difference is near 0, the resultant is large. When it is near \(\pi\), the resultant shrinks toward zero.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the path-difference story visible as a phase comparison.

graphs.label: Probe field
graphs.description: Shows the two slit contributions and their sum at one chosen screen point, so you can inspect the local oscillation without losing the interference story.
Shows intensity across the full screen, so you can read fringe spacing and bright or dark positions directly.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time. Keep the slit geometry, the current probe, and the screen-pattern graph in view together.

items.text: Notice that the center is bright because the two slit-to-screen paths are equal there, so the phase difference is near zero.
items.tryThis: Start from Center bright, then move the probe upward until the strip first turns dark.
items.whyItMatters: It gives you a clean reference point for the rest of the pattern.

items.text: Notice that increasing slit separation tightens the fringes because the same vertical move on the screen now creates path difference more quickly.
items.tryThis: Compare Tight fringes with Center bright and watch how many bright bands fit into the same screen height.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps d tied to path-difference geometry instead of treating it as a cosmetic spacing slider.

items.text: Notice that increasing wavelength widens the fringes because the same path difference represents fewer wavelengths of phase change.
items.tryThis: Raise lambda while holding d and L fixed, then compare how far apart the bright peaks sit on the pattern graph.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps wavelength connected to phase per meter rather than only to color language.

items.text: Notice that moving the screen farther does not change the interference angles, but it spreads those same angles over more screen height.
items.tryThis: Compare Center bright with Far screen before changing any other control.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the angular interference condition from the spacing measured in meters on the screen.

items.text: Notice that the time rail changes the instantaneous probe field, but the pattern graph stays position-based because the fringe pattern is an intensity map across space.
items.tryThis: Pause on the probe-field graph, step once, and compare what changed at the probe with what stayed fixed on the pattern graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates local oscillation from the spatial fringe pattern honestly.

items.text: Compare mode works best when one setup changes slit separation and the other changes wavelength or screen distance. Then you can see different ways to widen or tighten the same pattern.
items.tryThis: Clone the live setup, tighten one row with larger d, and widen the other row with larger lambda before reading the graph.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps fringe spacing tied to one shared geometry rule instead of one vague spreading effect.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict how the fringe spacing or local brightness should change before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time.

items.prompt: Keep wavelength and screen distance fixed, but increase the slit separation. What should happen to the bright-fringe spacing?

choices.label: The fringes should tighten because the same screen move now produces more path difference.
The fringes should widen because the slits are farther apart.
Only the probe-field graph should change. The pattern spacing should stay fixed.

items.explanation: Because \(\Delta y \approx \lambda L / d\), increasing d makes neighboring bright fringes closer together.
items.observationHint: The bright peaks pack closer together when the slits are farther apart.

items.prompt: Leave wavelength and slit separation alone, but move the screen farther away. What should happen to the fringe spacing in meters on the screen?

choices.label: The fringes should widen because the same interference angles now span more vertical distance.
The fringes should tighten because the paths get longer.
The fringes should stay fixed because only wavelength matters.

items.explanation: Increasing L spreads the same interference angles over a larger height range, so the fringe spacing in meters grows.
items.observationHint: The bright and dark bands spread apart vertically when the screen moves farther from the slits.

items.prompt: Starting from the center bright fringe, move the probe until the path difference is about half a wavelength. What should happen at that point?

choices.label: The point should become dark because the two slit contributions arrive nearly opposite in phase.
The point should stay bright because both slits still reach the screen.
The point should brighten more because the path difference is larger.

items.explanation: A path difference near half a wavelength gives a phase split near \(\pi\), so the two equal slit contributions cancel and the point goes dark.
items.observationHint: Watch the strip and pattern graph dim at the same probe height where the path-difference label reaches about 0.5 lambda.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the live geometry to hunt for fringes. The tasks read the same wavelength, slit separation, screen distance, and probe position shown on the bench.

items.title: Find the first dark fringe
items.prompt: Starting from Center bright, move the probe onto the first dark fringe without changing wavelength, slit separation, or screen distance.
items.successMessage: You found the first dark fringe by changing the real screen point, not by inventing a separate preview state. The geometry label, the probe field, and the pattern now agree on destructive interference.

setup.note: Use the pattern graph as the guide while you move only the probe.

hints: You are looking for a path difference near half a wavelength.
The first dark fringe should be closer to the center than the first bright spacing marker.

targets.label: Bring the path difference close to half a wavelength.
Drive the relative intensity below 0.08.

items.title: Set wide fringes
items.prompt: Starting from Tight fringes, tune the geometry until the bright-fringe spacing lands between 1.8 m and 2.1 m.
items.successMessage: You widened the fringes by changing the shared geometry rule \(\lambda L / d\), not by moving the probe. The stage labels and the spacing guide now support the same target.

setup.note: Use the spacing guide and the graph together while you tune wavelength, slit separation, and screen distance.

hints: You need a larger value of lambda times L over d than the Tight fringes preset gives.
Increasing wavelength or screen distance helps. Decreasing slit separation helps too.

targets.label: Set the bright-fringe spacing between 1.8 m and 2.1 m.

items.title: Specific Heat and Phase Change
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Changes how much sample there is.

controls.label: Specific heat c
controls.description: Changes how much energy each kilogram needs for each degree of temperature change.

controls.label: Heating or cooling power
controls.description: Positive values heat the sample and negative values cool it.
Moves the starting point below the shelf, onto the shelf, or above it.

controls.label: Latent heat L
controls.description: Changes how much energy the shelf absorbs or releases before temperature starts changing again.

presets.label: Low-c warm sample
