presets.description: Above the shelf with a low specific heat, so the same power produces a fast temperature rise.

presets.label: High-c warm sample
presets.description: Same mass and power as the low-c sample, but the larger specific heat makes temperature rise more slowly.

presets.label: Warming toward the shelf
presets.description: Starts below the phase-change temperature, warms up to it, and then begins moving across the shelf.

presets.label: On the shelf
presets.description: Starts on the flat shelf, so phase fraction changes while temperature stays near 0 degC.

presets.label: Cooling on the shelf
presets.description: Starts on the shelf with cooling power, so phase fraction falls before temperature drops below 0 degC.

overlays.label: Thermal-capacity cue
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the live m c value and temperature rate so you can connect specific heat to the slope of the curve.
whatToNotice: A larger m c makes the sloped parts of the heating curve less steep under the same power.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties specific heat to a visible rate of temperature change.

overlays.label: Shelf cue
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current phase fraction and the full m L shelf width on the same bench.
whatToNotice: The shelf bar can keep moving while the temperature marker stays near the phase-change temperature.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes latent heat visible instead of leaving the flat part of the curve unexplained.

overlays.label: Energy split
overlays.shortDescription: Shows how the current transferred energy is split between sensible and latent parts.
whatToNotice: Total transferred energy can keep increasing even when most of the change is going into only the latent bar.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the sloped parts and the flat shelf to one shared energy bookkeeping.

overlays.label: Curve guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the phase-change temperature line so the stage and the heating curve use the same shelf reference.
whatToNotice: Reaching the phase-change line does not always mean temperature changes right away. It can mean the run has entered the shelf.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the stage and graph aligned so the flat part of the heating curve has a clear physical meaning.

graphs.label: Heating curve
graphs.description: Compare the live temperature with the phase-change temperature. Sloped parts mean sensible heating or cooling, and the flat shelf means latent-energy change.
Compare total transferred energy with the sensible and latent parts. Use this graph to check whether a flat temperature really means the shelf is active.

graphs.label: Temperature change vs specific heat
graphs.description: Change only the specific heat while keeping the current mass and power. The same 4 minute pulse gives a smaller temperature change when c is larger, unless the run is on the shelf.

graphs.label: Shelf width vs latent heat
graphs.description: Change only the latent heat. A larger latent heat makes the shelf wider in energy, so the sample can spend longer near constant temperature under the same power.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Each one links one control change to the heating curve and the live energy split.

items.text: Changing only specific heat changes the steepness of the sloped parts, but it does not change the energy width of the shelf. That shows that c controls temperature response, not latent shelf energy.
items.tryThis: Switch between Low-c warm sample and High-c warm sample, then compare the heating curve and the specific-heat-response graph.
items.whyItMatters: This connects Q = m c delta T to a visible temperature response.

items.text: Mass changes both the m c chip and the m L shelf-width chip. A larger sample warms more slowly and also needs more energy to finish the phase change.
items.tryThis: Raise only the mass and watch both the m c chip and the m L chip increase together.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that mass affects both the sloped parts and the shelf.

items.text: A flat shelf does not mean the heater stopped. Check the energy-bookkeeping graph and the shelf bar: latent energy can still be changing while temperature stays near the phase-change temperature.
items.tryThis: Use On the shelf, scrub through the flat region, and compare the temperature line with the latent-energy line.
items.whyItMatters: This is the key habit for reading a heating curve honestly.

items.text: On the latent-response graph, increasing L makes the shelf wider in energy. Under the same power, the sample can spend longer near constant temperature.
items.tryThis: Hover a small-L and large-L point, then compare the preview badge and the shelf-width chip on the stage.
items.whyItMatters: It separates a wide shelf from a large specific heat.
It isolates the specific-heat idea before the shelf adds another layer of reasoning.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict where the next bit of energy goes before you change the controls. Does it change temperature, phase fraction, or both?

items.prompt: Starting from Low-c warm sample, you increase only the specific heat while keeping the same mass and power. Which statement is strongest after the same 4 minute pulse?

choices.label: The temperature change becomes smaller because the same energy is spread across a larger thermal capacity.
The temperature change becomes larger because a larger specific heat means the sample stores energy more easily.
Nothing changes because the power stayed the same.

items.explanation: At fixed mass and power, a larger specific heat increases m c, so the same 4 minute pulse produces a smaller temperature change on the sloped part of the curve.
items.observationHint: For the same pulse, the higher-c setup climbs less on the sloped part of the heating curve.

items.prompt: The sample is already on the shelf and the heater stays on. What should happen next before the shelf is finished?

choices.label: The phase fraction keeps increasing while temperature stays near the phase-change temperature.
Temperature must rise immediately because any added energy always changes temperature first.
Nothing changes because a flat temperature means no energy is entering.

items.explanation: On the shelf, transferred energy changes phase fraction. Temperature can stay nearly flat even while the latent-energy term keeps changing.
items.observationHint: Watch the shelf bar and the latent-energy line first.

items.prompt: Starting from Cooling on the shelf, you keep removing energy. What changes first before temperature falls below the shelf?

choices.label: The phase fraction decreases first while temperature stays near the shelf.
Temperature must drop immediately because cooling always changes temperature before phase.
The phase fraction increases because cooling makes the shelf wider.

items.explanation: Cooling reverses the same shelf logic: energy leaves the latent store first, so phase fraction falls before the sample drops below the phase-change temperature.
items.observationHint: The shelf fraction shrinks while the temperature marker stays near 0 degC.

items.title: Drag and Terminal Velocity
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

keyIdeas: In this model, drag depends on speed, so it starts small just after release and grows rapidly as the object speeds up.
Weight stays constant while drag changes, which is why the net force is largest early in the fall and much smaller later.
Terminal velocity means drag and weight are balancing, so the net force is near zero.
At terminal velocity the object is still moving downward. What becomes small is the acceleration, not the speed.
A larger mass raises terminal speed, while a larger area or stronger drag lowers it.

controls.description: Changes the object's mass and therefore the constant weight that drag must eventually balance.
Changes how much drag the object gets at the same speed.
Sets how strongly this bounded model resists motion through the fluid.

presets.label: Heavy sphere
presets.description: The same area and drag strength, but much more mass.

presets.label: Wide canopy
presets.description: A larger area makes drag catch up sooner, so terminal speed is lower.

presets.label: Thick fluid
presets.description: Stronger drag lowers the eventual fall speed without changing the mass.

presets.label: Matched ratio
presets.description: Doubling both mass and area keeps the same terminal-speed ratio when drag strength stays fixed.

presets.label: Draggy disk
presets.description: Large area and strong drag produce a low terminal speed quickly.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the constant weight arrow, the upward drag arrow, and the shrinking net-force arrow.
whatToNotice: At first the weight arrow is much larger than the drag arrow, but the drag arrow grows with speed until the two nearly match.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes terminal velocity a force-balance idea instead of a memorized speed limit.

overlays.label: Terminal-speed cue
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current terminal-speed label and how close the drag force is to the weight.
whatToNotice: Near terminal speed the object is still moving downward. What becomes small is the net force and acceleration.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates constant speed from zero motion.

overlays.label: Distance guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks how far the object has fallen through the fluid column.
whatToNotice: After the speed curve flattens, the object still keeps covering more distance.
overlays.whyItMatters: It prevents the mistake of confusing constant speed with stopping.

graphs.label: Speed vs time
graphs.description: The speed rises quickly at first and then bends toward the flat terminal-speed line as drag catches up to weight.

graphs.label: Force balance vs time
graphs.description: Weight stays flat, drag rises with speed, and the net downward force falls toward zero.

graphs.label: Terminal speed vs mass
graphs.description: Heavier objects need a higher speed before drag can balance the larger weight.

graphs.label: Terminal speed vs area
graphs.description: More area means more drag at the same speed, so terminal speed falls.

graphs.label: Terminal speed vs drag strength
graphs.description: A larger drag-strength constant lowers the speed needed for force balance.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep one force question in view at a time so the stage, time rail, and graphs all tell the same fall story.

items.text: Right after release, drag is still small because the speed is still small. The weight arrow is already there, so the net downward force starts large.
Near terminal speed, the object is still moving downward even though the net force is tiny. Force balance makes the acceleration nearly vanish; it does not erase the speed that is already there.
items.tryThis: Pause late in the fall and compare the small net-force line with the still-positive speed line.
items.whyItMatters: This is the key difference between terminal velocity and coming to rest.

items.text: On the mass sweep, larger mass raises terminal speed because the weight is larger while the drag law itself is unchanged.
On the area sweep, a larger frontal area lowers terminal speed because the same speed now creates a larger drag force.
Use compare mode to match the same terminal speed with two different setups. Equal terminal speed does not mean mass, area, and drag strength are all the same. It means the ratio $mg/(kA)$ matches.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict which side of the force balance changes before you run the new setup.

choices.label: Drag grows more strongly at the same speed, so the speed curve levels off sooner and terminal speed is lower.
Weight increases, so terminal speed is higher.
Nothing changes because area only affects position, not force.

items.explanation: Area appears directly in the drag law $F_d = kAv^2$, so a larger area makes drag stronger at every speed and lowers terminal speed.
items.observationHint: The drag line catches the weight sooner, and the terminal-speed-area graph drops as area grows.

choices.label: The weight is larger, so drag has to grow to a higher speed before the forces balance.
Drag becomes weaker at the same speed because heavier objects cut through the fluid.
Terminal speed stays the same because mass does not appear in the drag law.

items.explanation: Mass changes the weight side of the balance, so a heavier object reaches a higher terminal speed even though the drag law itself is unchanged.
items.observationHint: The speed-history curve approaches a higher flat terminal-speed value when you switch to the heavier setup.

choices.label: The drag force builds faster at every speed, so the object settles into a lower terminal speed.
The weight force falls because the fluid is thicker.
Only the position ruler changes; the speed and force graphs stay the same.

items.explanation: A larger $k$ makes drag stronger for the same $A$ and $v$, so the balance with weight happens at a lower speed.
items.observationHint: The terminal-speed-drag-strength graph slopes downward, and the force-balance graph reaches near-equality earlier.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same falling-body bench to hit terminal-speed targets and to catch the near-terminal moment from the live force balance.

items.title: Build a slower terminal-speed case
items.successMessage: You lowered the terminal speed by strengthening the drag side of the balance. The object still falls, but it settles into a slower steady-speed regime.

setup.note: Use the terminal-speed graphs and keep the mass close to baseline.

hints: Mass should stay near the same value, so the speed change must come from the drag side of the balance.
A larger area and a larger drag constant both lower terminal speed, so use both controls together.

targets.label: Keep the mass near $2.0\,\mathrm{kg}$.
Raise the area to about $0.10\,\mathrm{m^2}$.
Raise the drag strength to about $18$.
Bring the terminal speed into the $3.2$ to $3.5\,\mathrm{m/s}$ range.

items.title: Catch the near-terminal moment
items.prompt: Starting from Draggy disk, pause when the object is effectively at terminal speed: drag nearly equals weight and the remaining net downward force is tiny.
items.successMessage: You found the near-terminal moment. The object is still moving, but the force imbalance is now so small that the speed is hardly changing.

setup.note: Pause the fall and scrub until the drag and weight are almost matched.

hints: You want the drag line almost touching the weight line while the net-force line is very close to zero.
Late in this low-terminal-speed setup, the speed is still positive even though the force imbalance is tiny.

targets.label: Make the net downward force smaller than about $0.35\,\mathrm{N}$.
Bring the current speed within about $0.10\,\mathrm{m/s}$ of terminal speed.

items.title: Maxwell's Equations Synthesis
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Sets the electric source term for the closed-surface flux reading.
Sets the current contribution to the Ampere-Maxwell loop story.
Changes the size and sign of the changing-electric contribution over the shared cycle.
Changes the size and sign of the changing-magnetic contribution over the shared cycle.
Controls how quickly the shared changing-field clock runs.

presets.label: Balanced synthesis
presets.description: A compact baseline with charge, current, and both changing-field terms active together.

presets.label: Neutral enclosure
presets.description: Remove the electric source term while leaving the circulation story active.

presets.label: Current-dominated loop
presets.description: Let conduction current dominate the magnetic circulation while the changing-electric term stays small.

presets.label: Displacement-driven loop
presets.description: Turn off wire current and keep the changing-electric term strong so Ampere-Maxwell still drives a magnetic loop.

presets.label: Strong light bridge
presets.description: Keep both changing-field terms active together so the synthesis card shows a stronger light-like bridge cue.

presets.label: Misaligned bridge
presets.description: Flip the sign of the changing-magnetic term so the changing-field pair stops reinforcing a clean bridge.

overlays.label: Charge surface
overlays.shortDescription: Highlights the closed surface around enclosed charge so the electric source law stays visible.
whatToNotice: Changing the enclosed charge changes the electric source term directly, even if the circulation cards below do something different.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates electric sources from the loop laws instead of blending everything into one field-line picture.

overlays.label: Magnetic closure
overlays.shortDescription: Keeps the no-monopoles reminder on screen so local magnetic loops are not mistaken for a net source term.
whatToNotice: The local magnetic pattern can grow stronger while the net closed-surface magnetic flux still stays zero.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps Gauss for B conceptually separate from the Ampere-Maxwell loop law.

overlays.label: Faraday loop
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the circulating electric-field response created by changing magnetic flux.
whatToNotice: Flipping the sign of the magnetic-flux change flips the circulating electric response.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns Faraday's law into a loop-reading story rather than a detached minus sign.

overlays.label: Ampere-Maxwell loop
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current term and changing-electric term feeding one shared magnetic loop.
whatToNotice: Zero conduction current does not force zero magnetic circulation if the changing-electric term is still active.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps Maxwell's displacement-current idea visible instead of hiding it inside the formula.

overlays.label: Light bridge
overlays.shortDescription: Shows whether the current changing-field pair supports a clean light-like bridge cue.
whatToNotice: The bridge is strongest when both changing-field terms stay active together and aligned, not when only one law is doing all the work.
overlays.whyItMatters: It is the intuition-first bridge from Maxwell's equations to electromagnetic waves and light.

graphs.label: Flux laws
graphs.description: Compares the two source-law readings: net electric flux can change with enclosed charge, while net magnetic flux through a closed surface stays zero.

graphs.label: Ampere-Maxwell link
graphs.description: Shows the conduction-current term, the changing-electric term, and the resulting magnetic circulation on one time axis so you can see that either source can drive the loop response.

graphs.label: Faraday and light bridge
graphs.description: Shows how changing magnetic flux drives electric circulation and whether the changing-electric and changing-magnetic terms are aligned enough to support a strong light-bridge cue.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. First separate source laws from loop laws, then watch how the changing-field pair can bridge toward waves.

items.text: Start by separating jobs. Enclosed charge changes the electric source-law card directly, but the loop cards can still stay active for completely different reasons.
items.tryThis: Lower enclosed charge toward zero without touching the changing-field terms and watch which parts of the stage do and do not collapse.
items.whyItMatters: Many Maxwell confusions come from mixing source laws with circulation laws.

items.text: Gauss for B is not saying magnetism disappears. It is saying the net magnetic flux through a closed surface stays zero even while local magnetic loops are strong.
items.tryThis: Watch the closed-loop readout while the lower loop cards are active. Local magnetic structure is still present even when the net flux law stays at zero.
items.whyItMatters: This keeps Gauss for B from being misread as magnetism vanishing.

items.text: Ampere-Maxwell is the key upgrade. Even with no conduction current, a changing electric field can still keep the magnetic loop alive.
items.tryThis: Use the displacement-driven-loop preset, then compare the current-term trace with the total magnetic-circulation trace.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the displacement-current term into something you can inspect directly instead of only reciting.

items.text: The light bridge is strongest when both changing-field terms stay active together and with compatible signs. Flip one sign and the bridge cue weakens even though the loop laws themselves still remain.
items.tryThis: Clone the strong-light-bridge setup, then flip only the magnetic-change sign in Setup B and compare the faraday-wave graph.
items.whyItMatters: It is the intuition-first link from Maxwell's equations to electromagnetic waves and light.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Commit to the field-law consequence before moving the slider. Each scenario changes one part of the synthesis so the live stage can confirm or correct the idea.

items.prompt: If enclosed charge is reduced to zero while the current and changing-field terms stay active, what should happen on the synthesis page?

choices.label: The net electric-flux card should go neutral, but the circulation cards can still remain active.
All four cards should collapse because Maxwell's equations need charge to do anything.
The magnetic net-flux card should become nonzero to compensate for the missing charge.

items.explanation: Gauss for E depends on enclosed charge, so the electric source term can go neutral. The circulation laws depend on current and changing fields, and Gauss for B still keeps net magnetic flux at zero.
items.observationHint: Watch the source-law row separate from the loop row instead of expecting one master slider for everything.

items.prompt: If conduction current is set to zero but the changing electric field stays strong, what should happen to the magnetic circulation?

choices.label: The magnetic circulation can stay nonzero because the changing-electric term still feeds Ampere-Maxwell.
The magnetic circulation must vanish because only wire current can source B loops.
The electric source-law card should flip sign to replace the missing current.

items.explanation: Ampere-Maxwell includes both enclosed current and the changing-electric term. Zero conduction current does not remove magnetic circulation if the changing-electric contribution remains.
items.observationHint: Compare the current-term trace with the total magnetic-circulation trace after the conduction-current slider reaches zero.

items.prompt: If the sign of the changing-magnetic term is flipped while the changing-electric term stays the same, what should the synthesis page show?

choices.label: Faraday's circulating-E response should flip, and the light bridge cue should weaken into a misaligned state.
Only the electric source-law card should flip because charge and magnetic change are the same thing here.
Nothing important changes because both loop laws always point the same way.

items.explanation: Faraday depends directly on the sign of the magnetic-flux change, so the circulating-E response flips. If the changing-E and changing-B terms no longer align, the light-bridge cue weakens even though both loop laws still exist.
items.observationHint: Use the bridge card and the Faraday graph together. The loop law remains, but the combined bridge cue becomes misaligned.

items.title: Damping / Resonance
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Controls how quickly energy is lost from the motion.
Sets the system's preferred oscillation rate.
Controls how quickly the external force pushes the system.
Controls how strong the driver is.

controls.label: Resonance response
controls.description: Switch between following one oscillation through time and scanning steady-state amplitude across many driving frequencies.

presets.label: Free swing
presets.description: Low drive and gentle damping for a clear transient decay.

presets.label: Near resonance
presets.description: The driving frequency sits close to the natural frequency.

presets.label: Heavily damped
presets.description: The system loses energy quickly and the response stays controlled.

overlays.label: Driving force
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the external driver feeding energy into the system.
whatToNotice: This arrow shows the external driver, which can add energy even while damping is removing energy.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the source of the forcing from the oscillator's own preferred motion.

overlays.label: Response envelope
overlays.shortDescription: Outlines the decaying or driven amplitude.
whatToNotice: In transient mode, stronger damping makes the envelope tighten more quickly.
In response mode, the visible amplitude stays bounded instead of growing without limit.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you read damping as energy loss rather than just as a smaller-looking motion.

overlays.label: Resonance marker
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the driving frequency that gives the strongest steady-state response for the current damping and natural frequency.
whatToNotice: This marker belongs to the response curve, not to a later moment in time.
Changing damping can lower the peak and shift its sharpness without removing it.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the resonance condition readable as a frequency comparison rather than as a mystery spike.

graphs.label: Damped motion
graphs.description: This follows one oscillator through time and shows how damping makes the envelope shrink from cycle to cycle.
This is not a time trace. Each point shows the steady-state amplitude for one driving frequency, so the peak shows where resonance is strongest.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use the prompt to match the current view. In transient mode you are following one motion through time. In response mode you are comparing many possible steady-state responses across different driving frequencies.

items.text: Damping removes energy each cycle, so the envelope shrinks even while the oscillator keeps crossing equilibrium.
items.tryThis: Stay in the damped-motion view and compare the first few swings with the later ones instead of looking only at one instant.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest sign that the system is still oscillating while steadily losing energy.

items.text: Increasing damping makes the time trace die away faster and makes the resonance peak lower and broader.
items.tryThis: Increase Damping ratio, then compare the tighter transient envelope or the shorter, wider response peak.
items.whyItMatters: It connects one control to both the time-history view and the frequency-response view.

items.text: The response graph is not a later part of the motion. Its horizontal axis is driving frequency, so each point is a different operating condition.
items.tryThis: Switch between the stage and the response curve and ask whether you are following time or scanning operating conditions.
items.whyItMatters: Without this distinction, resonance can look like a time-based spike instead of a frequency-dependent effect.

items.text: The response is largest when the driving frequency sits close to the natural frequency.
items.tryThis: Move the drive frequency toward the peak and watch the response-curve marker and the stage amplitude strengthen together.
items.whyItMatters: Resonance is efficient energy transfer near a matching frequency, not just “big motion” for any reason.

items.text: When you pause late in the motion, the stage and graph marker still refer to the same instant even though the envelope has already shrunk.
items.tryThis: Pause late in the motion and compare the smaller displacement with the earlier swings you saw in live playback.
items.whyItMatters: Stepping through time makes damping visible as a drop in amplitude from cycle to cycle.

items.text: The setup with the taller response or the slower decay is usually the one with lower damping or a drive frequency closer to resonance.
items.tryThis: Keep one setup as the baseline, then change only Damping ratio or Drive frequency in the other setup and compare the response markers.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode helps separate changes caused by damping from changes caused by frequency matching.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict whether the change mainly affects time decay or the steady-state resonance peak, then test it on the live graph.

items.prompt: If damping is reduced while the drive stays near resonance, what happens to the response peak?

choices.label: The peak gets taller and narrower.
The peak disappears entirely.
The natural frequency changes.

items.explanation: Lower damping means less energy is lost each cycle, so the oscillator can build a larger steady-state response near resonance. That makes the peak taller and narrower.
items.observationHint: In response mode, compare both the height of the peak and how tightly it is concentrated around one frequency.

items.prompt: If the driving frequency moves away from the natural frequency, what happens to the response?

choices.label: The response drops because the driver and system stop lining up as well.
The response becomes infinite.
Damping disappears.

items.explanation: Moving the drive away from the natural frequency makes the driver and oscillator less well matched, so the energy transfer becomes less effective and the response falls.
items.observationHint: The peak stays near the natural frequency, but the marker moves down the curve as the drive frequency moves away from it.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same response curve and inspect-time tools to tune a driven oscillator instead of passively watching it.

items.title: Lock near resonance
items.prompt: Starting from Free swing, switch into the response view and tune the driver until it sits very close to resonance with a strong steady-state response.
items.successMessage: You tuned the driver onto the resonance peak instead of merely increasing amplitude blindly. The response curve and stage are now telling the same frequency story.

setup.note: Turn on the response view, then move the drive toward the peak marker.

hints: The goal is not just a large response. The drive frequency also has to sit close to the peak frequency.
A little damping still leaves a peak, but you have to line the driver up with it.

checks.label: Open the amplitude-response graph.
Keep the resonance marker visible.
Keep the drive within $0.08\,\mathrm{rad/s}$ of the peak frequency.
Reach a response amplitude between $1.7$ and $2.2$.

items.title: Late small motion
items.prompt: From Free swing, make the transient decay quickly enough that a late inspected sample shows only a very small displacement.
items.successMessage: You used damping to control the later motion honestly. The oscillator still exists, but the envelope has collapsed to a small late-time response.

setup.note: Raise damping, then pause later in the motion to inspect the remaining amplitude.

hints: This task needs inspect mode. Pause well after the first few swings, not near the launch.
If the envelope is still broad, the damping ratio is too small.

checks.label: Keep the damped-motion graph active.
Pause into inspect mode.
Inspect a late sample between $4$ and $6\,\mathrm{s}$.
Raise damping ratio into the $0.4$ to $0.6$ band.
Make the remaining displacement amplitude smaller than $0.15$.

items.title: Diffraction
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

keyIdeas: The important comparison is lambda / a. Larger lambda or smaller a makes diffraction more noticeable.
The first minimum marks the edge of the central bright region, so when it moves farther from the center the central peak gets wider.
The screen pattern is not random blur. It comes from path differences across different parts of the same slit.
Double-slit interference still sits inside diffraction envelopes from the individual slits.

controls.description: Longer wavelength makes the same opening spread the wave more strongly.
A narrower opening broadens the central diffraction peak.
Moves the screen probe to a new point on the diffraction pattern.

presets.label: Center bright
presets.description: The probe sits at the center of the main diffraction peak.

presets.label: Wide slit
presets.description: A wide opening keeps the spread relatively tight.

presets.label: Broad spread
presets.description: Wavelength and opening are close enough that the outgoing wave spreads much more strongly.

presets.label: Opening matches wavelength
presets.description: When lambda is about the same size as the opening, the pattern becomes very broad.

presets.label: Near first minimum
presets.description: The probe is placed close to the first dark band for the default opening and wavelength.

presets.label: Tight spread
presets.description: Shorter wavelength with a wide opening keeps the central peak narrow.

overlays.label: Slit-width guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the opening width directly on the barrier.
whatToNotice: Diffraction depends on how wide the opening is compared with the wavelength, not just on where the probe sits.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the aperture size visible as part of the physics.

overlays.label: Edge paths
overlays.shortDescription: Draws the top and bottom slit-edge paths to the current probe point.
whatToNotice: One screen point receives contributions from the top and bottom of the same slit along slightly different paths.
overlays.whyItMatters: This is the geometry behind the diffraction envelope.

overlays.label: First-minimum guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows where the first dark points would sit when the current ratio lambda / a allows a finite minimum.
whatToNotice: As lambda / a grows, the first minimum moves farther from the center, so the central peak gets wider.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns 'more spread' into a concrete screen location.

graphs.label: Probe field
graphs.description: Tracks one screen point in time, showing the probe field and its local envelope at that point.
Shows relative intensity across the whole screen, so it maps the diffraction pattern in space rather than in time.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. The goal is to connect the slit geometry to the width of the central peak and the location of the dark bands.

items.text: Notice that the center is not only the brightest part of the pattern. It is also the widest bright region, and its width changes when lambda / a changes.
items.tryThis: Start from Center bright, then compare Wide slit with Broad spread before reading the graph.
items.whyItMatters: The main learning target is how the spread changes, not just where the brightness is highest.

items.text: Notice that narrowing the slit makes the outgoing wave spread more, even though the physical opening got smaller.
items.tryThis: Lower the slit width while holding wavelength fixed and compare how far the first-minimum guide moves.
items.whyItMatters: This directly corrects the common beam-gets-narrower misconception.

items.text: Notice that increasing the wavelength broadens the central peak because the same opening is now smaller compared with one wavelength.
items.tryThis: Keep the slit fixed, raise lambda, and watch both the strip and the pattern graph spread outward.
items.whyItMatters: It turns the ratio lambda / a into something spatially visible.

items.text: Notice that stepping through time changes the oscillation at the current probe, but the pattern graph stays fixed because it is a spatial intensity map across the screen.
items.tryThis: Pause on the probe-field graph, step once, and compare what changed at the probe with what stayed fixed on the pattern graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates time variation at one point from the position pattern across the whole screen.

items.text: Compare mode is strongest when one setup changes lambda and the other changes a. Then you can see the same ratio story coming from two different causes.
items.tryThis: Clone the live setup, widen the slit in one row, and lengthen the wavelength in the other row before reading the graph.
items.whyItMatters: It stops diffraction from collapsing into one vague 'more spread' control.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the change in spread before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time.

items.prompt: Keep wavelength fixed and make the slit narrower. What should happen to the central diffraction peak?

choices.label: It should broaden because the wave spreads more strongly when the opening is smaller relative to the wavelength.
It should stay the same width because only wavelength matters for diffraction.
It should get narrower because the opening is narrower.

items.explanation: Diffraction depends on lambda / a. Making a smaller increases that ratio, so the central peak broadens and the first minimum moves outward.
items.observationHint: Watch the central peak widen and the first minimum move outward.

items.prompt: Keep the slit fixed and increase the wavelength. What should happen to the pattern?

choices.label: The pattern should spread out because the same opening is now small compared with the wavelength.
The pattern should tighten because longer waves bend less.
Only the probe-field graph should change. The screen pattern should stay fixed.

items.explanation: Making lambda larger increases lambda / a, so the central peak broadens and the minima move farther from the center.
items.observationHint: The graph spreads outward even before you move the probe.

items.prompt: Leave the slit and wavelength alone, but move the probe from the center toward the first dark band. What should happen locally?

choices.label: The probe-field envelope should shrink toward zero because the screen point approaches a diffraction minimum.
The probe should keep the same envelope because only slit width sets intensity.
The local field should grow because points farther from the center always receive more path difference.

items.explanation: Moving the probe does not change the pattern. It only changes which part of the existing envelope you sample, so the local intensity can drop toward zero near a minimum.
items.observationHint: Watch the probe marker approach the first dark region on the strip and graph.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Turn the spread pattern into small wave-optics hunts that stay tied to the same slit geometry.

items.title: Set a clear spread angle
items.prompt: Starting from Wide slit, tune the controls until the first minimum lands between 22 deg and 28 deg.
items.successMessage: You tuned the spread by changing the wavelength-to-opening ratio, not by moving the probe. The pattern width now matches the first-minimum target honestly.

setup.note: Use the first-minimum guide as the target while you adjust lambda and a.

hints: You need a larger ratio lambda / a than Wide slit currently has.
Increasing wavelength or decreasing slit width can move the first minimum outward.

targets.label: Set the first minimum angle between 22 deg and 28 deg.

items.title: Find the first dark band
items.prompt: Starting from Center bright, move the probe onto the first dark band without changing the slit or wavelength.
items.successMessage: You found the first minimum by changing only the sampled screen point. The edge-path split is about one wavelength and the local intensity has collapsed.

setup.note: Drag the probe instead of changing the opening.

hints: You are looking for the first place where the edge-path difference is about one wavelength.
The pattern graph is the quickest way to steer the probe toward a dark point.

targets.label: Bring the edge-path split close to one wavelength.
Drive the relative intensity below 0.08.

items.title: Basic Circuits
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Increase the push from the battery without changing the resistor values.
Sets the amber resistance in either the series loop or the upper parallel branch.
Sets the blue resistance and lets you test both unequal series division and unequal parallel current splits.

controls.label: Use parallel branches
controls.description: Off keeps one series loop. On gives charge two branches across the same battery.

presets.label: Matched series pair
presets.description: Two equal resistors in one loop make the current and voltage sharing easy to read.

presets.label: Matched parallel pair
presets.description: The same two resistors now sit in parallel so each branch sees the full battery voltage.

presets.label: Uneven series divider
presets.description: One small and one large series resistor show equal current but unequal voltage drops.

presets.label: Branch split
presets.description: Unequal parallel branches keep the same voltage but pull different currents.

presets.label: Low-voltage check
presets.description: A smaller battery makes the same matched series pair pull half as much current.

overlays.label: Current arrows
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the total current and the branch currents directly on the circuit.
whatToNotice: Series shows one loop current everywhere, while parallel shows one total current that splits into branch currents and recombines.
overlays.whyItMatters: Current direction and current sharing stay tied to one honest circuit instead of drifting into disconnected numbers.

overlays.label: Voltage drops
overlays.shortDescription: Labels the battery voltage and the resistor or branch voltages on the stage.
whatToNotice: In series the source voltage is shared, but in parallel each branch keeps the full battery voltage.
overlays.whyItMatters: Voltage becomes a real difference around the circuit rather than a detached symbol.

overlays.label: Node guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the shared node voltages that explain equal branch voltage in parallel and the internal node in series.
whatToNotice: Both parallel resistors connect across the same top and bottom nodes, so they must share the same branch voltage.
overlays.whyItMatters: Node thinking helps voltage feel like a location-to-location difference instead of a mysterious property attached to one resistor.

graphs.label: Ohm's law current map
graphs.description: Sweep the battery voltage while keeping the resistor values and topology fixed. Straight lines show that current changes linearly with voltage for one fixed circuit.

graphs.label: Voltage share vs resistor B
graphs.description: Sweep only resistor B while keeping the battery and resistor A fixed. In series the voltage shares depend on resistance ratio, while in parallel both branches stay at the battery voltage.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use each prompt as a short investigation. Make the change, then connect what the circuit shows on the stage to what the graphs show.

items.text: For one fixed circuit, the current map stays linear because current is proportional to voltage.
items.tryThis: Open the current map, hover a lower voltage and then a higher voltage, and compare how every current label on the stage scales with the same straight-line trend.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest graph version of Ohm's law before extra circuit complexity is added.

items.text: In series, the same loop current passes through both resistors. Changing either resistor changes that same current everywhere in the loop.
items.tryThis: Stay in the matched series preset, then drag only resistor B upward and watch both current labels shrink together.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents the common mistake of treating current like a quantity that gets partly consumed by the first resistor.

items.text: In parallel mode, both branches connect across the same two nodes, so each branch keeps the full battery voltage even when the branch currents are different.
items.tryThis: Switch to the branch-split preset and compare the flat voltage-share lines with the unequal current arrows on the stage.
items.whyItMatters: Parallel circuits only become intuitive once equal branch voltage and unequal branch current can coexist in the same picture.

items.text: Switching from series to parallel does not add more battery voltage. It changes the equivalent resistance the battery sees.
items.tryThis: Toggle only the topology while the battery and resistor values stay fixed. Watch the readout card: the battery stays the same, but R_eq and I_total jump immediately.
items.whyItMatters: Many beginner errors come from blaming the current increase on a stronger source instead of on a smaller equivalent resistance.

items.text: Compare mode is most useful here when both setups keep the same battery and resistor values but use different topologies.
items.tryThis: Clone a matched series setup into Setup B, switch only Setup B to parallel, and compare the ghost circuit, readout badges, and dashed graph lines.
items.whyItMatters: This isolates topology as the causal change instead of mixing topology changes with resistor changes at the same time.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the circuit response before you apply the change. Each scenario changes one cause at a time so you can track how voltage, current, and equivalent resistance are linked.

items.prompt: From the low-voltage series check, if the battery rises from 6 V to 12 V while both resistors stay at 6 ohm in series, what must happen?

choices.label: The total current doubles because the same 12 ohm series load now has twice the voltage across it.
The total current stays the same because the resistors did not change.
Only the voltage-share graph changes because current depends only on resistance.

items.explanation: For one fixed resistance, Ohm's law is linear. Doubling the battery voltage doubles the current everywhere in the same series loop.
items.observationHint: The lines on the current map keep the same slope family, and the live current labels jump from 0.5 A to 1 A.

items.prompt: If the same 6 ohm and 6 ohm pair switches from series to parallel at 12 V, which statement is most defensible?

choices.label: Equivalent resistance drops, total current rises, and each branch keeps the full 12 V.
Equivalent resistance stays the same, but the battery voltage is split in half between the branches.
Total current falls because there are now two resistors instead of one loop.

items.explanation: Parallel branches reduce the equivalent resistance seen by the battery. The same source voltage now drives a larger total current, and each branch still spans the full battery voltage.
items.observationHint: Compare the matched series and matched parallel presets: the battery value is unchanged while R_eq and I_total are not.

items.prompt: In series mode with V = 12 V and R_A = 4 ohm, what happens if R_B increases from 4 ohm to 12 ohm?

choices.label: Total current drops, and resistor B takes a larger share of the source voltage.
Total current rises because resistor B now stores more charge.
The current stays fixed, but resistor A takes the larger voltage share.

items.explanation: Raising one series resistor raises the equivalent resistance, so the same battery drives less loop current. With the same current through both resistors, the larger resistor gets the larger voltage drop.
items.observationHint: The current arrows shrink together, and the blue voltage tag grows larger than the amber one.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same compact battery-resistor model to build circuit targets. The checks come from the live stage, not a separate answer key.

items.title: Build a full-voltage parallel pair
items.prompt: Starting from the matched series pair, change only the circuit structure needed so each branch gets the full battery voltage and the total current is about 4 A.
items.successMessage: You turned one shared series loop into a true parallel pair. The branch voltage stayed at the battery value while the lighter equivalent resistance raised the total current.

setup.note: Use the voltage-share graph and the node guide together while you rewire the equal pair.

hints: Keep the resistor values matched. The target comes from changing topology, not from changing the load values.
If each branch keeps the full battery voltage, the total current should jump above the series-loop value.

targets.label: Keep resistancea between 5.8 ohm and 6.2 ohm.
Keep resistanceb between 5.8 ohm and 6.2 ohm.
Keep equivalentresistance between 2.9 ohm and 3.1 ohm.
Keep totalcurrent between 3.9 A and 4.1 A.
Keep branchbvoltage between 11.8 V and 12.2 V.

items.title: Polarization
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.label: Input amplitude
controls.description: Scales the incoming field height without changing the orientation geometry.

controls.label: Input angle
controls.description: Rotates the incoming linear polarization direction.

controls.label: Polarizer angle
controls.description: Rotates the transmission axis of the ideal polarizer.

controls.label: Unpolarized input
controls.description: Switch from one fixed input orientation to a mixed transverse input with no preferred axis.

presets.label: Baseline linear
presets.description: A partially misaligned linear input so both transmitted and blocked components stay visible.

presets.label: Aligned pass
presets.description: Input and axis match, so the detector stays bright.

presets.label: Half power
presets.description: A 45 degree mismatch leaves half the intensity on the detector.

presets.label: Crossed axes
presets.description: A 90 degree mismatch suppresses the transmitted intensity almost completely.

overlays.label: Transverse cross-section
overlays.shortDescription: Separates beam direction from the sideways field direction.
whatToNotice: The beam still travels to the right while polarization lives in the perpendicular cross-section.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates wave propagation from wave orientation.

overlays.label: Projection guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the axis-parallel component that survives and the perpendicular component that is blocked.
whatToNotice: Rotating the relative angle changes how much field lies along the axis; it does not turn the beam itself.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns Malus's law back into visible geometry.

overlays.label: Detector split
overlays.shortDescription: Shows transmitted and blocked shares next to the detector.
whatToNotice: For linear input, brightness follows orientation match. For an unpolarized first pass, the detector stays near half on average.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps compare aligned, crossed, and unpolarized cases without mixing them up.

graphs.label: Power split vs polarizer angle
graphs.description: Sweeps the polarizer axis to compare transmitted and blocked intensity fractions on the same response curve.

graphs.label: Field projection vs polarizer angle
graphs.description: Shows how the axis-aligned and perpendicular field components trade off as the polarizer rotates.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep one question in mind: how much of the field lies along the polarizer axis?

items.text: When the input and axis line up, almost the whole field already points along the axis, so the detector stays bright.
items.whyItMatters: A polarizer keeps an existing component; it does not create one from nothing.

items.text: With crossed axes, the beam direction is unchanged but the axis-parallel component is nearly zero, so the detector goes dark.
items.tryThis: Use the Crossed axes preset and compare the detector badge with the projection callout.
items.whyItMatters: It ties zero transmission to orientation mismatch, not to the beam stopping or bending.

items.text: Switching to unpolarized input removes the single starting direction. One ideal first polarizer still creates a linearly polarized output, but the detector stays near half intensity on average.
items.tryThis: Turn on Unpolarized input and rotate the axis through several angles.
items.whyItMatters: It separates a mixed input state from the linearly polarized output created by the filter.
That separates a cosine-projection story from the half-intensity average story.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the orientation consequence before you apply the change. Ask what component lies along the axis and what that means for detector brightness.

items.prompt: Start from Aligned pass and rotate the polarizer to 90 degrees away from the input. What should happen to the detector?

choices.label: The detector should go nearly dark because the input has almost no component along the new axis.
The detector should stay equally bright because the input amplitude has not changed.
The beam should bend toward the new axis while keeping the same detector fraction.

items.explanation: At 90° mismatch, an ideal linear input has zero projection onto the axis, so transmitted intensity collapses.
items.observationHint: Watch the blocked component grow as the detector drops.

items.prompt: Leave the input linear, but rotate the axis until the mismatch is 45 degrees. Which detector result should appear?

choices.label: The detector should read about half the incoming intensity because cos squared 45 degrees is one half.
The detector should read zero because any mismatch blocks the beam.
The detector should stay fully bright because the output always follows the axis.

items.explanation: At 45°, E_out = E_0 cos 45° and I_out = I_0 cos^2 45° = I_0/2.
items.observationHint: The field projection falls to about 0.707 while the detector settles near 0.5.

items.prompt: Switch the input from one fixed linear orientation to an unpolarized beam while leaving the axis alone. What should stay true on this ideal first-polarizer bench?

choices.label: The detector should settle near half intensity and the output should leave polarized along the axis.
The detector should go completely dark because the input no longer has one angle.
The output should remain unpolarized because one polarizer cannot create an orientation.

items.explanation: One ideal first polarizer creates a linearly polarized output from an unpolarized beam, but only half the intensity gets through on average.
items.observationHint: Rotate the axis and notice that the output axis changes, but the average transmitted fraction stays near one half.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same compact bench for small polarization hunts instead of separate worksheets.

items.title: Create a half-bright first polarizer
items.prompt: Starting from Crossed axes, switch the bench to an unpolarized first-pass case that still leaves the detector near half brightness.
items.successMessage: You turned the crossed linear case into an unpolarized first-polarizer case. The detector is back near one half because the input state changed, not because the axes re-aligned.

setup.note: Change the input state rather than chasing alignment.

hints: The key move is not rotating back to alignment.
A mixed input state can still give about half intensity through one ideal polarizer.

targets.label: Turn on the unpolarized input state.
Keep the detector fraction between 0.48 and 0.52.

items.title: Lens Imaging
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

keyIdeas: For a converging lens, an object outside the focal length gives a real inverted image, while an object inside the focal length gives a virtual upright image.
A diverging lens still forms an image for a real object on the left, but that image is always virtual, upright, and reduced.
The sign of the image distance tells which side of the lens the image is on: positive for a real image on the far side, negative for a virtual image on the object side.
The sign of magnification tells orientation, while its magnitude tells how much the image size has been scaled.

controls.label: Converging lens
controls.description: Turn this off to switch to a diverging lens with the same focal-length magnitude but the opposite focal-length sign.

controls.label: Focal-length magnitude
controls.description: Sets the focal-point positions and how strongly the lens bends the rays.
Moves the object along the principal axis and can move a converging-lens image from the real branch to the virtual branch when the object crosses F.
Changes the object size so the magnified image height can be compared directly without changing where the image forms.

presets.label: Converging reference
presets.description: A converging lens with the object outside F, giving a real inverted image on the far side.

presets.label: Diverging reference
presets.description: A diverging lens giving a virtual upright reduced image.

overlays.label: F and 2F markers
overlays.shortDescription: Shows F and 2F on both sides of the lens.
whatToNotice: For a converging lens, crossing F changes the image from real to virtual.
overlays.whyItMatters: The focal markers anchor the standard lens regions directly on the stage.

overlays.label: Principal rays
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the construction rays that locate the image point.
whatToNotice: Actual ray intersections give real images, while backward extensions give virtual images.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the sign of d_i to visible ray behavior instead of to a memorized sign rule.

overlays.label: Distance and height guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the signed distances and the object/image height comparison used in magnification.
whatToNotice: The sign of m sets orientation, while |m| sets the size change.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the stage directly to the magnification equation.

graphs.label: Object to image map
graphs.description: Shows how signed image distance changes with object distance for the current lens. Positive values are real images on the far side, and negative values are virtual images on the object side.
Shows how m changes with object distance for the current lens. Negative values are inverted images, positive values are upright images, and |m| gives the size scale.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use the live prompt to connect the signed equation, the rays, and the graph branch for the current setup.

items.text: Notice that real images come from real ray crossings, while virtual images come from backward extensions.
items.tryThis: Switch between the converging reference and the inside-focus preset, then compare whether the rays really meet on the far side of the lens.
items.whyItMatters: That one difference explains why only real images can land on a screen.

items.text: Notice that the object-image graph is based on object distance, not time. Hovering it selects a different geometric setup immediately.
items.tryThis: Hover the image-distance graph and watch the ray diagram jump to the matching object position immediately.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps the graph honest: you are comparing families of lens setups, not stepping through time.

items.text: Notice how the image distance grows very large as a converging-lens object approaches F from outside.
items.tryThis: Slide the object inward on a converging lens until it is just outside F, then compare the graph and the stage edge label.
items.whyItMatters: F is the dividing line between the real-image branch and the virtual-image branch.

items.text: Notice that the sign of m tells orientation right away: negative means inverted and positive means upright.
items.tryThis: Open Magnification, then compare the image arrow direction with whether the graph point sits above or below zero.
items.whyItMatters: The magnification graph is not only about size. It also tells you image orientation.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the branch change or orientation change first, then apply the setup and compare the ray diagram with the graphs.

items.prompt: Starting from the converging reference, what happens if the object moves closer to the lens but stays outside the focal point?

choices.label: The real image moves farther away and the magnitude of the magnification grows.
The image flips upright immediately and moves onto the object side.
Nothing important changes because the focal length stayed fixed.

items.explanation: For a converging lens with the object still outside F, moving the object inward pushes the real image farther away and increases the magnitude of m. The image stays real and inverted until the object crosses F.
items.observationHint: Watch the image-map point climb the real branch while the inverted image arrow grows.

items.prompt: What changes if the converging-lens object crosses inside the focal length?

choices.label: The image becomes virtual, upright, and enlarged on the object side.
The image stays real, inverted, and closer to the lens on the far side.
The lens stops producing any image point.

items.explanation: Inside F, the refracted rays diverge after the lens. Their backward extensions meet on the object side, so the image becomes virtual, upright, and enlarged.
items.observationHint: The dashed backward extensions now locate the image on the virtual branch of the graph.

items.prompt: If you switch from the converging reference to a diverging lens with the same |f| and a similar object distance, what should happen?

choices.label: The image stays virtual, upright, and reduced on the object side.
The image becomes real and easier to project because the lens spreads the rays more.
The image keeps the same branch and orientation because only the sign of f changed.

items.explanation: Changing the sign of the focal length changes the whole branch structure. For a real object on the left, a diverging lens keeps the image virtual, upright, and reduced.
items.observationHint: The graph point stays on the virtual and upright side while the backward extensions locate the image on the left.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the real lens controls and signed graphs to hit image targets. The checks read the live distances and magnification from the same bench.

items.title: Build a real inverted image
items.prompt: Starting from the converging reference, tune the setup until the image stays real with d_i between 1.0 and 1.2 m and m between -1.4 and -1.1.
items.successMessage: You matched a real inverted image by steering the object onto the correct real-image branch instead of guessing by eye.

setup.note: Use the image-distance graph and the ray intersection together.

hints: Move the object inward from the reference setup, but keep it outside the focal point.
The magnification should stay negative because the image is still inverted.

checks.label: Open the object-image map.
Keep the principal rays visible.
Make d_i land between 1.0 and 1.2 m.
Keep magnification between -1.4 and -1.1.

items.title: Build a virtual upright image
items.prompt: Starting from the diverging reference, tune the setup until the image stays virtual with d_i between -0.65 and -0.45 m and m between 0.3 and 0.5.
items.successMessage: You used the signed graph honestly: the image stayed virtual and upright while the reduced magnification set the final size.

setup.note: Use the magnification graph and the height guide together.

hints: A diverging lens keeps the image on the virtual side, so do not try to force d_i positive.
Adjust object distance and focal-length magnitude to move both d_i and m.

checks.label: Open the magnification graph.
Keep the distance and height guide visible.
Make d_i land between -0.65 and -0.45 m.
Keep magnification between 0.3 and 0.5.

items.title: Photoelectric Effect
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Changes the energy of each photon and the beam's color band.
Changes photon flux, so above threshold it mainly changes current.
Changes how much energy electrons need to escape the metal.
Helps or opposes electrons after they leave the metal.

presets.label: Red below threshold
presets.description: Long-wavelength light that cannot eject electrons from this metal.

presets.label: Bright but still below threshold
presets.description: Raise intensity without crossing the threshold.

presets.label: Violet above threshold
presets.description: Shorter wavelength light now clears the work function.

presets.label: Dim but above threshold
presets.description: Keep electron energy but lower the emission rate.

presets.label: Stopping-potential cutoff
presets.description: Use a retarding field near the stopping point.

presets.label: Harder metal
presets.description: Raise the work function so the same light gets closer to threshold.

overlays.label: Threshold gate
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the threshold frequency and work-function barrier together.
whatToNotice: Emission starts only when the frequency marker crosses f0 for the chosen metal.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the threshold visible before you calculate it.

overlays.label: Intensity flux
overlays.shortDescription: Shows that brightness changes photon flow and current, not energy per photon.
whatToNotice: Above threshold, the electron stream gets denser when intensity rises, but the energy budget changes only when frequency or work function changes.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates electron count from electron energy.

overlays.label: Stopping field
overlays.shortDescription: Shows whether the collector voltage helps, reduces, or stops the collected electrons.
whatToNotice: The cutoff voltage depends on the emitted electrons' energy, not just on how bright the light is.
overlays.whyItMatters: It connects the collector graph to the same energy story shown on the stage.

overlays.label: Energy budget
overlays.shortDescription: Keeps hf, phi, and Kmax visible in one place near the stage.
whatToNotice: Changing intensity does not move hf, phi, or Kmax. Only frequency and work function do.
overlays.whyItMatters: It is the clearest way to see why threshold depends on photon energy rather than brightness alone.

graphs.label: Energy budget vs frequency
graphs.description: Shows when photon energy is below the work function, equal to it, or above it, and how much is left as Kmax.

graphs.label: Collected current vs collector voltage
graphs.description: Shows how a more negative collector voltage reduces collected current until the stopping point is reached.

graphs.label: Current vs intensity
graphs.description: Shows that changing intensity changes current size while the per-electron energy stays fixed.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to separate threshold, intensity, and collector-voltage ideas while staying on the same bench.

items.text: Below threshold, making the lamp brighter still does not produce collected electrons. The missing ingredient is photon energy, not total brightness.
items.tryThis: Switch from Red below threshold to Bright but still below threshold while keeping the energy-budget card in view.
items.whyItMatters: It directly tests the idea that brightness alone should eject electrons.

items.text: Once the frequency is above threshold, raising intensity mainly raises the current, not the maximum kinetic energy.
items.tryThis: Compare Violet above threshold with Dim but above threshold.
items.whyItMatters: It separates how many electrons are emitted from how energetic the fastest ones are.

items.text: Making the collector voltage more negative reduces the collected current, and cutoff happens near the displayed stopping potential.
items.tryThis: Open the collector-voltage sweep and hover near the cutoff point.
items.whyItMatters: It turns stopping potential into a measurable quantity on the same bench.

items.text: Compare mode works best when one setup changes frequency and the other changes intensity. That lets you separate threshold from brightness.
items.tryThis: Keep Setup A on Bright but still below threshold and Setup B on Violet above threshold.
items.whyItMatters: Side by side, it becomes clear that frequency and intensity do different jobs.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the cause-and-effect change before you move the control.

items.prompt: Start with Red below threshold and raise only the intensity. What should the bench do next?

choices.label: The beam looks brighter, but no electrons are emitted because each photon is still below threshold.
Electrons begin to emerge slowly because the beam is now bright enough overall.
The stopping potential rises because brighter light always gives faster electrons.

items.explanation: Increasing intensity changes photon flux, not photon energy, so below-threshold light still cannot eject electrons.
items.observationHint: Watch the beam brighten while the energy-budget card stays below the work-function barrier and current stays zero.

items.prompt: Keep intensity fixed but increase the frequency from red below threshold to violet above threshold. What must change together?

choices.label: Emission turns on, the maximum kinetic energy rises above zero, and the stopping potential becomes nonzero.
Only the beam color changes because the intensity stayed fixed.
The current rises, but the stopping potential stays the same because voltage belongs to the collector only.

items.explanation: Frequency changes energy per photon. Crossing threshold is what turns emission on and makes Kmax and stopping potential nonzero.
items.observationHint: Watch hf cross phi on the energy graph, then read Kmax and Vstop.

items.prompt: Keep the same above-threshold light but make the collector voltage more negative. Which statement stays honest?

choices.label: The collected current drops toward zero, but the electrons leave the metal with the same Kmax.
The retarding field lowers the photon's energy before it reaches the metal.
The current stays unchanged because only frequency matters in the photoelectric effect.

items.explanation: The collector field changes how many emitted electrons reach the collector. It does not change the surface energy budget set by hf and phi.
items.observationHint: Track the collector-sweep graph while the energy-budget card stays fixed.

targets.label: Keep collectorvoltage between -1.95 V and -1.72 V.
Keep maxkineticenergyev between 1.75 eV and 1.92 eV.
Keep collectorcurrent between 0 arb. and 0.05 arb..
Keep intensity between 1.45 arb. and 1.8 arb..
Keep frequencyphz between 0.3 PHz and 0.4 PHz.
Keep collectorcurrent between 0 arb. and 0.02 arb..

items.title: Atomic Spectra
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Set the energy difference between levels 2 and 1.
Set the energy difference between levels 3 and 2.
Set the energy difference between levels 4 and 3.

controls.label: Show absorption case
controls.description: Switch between bright emission lines and dark absorption notches while keeping the same allowed wavelengths.

presets.label: Hydrogen-like emission
presets.description: Three visible emission lines with several ultraviolet jumps above them.

presets.label: Hydrogen-like absorption
presets.description: The same line positions now appear as dark notches in a continuum.

presets.label: Clustered visible lines
presets.description: Place three visible lines closer together to compare their spacing.

presets.label: Wide upper gap
presets.description: Push the highest single-step line toward the ultraviolet while lower gaps stay visible.

presets.label: Infrared plus visible
presets.description: Let one smaller gap slip into the infrared while two other lines stay visible.

overlays.label: Transition pairs
overlays.shortDescription: Links the active ladder jump directly to its wavelength in the spectrum strip.
whatToNotice: The active ladder arrow, strip line, and graph position move together because they represent the same energy change.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you connect energy-level changes to spectral lines instead of treating them as separate pictures.

overlays.label: Line labels
overlays.shortDescription: Shows which level pair belongs to each spectral line and where it lands in nanometers.
whatToNotice: Changing one gap can move several labeled lines because some larger jumps include that same gap.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the line pattern easier to explain from the ladder.

overlays.label: Mode lock
overlays.shortDescription: Keeps the same allowed wavelengths visible when the page flips between emission and absorption.
whatToNotice: Bright peaks become dark notches at the same wavelengths because the allowed level differences are unchanged.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates where a line appears from how it appears.

overlays.label: Quantized spacing
overlays.shortDescription: Reminds you that only allowed level gaps produce lines, so the spectrum is not continuously filled in.
whatToNotice: Empty wavelength regions are expected whenever no allowed energy gap lands there.
overlays.whyItMatters: It is the clearest visual cue that the spectrum is discrete, not continuous.

graphs.label: Observed spectrum
graphs.description: Shows the current line pattern across ultraviolet, visible, and infrared wavelengths. In absorption mode, the continuum stays in place while dark notches mark the same allowed wavelengths.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time so the ladder, strip, and graph keep telling the same energy-gap story.

items.text: Most wavelengths stay empty because this ladder allows only a few energy differences. The spectrum is sparse because of the model, not because the graph is hiding anything.
items.tryThis: Start on Hydrogen-like emission and look between the visible lines on the graph.
items.whyItMatters: It is the clearest reason this page shows lines instead of a smooth rainbow.

items.text: Changing one gap can move more than one line because some larger jumps include that same gap.
items.tryThis: Raise only the 2 to 1 gap and watch which labeled lines shift together.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you read the spectrum as a map of level differences, not a list of unrelated colors.

items.text: Switching to absorption does not move the allowed wavelengths. It changes the same lines from bright peaks into dark notches.
items.tryThis: Flip between the hydrogen-like emission and absorption presets.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps emission and absorption tied to the same energy-level structure.

items.text: Compare mode works best when one setup changes a gap and the other changes only the mode. That lets you separate changes in line position from changes in appearance.
items.tryThis: Keep Setup A on Hydrogen-like emission and Setup B on Hydrogen-like absorption, then change only one gap in Setup A.
items.whyItMatters: Side by side, you can tell whether a difference came from the energy ladder or just from emission versus absorption.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Commit to what the line pattern should do before you move a gap or flip the mode.

items.prompt: Start from Hydrogen-like emission and increase only the 2 to 1 gap. What should happen first?

choices.label: The 2 -> 1 line shifts to shorter wavelength, and any larger jump that includes level 1 also shifts.
Only the uppermost 4 -> 3 line shifts because the bottom gap is isolated.
The line intensities change, but the wavelengths stay fixed because the number of levels did not change.

items.explanation: Wavelength comes from the energy gap. If a larger jump includes the same lower gap, its total energy changes too.
items.observationHint: Watch the labeled lines move on the strip and the graph together.

items.prompt: Keep the same ladder but switch from emission to absorption. Which statement stays honest?

choices.label: The allowed wavelengths stay the same, but bright peaks become dark notches.
All wavelengths reverse order because absorption reads the ladder upward.
Only ultraviolet lines remain because absorption removes visible light first.

items.explanation: The level differences have not changed, so the allowed wavelengths remain locked. Only the appearance of each line changes.
items.observationHint: Focus on the same x positions on the spectrum graph before and after the toggle.

items.prompt: Reduce only the 3 to 2 gap while leaving the other gaps alone. What should the pattern do?

choices.label: The 3 -> 2 line moves to longer wavelength, and some larger jumps shift while others stay where they were.
Every line moves by the same wavelength amount because all gaps shrink together.
Nothing changes in the spectrum because the top and bottom gaps stayed fixed.

items.explanation: Only the jumps that include the 3 to 2 spacing change. That makes the line pattern shift non-uniformly, which is part of the evidence for structured level differences.
items.observationHint: Compare the labeled 3 -> 2 line with the larger jumps that bypass it.

targets.label: Keep modeflag between 0 and 0.
Keep visiblelinecount between 2 and 2.
Keep shortestvisiblewavelengthnm between 43 nm and 52 nm.
Keep longestvisiblewavelengthnm between 62 nm and 72 nm.
Keep minvisibleseparationnm between 12 nm and 26 nm.
Keep modeflag between 1 and 1.
Keep visiblelinecount between 3 and 3.
Keep shortestvisiblewavelengthnm between 46 nm and 54 nm.
Keep longestvisiblewavelengthnm between 62 nm and 7 nm.
Keep minvisibleseparationnm between 50 nm and 11 nm.

items.title: de Broglie Matter Waves
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

keyIdeas: de Broglie wavelength is tied to momentum by \(\lambda = h / p\), so the main inverse relation is between wavelength and momentum.
On this bounded non-relativistic page, \(p = mv\), so increasing either mass or speed increases momentum and shortens the wavelength.
The strip and the loop are two views of the same \(\lambda\): when the spacing shrinks on the strip, more wavelengths fit around the loop.
A whole-number loop fit is a useful bridge toward quantized behavior and the Bohr model, but it is not the full modern quantum picture.

controls.description: Set the particle mass in electron-mass units.
Change the speed and watch momentum, wavelength, and loop fit update together.

presets.label: Slow electron
