presets.description: A low-momentum electron with a comparatively long matter wavelength.

presets.label: Near one-fit electron
presets.description: An electron speed that makes the fixed loop close to one wavelength long.

presets.label: Near two-fit electron
presets.description: Roughly double the momentum, so the wavelength is about half as long and the loop fit count is close to two.

presets.label: Heavier same speed
presets.description: Keep the speed similar but raise the mass so the wavelength shortens from momentum alone.

presets.label: Same momentum swap
presets.description: Double the mass and halve the speed so the momentum, wavelength, and loop fit stay about the same.

overlays.label: Wavelength guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks one crest-to-crest spacing on the local matter-wave strip.
whatToNotice: When momentum increases, the marked spacing shrinks on the strip and the loop fit count rises.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes wavelength visible as a spacing on the stage, not just as a number in an equation.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows mass and speed combining into one momentum before wavelength is set.
whatToNotice: Different mass-speed pairs can produce the same momentum and therefore the same wavelength.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the focus on momentum as the variable behind the wavelength.

overlays.label: Whole-number fit
overlays.shortDescription: Compares the current loop count with the nearest whole-number match.
whatToNotice: At whole numbers the wrapped wave meets itself cleanly; between them the seam does not match.
overlays.whyItMatters: It gives a compact visual bridge from wavelength to early quantized behavior.

graphs.label: Wavelength vs momentum
graphs.description: Read the inverse de Broglie relation directly: moving to larger momentum means moving to shorter wavelength.

graphs.label: Loop fit vs momentum
graphs.description: As momentum increases, the fixed loop holds more wavelengths because the wavelength gets shorter.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time so the strip, loop, and graphs all stay tied to the same momentum-based state.

items.text: The strip and the loop are showing the same wavelength. When the spacing on the strip shrinks, the loop fit count rises.
items.tryThis: Start from Near one-fit electron, then raise the speed and watch both panels together.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you read the bench as one connected story instead of two separate pictures.

items.text: Hovering either graph previews the same bench at that momentum, not a separate example.
items.tryThis: Hover farther to the right on either graph and compare the tighter spacing with the larger loop count.
items.whyItMatters: The graphs and the bench are two views of the same momentum-based state.

items.text: At fixed mass, increasing speed raises momentum, shortens the wavelength, and pushes the loop toward a larger fit count.
items.tryThis: Move from Near one-fit electron toward Near two-fit electron.
items.whyItMatters: It turns \(\lambda = h / p\) into something you can see on the stage.

items.text: At fixed speed, increasing mass still shortens the wavelength here because mass changes momentum too.
items.tryThis: Compare Near one-fit electron with Heavier same speed.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents the common mistake that only speed matters for de Broglie wavelength.

items.text: Compare a heavier slower case with a lighter faster case that keeps the same momentum. The wavelength should stay the same.
items.tryThis: Put Setup A on Near one-fit electron and Setup B on Same momentum swap.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that momentum, not mass or speed alone, is the control variable behind the wavelength.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the momentum and wavelength change before you test it on the bench.

items.prompt: Start from Near one-fit electron and raise only the speed while keeping the mass fixed. What should happen to the wavelength and the loop fit count?

choices.label: The wavelength gets shorter and more wavelengths fit around the loop.
The wavelength gets longer because the particle covers more distance each second.
The wavelength stays the same because the mass did not change.

items.explanation: At fixed mass, a larger speed means a larger momentum. Since \(\lambda = h / p\), the wavelength gets shorter and \(N = L / \lambda\) rises.
items.observationHint: The local spacing shrinks while the loop count moves from about one fit toward about two.

items.prompt: Keep the speed about the same but make the particle heavier. Which statement stays honest in this bounded model?

choices.label: Momentum rises, so the wavelength gets shorter and the loop fit count increases.
Nothing changes because only speed matters for wavelength.
The wavelength gets longer because the heavier particle needs more room.

items.explanation: Mass enters through momentum too. At the same speed, a heavier particle has larger \(p\), so the de Broglie wavelength is shorter.
items.observationHint: Watch the momentum readout change first, then read the shorter wavelength and higher loop count.

items.prompt: From Near one-fit electron, double the mass and halve the speed so the momentum stays about the same. What should happen to the wavelength?

choices.label: The wavelength should stay about the same because the momentum stayed about the same.
The wavelength should get shorter because the particle is heavier.
The wavelength should get longer because the particle is slower.

items.explanation: de Broglie wavelength depends on momentum, not on mass or speed separately. If \(mv\) stays the same, \(\lambda\) stays the same too.
items.observationHint: Use the momentum readout and the wavelength graph together. The point should stay near the same place on the curve.

targets.label: Keep massmultiple between 1 m_e and 1 m_e.
Keep speedmms between 2.1 Mm/s and 2.3 Mm/s.
Keep wavelength between 0.315 nm and 0.35 nm.
Keep fitcount between 0.95 and 1.05.
Keep massmultiple between 1.9 m_e and 2.1 m_e.
Keep speedmms between 2.1 Mm/s and 2.3 Mm/s.
Keep wavelength between 0.155 nm and 0.175 nm.
Keep fitcount between 1.9 and 2.1.

items.title: Light as an Electromagnetic Wave
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

paragraphs: The probe-field graph shows electric and magnetic values at one downstream point so their matched oscillation stays visible. The source-probe graph compares the same electric field at the source and at the downstream probe.
The display-space graph keeps a compact electric-and-magnetic wave sketch visible while the spectrum rail above carries the true band ordering.

controls.description: Scales the displayed E and B height.
Moves from gamma rays to radio on a logarithmic wavelength axis.
Raises or lowers the refractive index.
Places the probe a chosen number of in-medium wavelengths from the source.

presets.label: Green in air
presets.description: A visible-green baseline.

presets.label: Violet in air
presets.description: Shift to the short-wavelength edge of visible light.

presets.label: Red in air
presets.description: Shift to the long-wavelength edge of visible light.

presets.label: Green in glass
presets.description: Keep the same visible label but raise n.

presets.label: Infrared
presets.description: Move just beyond visible red.

presets.label: Microwave
presets.description: Jump to a much longer wavelength.

presets.label: X-ray
presets.description: Jump far beyond visible violet.

overlays.label: Visible window
overlays.shortDescription: Frames the tiny visible slice inside the full electromagnetic spectrum.
whatToNotice: Most of the spectrum rail lies outside visible light.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps visible-color intuition connected to the full electromagnetic family.

overlays.label: Medium link
overlays.shortDescription: Shows how the same light keeps its frequency but gets a shorter wavelength in a denser medium.
whatToNotice: Changing n changes speed and in-medium wavelength together while the frequency and spectrum label stay fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: This is the clean bridge from spectrum language to refractive-index optics.

overlays.shortDescription: Marks the probe in units of the current in-medium wavelength and converts that spacing into travel delay.
whatToNotice: The source and probe show the same wave at two positions, separated by travel time through the medium.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates propagation delay from the false idea that E and B are out of step.

overlays.label: Field triad
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the electric direction, magnetic direction, and propagation direction together.
whatToNotice: Changing band or medium does not stop the wave from being electromagnetic.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the field story visible while the page introduces color and optics.

graphs.label: Probe field pair
graphs.description: Shows electric and magnetic values at the same probe point so their synchronized oscillation stays visible.

graphs.label: Source and probe electric field
graphs.description: Shows the source electric field and the downstream probe field on the same time axis, so the probe shift is read as travel delay through space.

graphs.label: Display-space field sketch
graphs.description: Keeps one compact electromagnetic-wave sketch visible while the rail above carries the true spectrum ordering.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Each one links the spectrum rail, the wave sketch, and the readout from the same live state.

items.text: Visible light is only a narrow strip on the full spectrum rail. Moving a little within that strip changes wavelength and frequency, but most of the rail lies outside human vision.
items.tryThis: Jump from green light to the microwave preset, then back to violet.
items.whyItMatters: It places color inside the much larger electromagnetic spectrum.

items.text: Raising the medium index shortens the in-medium wavelength and lowers the speed, but the source frequency and visible label stay the same.
items.tryThis: Keep the marker on green, then raise n and compare the medium link with the readout card.
items.whyItMatters: It is the key step that later makes refraction make sense.

items.text: Leaving the visible window removes the color cue, but the wavelength, frequency, electric field, and magnetic field still describe the same kind of electromagnetic wave.
items.tryThis: Stay on X-ray or ultraviolet and notice that the field pair does not vanish.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents light from being treated as only what the eye can see.

items.text: Compare mode works best when one setup changes source wavelength and the other changes only the medium. Then you can separate spectrum changes from medium changes.
items.tryThis: Keep Setup A on green in air, switch Setup B to green in glass, then compare that with red in air.
items.whyItMatters: It stops color-linked differences and refractive-index differences from blending into one idea.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the spectrum or medium consequence before you apply it. Change one cause at a time.

items.prompt: Start from green light in air and shift toward violet while leaving the medium alone. What should change together?

choices.label: The wavelength gets shorter, the frequency rises, and the visible cue moves toward violet.
Only the field amplitude changes because the band is still visible light.
The frequency stays the same because all visible light is one band.

items.explanation: Inside the visible window, moving toward violet means shorter vacuum wavelength and higher frequency.
items.observationHint: Watch the marker shift within the visible strip while the frequency label rises.

items.prompt: Keep the source on green light but raise the medium index from air to glass. Which bundle of changes should happen?

choices.label: Speed and in-medium wavelength drop, but frequency and visible label stay the same.
The visible color shifts toward blue because the wavelength gets shorter in the medium.
The frequency drops because the light now travels more slowly.

items.explanation: A larger refractive index lowers the speed and shortens the in-medium wavelength, but the source still launches the same oscillation rate, so the visible label stays green.
items.observationHint: Compare the medium link with the unchanged frequency and color label.

items.prompt: Move the marker from visible green into infrared. What should the page tell you next?

choices.label: The wavelength gets longer, the frequency drops, and the visible-color cue disappears.
The page still calls it green because infrared sits next to red.
The magnetic field vanishes because infrared is not electromagnetic light.

items.explanation: Infrared lies beyond visible red at longer wavelength and lower frequency. The color cue disappears, but the electromagnetic-wave sketch remains valid.
items.observationHint: Watch the marker leave the visible window while the field sketch remains.

items.title: Optical Resolution / Imaging Limits
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

keyIdeas: A finite aperture turns each object point into a diffraction blur, not a perfect point.
A larger aperture or a shorter wavelength shrinks that blur, while a smaller aperture or a longer wavelength broadens it.
Two points are easier to resolve when their detector spacing is large compared with the blur width.
The Rayleigh limit gives a practical scale for when two equal point sources are only just distinguishable.

controls.description: Longer wavelength broadens each point-spread blur.
A larger clear aperture narrows each diffraction blur and improves resolution.
Sets the angular gap between the object points. Smaller gaps are harder to preserve when the blur width is fixed.
Moves the live readout along the detector profile without changing the optical image itself.

presets.label: Blurred pair
presets.description: The diffraction blur is still wider than the point spacing, so the detector profile is mostly merged.

presets.label: Near threshold
presets.description: The spacing sits close to the Rayleigh limit, so the split is only just beginning to appear.

presets.label: Wide aperture split
presets.description: A larger aperture makes the two peaks clearly distinct.

presets.label: Long-wave blur
presets.description: A longer wavelength broadens the blur enough to erase an otherwise visible split.

presets.label: Off-center sample
presets.description: The sample point is moved onto one side of the detector profile instead of staying at the middle dip.

overlays.label: Aperture guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the clear aperture diameter directly on the lens.
whatToNotice: Changing D changes the diffraction width itself, not just the brightness of the image.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps resolving power tied to aperture geometry instead of making blur look like a mysterious detector effect.

overlays.label: Rayleigh guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the current image-point centers and the first-minimum blur scale around them.
whatToNotice: At about the Rayleigh limit, one image-point center sits close to the first-minimum scale of the other blur pattern.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the Rayleigh criterion into a visible comparison on the detector.

overlays.label: Component centers
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the centers of the two individual point-spread patterns.
whatToNotice: Whether the pair is resolved depends on center spacing compared with blur width, not on focus alone.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the merged-versus-separated transition visible as overlap between two real blur patterns.

graphs.label: Image-plane profile
graphs.description: Shows the combined detector exposure together with the two individual point-spread profiles, so you can see whether their overlap still leaves a central dip.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time and keep the aperture, detector strip, and graph tied to the same diffraction-limited image.

items.text: Notice that widening the aperture does not move the point centers. It narrows each diffraction blur, so the gap between the peaks is easier to preserve.
items.tryThis: Start from Blurred pair, then widen the aperture before touching the point separation.
items.whyItMatters: Resolution improves because the blur shrinks, not because the object points move farther apart.

items.text: Notice that increasing the wavelength broadens each blur even though the lens, detector, and point spacing stay fixed.
items.tryThis: Compare Near threshold with Long-wave blur, then watch how the middle dip fills in.
items.whyItMatters: It turns lambda over D into a visible imaging limit instead of a formula to memorize.

items.text: Notice that reducing point separation does not change the blur width. It only pushes the same two blur centers closer together.
items.tryThis: Lower the point separation while leaving aperture and wavelength fixed, then compare how the two dashed component centers crowd together.
items.whyItMatters: It cleanly separates object spacing from optical blur width.

items.text: Notice that the graph is detector position versus exposure, not a time trace. Hovering it moves the sample along the current image plane.
items.tryThis: Hover the profile near the center dip, then near one peak, and compare the live detector sample readout.
items.whyItMatters: Resolution lives in the spatial profile across the detector, not in a later-versus-earlier time story.

items.text: Compare mode works best when one setup changes aperture and the other changes wavelength. Then you can see two different ways to change the same diffraction width.
items.tryThis: Clone Near threshold, widen the aperture in one row, and lengthen the wavelength in the other row before reading the profile.
items.whyItMatters: It stops imaging limits from collapsing into one vague blur slider.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict whether the image should split more clearly or merge more strongly before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time.

items.prompt: Keep the wavelength and point separation fixed, then widen the aperture. What should happen to the detector profile?

choices.label: The blur width should shrink, so the pair becomes easier to separate.
The points should move farther apart on the detector even though the object spacing stayed fixed.
Only the detector sample value should change. The full profile should stay the same.

items.explanation: A larger aperture lowers the Rayleigh limit and narrows each diffraction blur. The point spacing itself does not change.
items.observationHint: Watch the Rayleigh guide tighten while the two peaks separate more cleanly.

items.prompt: Keep the aperture and point separation fixed, then increase the wavelength. What should happen?

choices.label: The blur should broaden, so the middle dip fills in and the pair becomes harder to distinguish.
The blur should shrink because longer waves stay straighter through the lens.
The graph should shift left or right, but the peak overlap should stay unchanged.

items.explanation: A longer wavelength raises the Rayleigh limit and broadens each diffraction blur, so nearby points merge more easily.
items.observationHint: The point centers stay where they are, but the middle dip fills in as the blurs widen.

items.prompt: Leave the optics alone, but move the two point sources closer together. What should the detector do?

choices.label: The two blur patterns should overlap more, so the central dip fills in toward one broad hump.
The blur width should shrink because the pair is closer together.
Nothing important should change because aperture and wavelength stayed fixed.

items.explanation: Changing the point separation moves the blur centers closer together or farther apart. The blur width itself stays set by wavelength and aperture.
items.observationHint: Watch the two centers crowd together while the same blur size tries to preserve a smaller gap.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the live detector profile to hit resolution targets. The task is to change the optics or the point spacing, not to game the detector sample.

items.title: Hit the Rayleigh threshold
items.prompt: Starting from Blurred pair, tune the aperture or wavelength until the point spacing sits right on the Rayleigh limit.
items.successMessage: You matched the threshold honestly: the object spacing did not move, but the diffraction scale now lines up with it.

setup.note: Use the Rayleigh guide and the profile dip together.

targets.label: Bring Delta theta / theta_R between 0.97 and 1.03.
Keep the center-to-peak ratio between 0.7 and 0.9.

items.title: Open a clear central dip
items.prompt: Starting from Near threshold, make the split clearly visible without changing the detector sample control.
items.successMessage: You created a clearly resolved pair by changing the optics or the point spacing itself, not by sliding the detector sample to a friendlier position.

setup.note: The sample slider only reads the profile. Use the optics or the point spacing to change the actual split.

targets.label: Raise Delta theta / theta_R above 1.2.
Push the center-to-peak ratio below 0.8.

items.title: Temperature and Internal Energy
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.description: Changes how much sample is present while keeping the same bounded particle-box picture.
Raises or lowers the energy input rate without changing the sample amount.
Changes the starting average particle motion for the same sample.

presets.label: Small warm sample
presets.description: A smaller sample at a moderate temperature. It matches the large sample's temperature but not its total internal energy.

presets.label: Large warm sample
presets.description: The average motion matches the small sample, but the larger amount stores much more total internal energy.

presets.label: Warming toward a shelf
presets.description: A moderate sample that will heat up, pause near a phase-change shelf, and then continue warming after the shelf is filled.

presets.label: On the shelf
presets.description: Starts directly on the phase-change shelf so internal energy can keep rising while temperature stays nearly flat.

presets.label: After the shelf
presets.description: The same sample has already filled the shelf, so new heating once again raises temperature directly.

overlays.label: Motion vectors
overlays.shortDescription: Shows motion traces on representative particles so the average microscopic motion stays visible.
whatToNotice: Faster average motion means higher temperature, but it does not by itself tell you the total internal energy.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links temperature to visible particle motion instead of treating temperature as a disconnected meter.

overlays.label: Internal-energy split
overlays.shortDescription: Shows how the current internal energy is divided between thermal motion and the other microscopic store.
whatToNotice: During shelf heating, the total internal-energy bar can keep growing even when the temperature graph is flat.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you separate temperature from total internal energy on the same bench.

overlays.shortDescription: Shows the progress bar for the simplified phase-change shelf.
whatToNotice: If the shelf is only partly filled, the heater can keep adding energy while the temperature changes very little.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows why added energy does not always produce an immediate temperature rise.

overlays.label: Particle and temperature chips
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the live particle count, average kinetic energy per particle, and current temperature directly on the bench.
whatToNotice: Samples with the same temperature chip can still have different particle counts and different total internal energies.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the difference between amount and average motion visible before you read the graphs.

graphs.label: Temperature over time
graphs.description: Use this graph to follow how the sample warms over time. A flat section does not automatically mean energy has stopped entering the sample.
Compare total internal energy with its thermal-motion part and the other microscopic store. This is the clearest graph for seeing what happens on the shelf.

graphs.label: Internal energy vs particle count
graphs.description: This graph changes only particle count while the temperature-like scale stays fixed, so it cleanly shows how same-temperature samples can have different total internal energies.

graphs.label: Temperature rise rate vs particle count
graphs.description: This graph changes only particle count at the current heater setting. It shows why larger samples warm more slowly under the same energy input.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Each one links a control change to what you should see on the stage, overlays, and graphs.

items.text: If you change only particle count, the temperature chip can stay nearly the same while the internal-energy bar grows a lot. That is the clearest sign that temperature and total internal energy are different quantities.
items.tryThis: Switch between the small-warm-sample and large-warm-sample presets. Compare the temperature chip, motion cues, internal-energy bar, and amount scan.
items.whyItMatters: You need this distinction before ideas like heat transfer, specific heat, and phase change can make sense.

items.text: At fixed heater power, the temperature-rise graph falls as particle count increases because the same input energy is shared across more particles.
items.tryThis: Hover a point at small particle count and one at large particle count, then compare the preview values with the particle box.
items.whyItMatters: It shows why warming rate depends on amount, not just on heater power.

items.text: A flat temperature graph does not mean the heater turned off. If the shelf is still filling, internal energy is still increasing even while temperature stays nearly constant.
items.tryThis: Use the on-the-shelf preset, scrub through the flat part of the temperature graph, and compare it with the energy-breakdown graph and the shelf-progress bar.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents the common mistake that flat temperature means no energy is entering the sample.

items.text: Raising starting temperature makes the motion cues more active immediately, but total internal energy still depends on particle count and the other store.
items.tryThis: Raise only the starting temperature and compare how the motion vectors, temperature chip, and internal-energy bar move together but not by the same amount.
items.whyItMatters: It blocks the shortcut that hotter must always mean more total energy in every comparison.
It lets you test one causal change at a time without losing the live particle picture.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict before you apply the change. Each scenario checks whether you can keep average motion, total internal energy, and shelf behavior separate.

items.prompt: From the small warm sample, you increase only particle count and keep the starting temperature the same. Which statement best matches the bench?

choices.label: Temperature can stay about the same, but total internal energy rises and the future warming rate becomes smaller.
Temperature must rise immediately because the larger sample automatically contains more internal energy.
Both temperature and internal energy must stay exactly the same because the heater was not changed.

items.explanation: Temperature follows the average per-particle motion, while internal energy counts the whole sample. More particles at the same temperature give a larger total internal energy, and the same heater would warm that larger sample more slowly.
items.observationHint: Look for a similar temperature chip but a taller internal-energy bar and a lower temperature-rise graph.

items.prompt: A sample is already on the phase shelf and the heater stays on. What should happen before the shelf is finished filling?

choices.label: Internal energy keeps increasing while temperature stays nearly flat because the input is filling the shelf.
Temperature must rise immediately because any added energy always means faster particle motion.
Nothing changes because a flat temperature line means the heater is no longer adding energy.

items.explanation: During the shelf, the heater still adds energy. That energy goes into the other microscopic store, so temperature does not need to rise at the same moment.
items.observationHint: The temperature graph stays nearly flat while the internal-energy curve and shelf bar keep moving.

items.prompt: Two samples start at the same temperature and get the same heater power, but one has far fewer particles. Which sample warms faster before any shelf behavior matters?

choices.label: The smaller sample warms faster because the same input energy is shared across fewer particles.
Both samples warm at the same rate because identical heater power always means identical temperature rise.
The larger sample warms faster because it already contains more internal energy.

items.explanation: Heater power sets the input rate, but the temperature response depends on how many particles have to share that incoming energy.
items.observationHint: The amount-heating-rate graph is highest at small particle counts.

items.title: Beats
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Scales the source motion and the maximum possible envelope without changing the beat frequency.
Sets the first source timing so you can tune the carrier and the beat difference.
Sets the second source timing. Matching it to Source A removes beats, while widening the split speeds the envelope.

presets.label: Near unison
presets.description: Two close frequencies create a slow, obvious envelope.

presets.label: Slow pulses
presets.description: A small frequency split makes the loud-soft cycle easy to track.

presets.label: No beats
presets.description: Equal frequencies remove the separate envelope pulse.

presets.label: Same beat, higher carrier
presets.description: The source pair keeps a 0.3 Hz split while the average frequency is higher.

presets.label: Same beat, lower carrier
presets.description: The source pair keeps the same 0.3 Hz split but lowers the average frequency.

presets.label: Wide split
presets.description: A larger difference speeds the envelope enough that the simple one-beat listening picture starts to feel crowded.

overlays.label: Envelope guide
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the slow upper and lower bounds that wrap the faster resultant trace.
whatToNotice: The fast oscillation keeps running inside the slow envelope instead of turning into a separate third wave.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the carrier and the beat envelope visible as two roles inside one honest superposition.

overlays.label: Listener cue
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the bounded loudness cue that rises and falls with the envelope, not with either source alone.
whatToNotice: The loud-soft pulse changes even though both source amplitudes stay fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the listening idea to superposition without pretending the page is a full audio analyzer.

overlays.label: Frequency-difference callout
overlays.shortDescription: Keeps $|f_2-f_1|$ and the beat rate visible while you retune the two sources.
whatToNotice: A larger frequency split speeds the envelope even if the average carrier stays similar.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps beat frequency tied to the actual source difference instead of to the average pitch cue.

graphs.label: Source and resultant motion
graphs.description: Shows the two source traces and their sum so you can see the fast carrier inside the slower beat envelope.

graphs.label: Envelope and loudness cue
graphs.description: Shows the slow envelope ratio and the bounded loudness cue, so beat rate can be separated from the faster carrier.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time. The goal is to separate the fast carrier, the slow envelope, and the loudness cue on the same superposition bench.

items.text: Notice that the rapid oscillation never disappears. Beats are the slow growth and fade of the envelope wrapped around that faster carrier.
items.tryThis: Start from Near unison and watch the resultant trace before you look at the envelope graph.
items.whyItMatters: It prevents the loud-soft pulse from being mistaken for a separate low-frequency source.

items.text: Notice that matching the two source frequencies removes the separate beat envelope. The resultant can still oscillate strongly, but it no longer swells and fades.
items.tryThis: Move from Near unison to No beats and watch the envelope graph flatten into a steady value.
items.whyItMatters: Beat frequency comes from the difference, so making that difference zero removes the beat cycle.

items.text: Notice that increasing $|f_2-f_1|$ speeds up the envelope directly. The carrier can stay in a similar range while the beat rate changes a lot.
items.tryThis: Open the envelope graph, widen the split, and compare how quickly the loudness cue rises and falls.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps the pulse rate tied to the actual frequency difference.

items.text: Notice which setup keeps the same beat rate but changes the faster carrier. Matching the frequency difference does that even when the average frequency changes.
items.tryThis: Use compare mode to put Same beat, higher carrier beside Same beat, lower carrier and read the displacement rows together.
items.whyItMatters: Compare mode makes it easier to separate beat rate from the average pitch or carrier cue.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict which part of the motion changes: the fast carrier, the slow envelope, or both.

items.prompt: The two sources are close but not equal. If you tune them to exactly the same frequency while keeping amplitude fixed, what should happen?

choices.label: The separate beat envelope should disappear, leaving one steady resultant frequency.
The beats should get slower and slower but never fully disappear.
The loudness cue should vanish completely because matching frequencies always cancels the sound.

items.explanation: When $f_1=f_2$, the frequency difference is zero, so there is no separate beat envelope. The two sources reinforce as one steady-frequency resultant instead of pulsing in and out.
items.observationHint: Watch the envelope graph stop pulsing when the two source frequencies match.

items.prompt: Keep the amplitudes fixed but move the two frequencies farther apart. What should happen to the slow loud-soft cycle?

choices.label: The beat envelope should pulse faster because the frequency difference is larger.
The beat envelope should stay the same because amplitude did not change.
The beat envelope should slow down because each source now has more energy.

items.explanation: Beat frequency follows $|f_2-f_1|$, so widening the split speeds the envelope directly even when amplitude stays fixed.
items.observationHint: Compare the slower pulses near unison with the faster pulsing in the wider-split preset.

items.prompt: Case A uses $1.3\,\mathrm{Hz}$ and $1.6\,\mathrm{Hz}$. Case B lowers both to $0.7\,\mathrm{Hz}$ and $1.0\,\mathrm{Hz}$. What should change?

choices.label: The beat rate stays the same, but the faster carrier drops because the average frequency is lower.
The beat rate drops because both individual frequencies are lower.
Both the beat rate and the carrier stay the same because the frequency split is the same.

items.explanation: Both cases keep the same $0.3\,\mathrm{Hz}$ difference, so the beat rate stays the same. Lowering both frequencies together lowers the average carrier without changing the envelope rate.
items.observationHint: Use compare mode later to confirm that matching $\Delta f$ keeps the pulse rate while the inner wiggle slows down.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the same compact bench to separate beat rate from carrier frequency and from source amplitude.

items.title: Wave Speed and Wavelength
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

paragraphs: Start with the Wavelength guide on the stage and the source and probe graph below it. The guide marks one crest-to-crest spacing in space, while the graph shows how the source point and one downstream probe repeat in time. These are two views of the same wave: the probe is showing the same oscillation later, after the disturbance has had time to travel through the medium.

Now change Wave speed while keeping Wavelength fixed. The crest pattern crosses the ruler faster, the source and probe traces complete each cycle in less time, and the Delay guide shrinks because the same distance is covered more quickly. Instead, keep Wave speed fixed and increase Wavelength. The crests spread farther apart, fewer cycles fit into each meter, and the source frequency falls.

The phase-map graph turns that timing idea into a distance picture. Every extra wavelength of downstream distance adds one full cycle of lag, so a probe one wavelength farther away returns to the same phase, just one period later. Moving the probe does not change the wave being launched. It only changes the delay and phase difference between the source and that location.

That is why v = f lambda is so useful: spacing, timing, and travel must all agree. The same relation later helps explain sound arrival delays, interference, and standing waves. Next, keep the probe fixed and compare a pure Wave speed change with a pure Wavelength change, then move the probe by one full wavelength and check that the phase repeats while the delay increases.

controls.description: Changes the wave height without changing v, lambda, f, or T.
Controls how quickly the crest train moves through the medium.
Controls the crest spacing along the medium.
Moves the live measurement point farther from or closer to the source.

presets.label: Faster medium
presets.description: The crests travel faster while the wavelength stays fixed, so the source frequency rises.

presets.label: Longer wavelength
presets.description: The same medium speed now spreads the crests farther apart, so the frequency drops.

presets.label: Shorter wavelength
presets.description: More cycles fit into the same medium length, so the source must oscillate faster.

presets.label: One wavelength away
presets.description: The probe sits exactly one wavelength from the source, so it is delayed by one full cycle.

overlays.label: Wavelength guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks one crest-to-crest spacing directly on the live wave.
whatToNotice: Changing lambda changes the crest spacing on the stage and the slope of the phase map at the same time.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps wavelength visible as a distance on the wave, not just a symbol in a formula.

overlays.label: Delay guide
overlays.shortDescription: Labels the source-to-probe distance, travel delay, and cycle lag together.
whatToNotice: Moving the probe does not reshape the wave train. It only changes how long that point waits and how far it lags behind the source.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links downstream distance to both travel time and phase lag on the same live view.

overlays.label: One-period travel
overlays.shortDescription: Shows that one period of source motion sends a crest forward by one wavelength.
whatToNotice: The distance a crest travels in one period matches the wavelength spacing marked on the stage.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the link between time and distance visible instead of leaving it hidden in algebra.

graphs.label: Source and probe motion
graphs.description: Shows source motion and probe motion on the same time axis, so you can read travel delay and phase difference directly from the live wave.

graphs.label: Position to cycle lag
graphs.description: Shows how cycle lag grows with downstream distance. Every extra wavelength adds one more cycle.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time and match it to something you can actually see on the stage or graphs.

items.text: Notice that the graph and the stage tell the same story. When the crest pattern moves faster or the spacing changes, the timing at the source and probe changes with it.
items.tryThis: Keep the probe fixed, then change wave speed or wavelength one at a time and compare the stage, time graph, and readout card.
items.whyItMatters: The graph is the same traveling wave measured at two positions, not a separate math display.

items.text: Notice that hovering the phase map moves the live probe to that downstream position. The graph is pointing to the same wave, not a separate preview.
items.tryThis: Hover one wavelength farther to the right and compare how the probe phase relation changes.
items.whyItMatters: The phase map and the stage are two views of the same distance-based lag.

items.text: Notice that shortening lambda packs more cycles into the same distance. The phase map gets steeper, and if v stays fixed the source frequency must increase.
items.tryThis: Lower lambda while keeping v fixed, then compare the crest spacing on the stage with the frequency readout.
items.whyItMatters: Wavelength is a spatial quantity, but through v = f lambda it also sets the timing.

items.text: Notice that increasing v while lambda stays fixed makes the time graph cycle faster even though the crest spacing on the stage does not change.
items.tryThis: Keep lambda fixed, raise the wave speed, and compare the period readout before and after.
items.whyItMatters: Changing wave speed moves the pattern through the medium faster, which raises frequency when the spacing is held fixed.

items.text: Notice that a taller wave does not change the crest spacing, travel delay, frequency, or period. It only changes the displacement height.
items.tryThis: Increase amplitude and compare what changed on the time graph with what stayed fixed on the phase map and readout card.
items.whyItMatters: Amplitude is often confused with speed or energy. Here it is visually separated from the timing and spacing relations.

items.text: Notice that the time rail changes the instantaneous wave shape and the source-probe displacements, but the phase map stays the same because it depends on position, not on which instant you paused.
items.tryThis: Pause, step once, and compare what changed on the stage with what stayed fixed on the phase map.
items.whyItMatters: It separates a snapshot in time from the distance-based lag relation.

items.text: Notice which setup packs more cycles into the same distance and which setup cycles faster in time. Those are different questions.
items.tryThis: Clone the current state, then shorten lambda in one setup and raise v in the other before comparing the two graphs.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the effect before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time so you can connect the stage, graph, and equation.

items.prompt: Keep lambda fixed at the current value and make the wave move faster through the medium. What must happen to the source frequency and period?

choices.label: The frequency should increase and the period should get shorter.
The frequency should stay the same because the crest spacing did not change.
The period should get longer because the wave is moving farther each second.

items.explanation: With lambda fixed, v = f lambda means a larger wave speed requires a larger frequency. Since T = 1 / f, the period must become shorter.
items.observationHint: Watch the crest pattern move faster while both time traces complete each cycle sooner.

items.prompt: Keep the wave speed fixed and increase lambda. What should happen to the phase-map slope and the source frequency?

choices.label: The phase-map slope should flatten and the frequency should decrease.
The phase-map slope should get steeper and the frequency should increase.
Neither graph nor frequency should change because the medium speed stayed fixed.

items.explanation: A larger wavelength means fewer cycles fit into each meter, so x / lambda grows more slowly with position. With v fixed, f = v / lambda, so frequency decreases.
items.observationHint: Compare the wider crest spacing with the flatter phase map and slower time graph.

items.prompt: Move the probe one full wavelength farther from the source without changing the wave itself. What should happen to the source-probe phase relation?
items.scenarioLabel: Shift the probe by one lambda

choices.label: The probe should keep the same phase relation, but arrive one full cycle later.
The probe should flip to the opposite phase because the distance got larger.
The probe should gain amplitude because it is farther from the source.

items.explanation: Adding one full wavelength adds one full cycle of lag. The probe therefore reaches the same phase again, but it does so one period later.
items.observationHint: On the phase map, move one wavelength to the right and watch the source and probe line up in phase again.

items.title: Doppler Effect
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

paragraphs: The first graph compares the emitted source signal with the observer signal over time.

The second graph shows how source speed changes front and rear spacing, and the third graph shows how observer speed changes heard frequency on the chosen side relative to the emitted-frequency baseline.

controls.description: Sets how often the source emits new wavefronts.
Moves the source through the medium, which compresses spacing ahead and stretches spacing behind.
Positive values move the observer toward the source on the chosen side; negative values move away and change only the arrival rate.

controls.label: Listen ahead
controls.description: Choose whether the observer listens on the compressed front side or the stretched rear side of the moving source.

presets.label: Steady siren
presets.description: A stationary source gives the same spacing on both sides.

presets.label: City pass
presets.description: A forward-moving source compresses the front spacing while leaving the observer stationary.

presets.label: Observer moves in
presets.description: The observer moves toward the source on the front side to further raise the heard pitch.

presets.label: Behind the source
presets.description: The observer listens on the stretched rear side of the moving source.

presets.label: Observer moves away
presets.description: The observer backs away on the front side, reducing the arrival rate even though the source still moves forward.

overlays.label: Motion vectors
overlays.shortDescription: Marks source and observer motion on the same pass-by scene.
whatToNotice: Source motion changes the spacing laid down in the medium, while observer motion changes how quickly those wavefronts are encountered.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps two different Doppler causes from being blended into one.

overlays.label: Front and rear spacing
overlays.shortDescription: Shows one spacing ahead of the source and one behind it at the same moment.
whatToNotice: As soon as the source moves forward, spacing ahead becomes smaller and spacing behind becomes larger.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the pitch shift to a visible change in the medium rather than to the source somehow emitting faster.

overlays.label: Arrival timing
overlays.shortDescription: Labels the current arrival rate on the observer's chosen side.
whatToNotice: Changing observer speed changes the heard arrival rate even when the chosen-side spacing stays the same.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates spacing in the medium from the rate at which the observer encounters those wavefronts.

graphs.label: Source and observer signals
graphs.description: Compares the emitted signal with the received signal, so you can see that the source keeps one emitted frequency while the observer receives a different arrival rate.

graphs.label: Source speed and wavefront spacing
graphs.description: Shows how source speed changes the spacing in the medium ahead of the source and behind it while the emitted frequency stays fixed.

graphs.label: Observer motion and heard frequency
graphs.description: Shows how observer motion changes heard frequency on the chosen side relative to the emitted-frequency baseline.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Change one cause at a time and link it to something visible on the pass-by bench.

items.text: Notice that faster source motion compresses the front markers and stretches the rear markers at the same time. The source is not emitting faster; it is moving between emissions.
items.tryThis: Start from Steady siren, then move to City pass without changing the source frequency.
items.whyItMatters: The Doppler shift begins with a spacing change laid down in the medium.

items.text: Notice that changing observer speed moves the heard-frequency trace even though the front and rear spacing markers do not move. The observer is changing the encounter rate, not the medium.
items.tryThis: Keep the source settings fixed and sweep the observer speed through zero while watching the observer-response graph.
items.whyItMatters: It separates source-created spacing changes from observer-created arrival-rate changes.

items.text: Notice that switching from ahead to behind changes which spacing the observer samples. Ahead is compressed and sounds higher; behind is stretched and sounds lower.
items.tryThis: Keep the same source speed and observer speed, then toggle between listening ahead and listening behind.
items.whyItMatters: The pitch shift depends on which side of the moving source the observer listens from.

items.tryThis: Clone City pass, then make one setup trail behind and the other move toward the source on the front side.
items.whyItMatters: The compare surface shows that two different motion stories can produce different pitch shifts on the same classical bench.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the pitch and spacing change before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time so you can separate emitted frequency, spacing in the medium, and arrival rate.

items.prompt: The source keeps the same emitted frequency, but its forward speed increases while the observer stays ahead and stationary. What should happen?

choices.label: The front spacing shrinks and the observer hears a higher pitch.
The emitted frequency rises, so the source itself changes its tone.
Only the rear spacing changes because the observer is in front.

items.explanation: A faster source does not change its emitted frequency here. It emits each crest from a more forward position, which compresses the front spacing and raises the arrival rate for an observer ahead.
items.observationHint: Watch the front markers pack together and the observer trace cycle faster.

items.prompt: The source settings stay fixed, but the observer moves toward the source on the chosen side. What should happen?

choices.label: The heard pitch rises, but the spacing already in the medium does not change.
The front and rear spacing both change because the observer is moving.
Nothing changes because only the source can produce Doppler shifts.

items.explanation: Observer motion changes how quickly the observer meets wavefronts on the chosen side. It does not redraw the spacing already laid down by the source.
items.observationHint: Keep the spacing markers fixed and watch only the observer-response graph move.

items.prompt: The source keeps moving forward, but the observer switches from listening ahead to listening behind. What should happen to the heard pitch?

choices.label: The heard pitch drops because the observer now samples the stretched rear spacing.
The heard pitch stays the same because the source frequency did not change.
The heard pitch rises because the observer is farther from the source.

items.explanation: Behind the source, the observer samples stretched rear spacing, so fewer crests arrive each second and the heard pitch drops.
items.observationHint: Toggle to the rear side and compare the source-spacing graph with the observer trace.

items.title: Exponential Change / Growth, Decay, and Logarithms
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

correction: A negative rate gives honest exponential decay, so the same model can fall toward zero instead of rising away from the start.
A logarithm appears naturally when you solve y_0 e^{kt} = T for time.
So the logarithm is not a disconnected new object here. It is the inverse question for the same exponential curve.

controls.description: Set the starting amount y_0 at time 0.
Use positive values for growth and negative values for decay.
Choose the amount the curve is trying to reach.

presets.label: Growth to target
presets.description: A growth case where the target sits above the start and the crossing happens inside the visible window.

presets.label: Decay half-life
presets.description: A decay case where the target sits below the start and the half-life cue is immediately useful.

presets.label: Slow growth
presets.description: A smaller positive rate makes the curve rise more slowly, so the same kind of target takes longer to reach.

presets.label: Unreachable target
presets.description: A decay case with a target above the start, so the curve moves away from the target instead of reaching it.

overlays.label: Target marker
overlays.shortDescription: Show the target line and the crossing point when the curve can actually reach it.
whatToNotice: The hit point only appears when the target lies on the side of the start that matches growth or decay.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the inverse-time question to one visible crossing on the curve.

overlays.label: Opposite-rate comparison
overlays.shortDescription: Show the same starting value with the opposite sign of the rate.
whatToNotice: Keeping the same start but flipping the sign of k turns growth into decay, or decay into growth.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes growth and decay two versions of the same model instead of two separate topics.

overlays.label: Doubling or half-life guide
overlays.shortDescription: Mark one doubling time or one half-life on the curve when that guide exists.
whatToNotice: One fixed time step always doubles the amount in growth or halves it in decay.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes multiplicative cadence visible instead of leaving doubling time and half-life as slogans.

overlays.label: Log guide
overlays.shortDescription: Show the target level in the straightened log view.
whatToNotice: The straight line hits the target-log level at exactly the same time the curved graph hits the target.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows why solving for time becomes a logarithm.

graphs.label: Amount vs time
graphs.description: Shows the current exponential curve, the opposite-rate comparison, and the target crossing on the same amount-versus-time graph.

graphs.label: Log target view
graphs.description: Shows that ln(amount / initial) changes linearly in time, so the target-time question becomes one line-crossing problem.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to keep the curve, the target line, and the inverse-time readout on the same story.

items.text: Flip the sign of k while keeping the same start. One curve rises and the other falls, so the same target can become reachable or unreachable.
In the log view, the curved target question becomes a straight-line crossing because ln(y / y_0) = kt.
A fixed positive k gives one doubling cadence. A fixed negative k gives one half-life cadence.
Try the unreachable-target preset and check which side of the start the target lies on before you look for a hit time.
items.tryThis: Apply the unreachable-target preset, then compare the target line with the direction of the decay curve before reading the inverse-time card.

predictionMode.title: Predict the target behavior
predictionMode.intro: Commit to what the curve and the hit time should do before you change the rate or the target.

items.prompt: If the rate changes from positive to negative while the start and target stay fixed above the start value, what should happen first?
items.scenarioLabel: Flip the sign of the rate

choices.label: The curve turns into decay and the higher target is no longer reached.
The curve keeps growing, but the logarithm disappears.
Only the target line moves while the curve stays the same.

items.explanation: Changing only the sign of k flips growth into decay, so a target above the start stops being reachable on this bench.
items.observationHint: Watch the target marker and the opposite-rate comparison together.

items.prompt: In a growth case, if the target is pushed farther above the start while the rate stays fixed, what happens to the target time?

choices.label: The target time increases because the same rate has farther to go multiplicatively.
The target time decreases because the target line is higher.
The target time stays exactly the same because k did not change.

items.explanation: With the same positive rate, a larger target ratio means a larger logarithm and therefore a larger hit time.
items.observationHint: Compare the target-log line before and after the target changes.

items.prompt: If the positive rate doubles while the start and target stay fixed, what should happen to the doubling time?

choices.label: The doubling time becomes shorter.
The doubling time becomes longer.
The doubling time stays fixed because doubling only depends on the start value.

items.explanation: Doubling time is ln 2 / k, so a larger positive k makes that fixed time shorter.
items.observationHint: Watch the doubling-time guide move left as the rate increases.

challengeMode.title: Read a quarter-target as two half-lives
challengeMode.intro: Use the curve, the target line, and the half-life guide together. The goal is to see a quarter-target as two multiplicative steps, not just as a number in a formula.

items.title: Make the target one quarter of the start
items.prompt: Build a decay case where the target is about one quarter of the start, so the curve reaches it in about two half-lives.
items.successMessage: You built a decay case where two half-life steps land on the target, so the ratio, the half-life guide, and the hit time all agree.

setup.note: Start from a simple decay case, then lower the target until it sits at one quarter of the start.

hints: A quarter of the start means two halvings, not one.
If the hit time is only one half-life, the target is still too high.

targets.label: Keep the start value near $8$, between $7.9$ and $8.1$.
Keep the continuous decay rate between $-0.24$ and $-0.22$.
Set the target near one quarter of the start, between $1.95$ and $2.05$.

items.title: Momentum and Impulse
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Change the cart's mass and compare how the same impulse changes velocity less or more.
Set the cart's starting direction and starting momentum before the pulse begins.
Set the height and sign of the pulse. Positive force pushes right; negative force pushes left.
Set how long the force stays on. A longer pulse can produce more impulse even if the force is smaller.

presets.label: Short hard push
presets.description: A tall, narrow pulse with the same impulse as the long gentle push.

presets.label: Long gentle push
presets.description: A lower, wider pulse with the same impulse as the short hard push.

presets.label: Heavy cart
presets.description: The same pulse on a heavier cart, so the momentum change is the same but the speed change is smaller.

presets.label: Braking pulse
presets.description: A leftward pulse that reduces the cart's rightward momentum and can even reverse it.

overlays.label: Force arrow
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the direction and size of the force while the pulse is active.
whatToNotice: The arrow matters only during the shaded pulse window. Before and after it, the cart coasts.
Reversing the arrow changes the sign of both the impulse and the momentum change.
overlays.whyItMatters: Impulse is signed, so direction matters as much as size.

overlays.label: Pulse window
overlays.shortDescription: Shades the time interval when the force is on.
whatToNotice: The shaded region marks time, not position on the track.
A wider pulse can give the same impulse as a taller one if the signed area matches.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you read impulse as force multiplied by time.

overlays.label: Momentum bars
overlays.shortDescription: Compares the initial, current, and final momentum on one signed scale.
whatToNotice: The current bar changes only while the force is on, then it stays fixed.
The gap from $p_i$ to $p_f$ matches the total accumulated impulse.
overlays.whyItMatters: The bars make $J = \Delta p$ visible on the same stage as the motion.

graphs.label: Force vs time
graphs.description: Shows the sign, height, and duration of the pulse, so the area under the graph represents impulse.

graphs.label: Momentum vs time
graphs.description: Shows momentum staying constant before and after the pulse and changing only while the force acts.

graphs.label: Impulse and change in momentum
graphs.description: Shows accumulated impulse alongside change in momentum so you can compare the two directly.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts while you change one control at a time. Match what happens on the track to the force, impulse, and momentum graphs.

items.text: Notice that the signed area of the force pulse is the same quantity as the momentum change.
items.tryThis: Watch the force pulse and accumulated-impulse graph together, then compare the final shift on the momentum graph.
items.whyItMatters: This is the impulse-momentum theorem in one connected visual loop.

items.text: Notice that momentum changes only while the force pulse is actually on.
items.tryThis: Pause before the pulse, during it, and after it. The momentum graph should be flat, changing, then flat again.
items.whyItMatters: It separates motion that continues from momentum that is currently changing.

items.text: Notice that a lower wider pulse can end at the same final momentum as a taller narrower pulse.
items.tryThis: Compare the short hard push with the long gentle push. The force graphs look different even when the final momentum matches.
items.whyItMatters: It breaks the mistake that only force size matters.

items.text: Notice that increasing pulse duration widens the force-time area even when the force height stays fixed.
items.tryThis: Lengthen the pulse without changing the force. The graph gets wider and the final momentum shifts farther.
items.whyItMatters: Impulse depends on time as directly as it depends on force.

items.text: Notice that mass changes the velocity response more than the momentum change for the same impulse.
items.tryThis: Keep the pulse the same and compare a light cart with a heavy cart. The momentum change matches, but the heavy cart's speed changes less.
items.whyItMatters: It helps separate $\Delta p$ from $\Delta v$, which students often merge.

items.text: Notice that the impulse graph stops rising exactly when the force pulse ends.
items.tryThis: Scrub near the end of the pulse and compare the time cursor with the shaded pulse window.
items.whyItMatters: Accumulated impulse is a running total, not a force that keeps acting.

predictionMode.title: Predict the pulse
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the pulse will do before you run it. Then check the stage against the force, impulse, and momentum graphs.

items.prompt: If you switch from the short hard push to the long gentle push on the same $1\,\mathrm{kg}$ cart, what should stay the same?

choices.label: The final momentum change stays the same, even though the force graph gets lower and wider.
The final momentum must get smaller because the peak force is smaller.
Nothing changes because force and time always cancel out completely.

items.explanation: Both presets have the same signed force-time area, so they give the same impulse and the same momentum change to the same cart, even though the pulse shapes differ.
items.observationHint: Compare the end of the impulse graph, the momentum graph, and the final momentum bar after the long gentle pulse.

items.prompt: If mass increases while the same short hard pulse acts, what should change on the stage and graphs?
items.scenarioLabel: Heavy cart with the same pulse

choices.label: The momentum change stays the same, but the cart's velocity changes less.
The impulse gets smaller automatically because the cart is heavier.
The force graph must get taller to keep the momentum graph honest.

items.explanation: The force-time area is unchanged, so the impulse and momentum change are unchanged. A heavier cart turns the same $\Delta p$ into a smaller $\Delta v$.
items.observationHint: Watch the momentum graph rise by the same amount while the cart's speed changes less.

items.title: Bernoulli's Principle
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Sets the static pressure at section A.
Changes how much fluid volume moves through the pipe each second.
Adjusts the wider entry section.
Adjusts the throat width. The throat can narrow, match, or widen relative to the entry section.
Raises or lowers the throat relative to section A.

presets.label: Level venturi
presets.description: Keep the same narrowing but remove the height term.

presets.label: Wide throat
presets.description: A less dramatic narrowing keeps the same entry state but recovers more throat pressure.

presets.label: Uniform pipe
presets.description: Matching areas keep the continuity speed contrast small.

presets.label: Raised throat
presets.description: Keep the narrow throat but lift it higher so the height term matters more.

presets.label: Gentle flow
presets.description: A slower stream softens the speed-driven pressure drop.

overlays.label: Continuity bridge
overlays.shortDescription: Marks sections A and B and keeps the same $Q$ attached to both.
whatToNotice: The same flow rate label stays attached to both sections even when their speeds differ.
overlays.whyItMatters: It reminds you that Bernoulli is responding to a continuity-defined speed change, not an arbitrary one.

overlays.label: Speed arrows
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the section A and throat speeds directly on the pipe.
whatToNotice: The narrower throat gets the longer speed arrow because the same $Q$ has to pass through less area.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows the continuity part of the story before you interpret the pressure change.

overlays.label: Pressure gauges
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the entry and throat static pressures side by side.
whatToNotice: The throat gauge can be lower because some of the same total is showing up as speed and height instead of static pressure.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the pressure trade visible on the same pipe.

overlays.label: Bernoulli budget bars
overlays.shortDescription: Shows how pressure, kinetic, and height terms share the same total at section A and throat B.
whatToNotice: The throat can gain kinetic and height share while losing static pressure share, with the total staying nearly the same.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows Bernoulli as a trade between terms, not as a memorized slogan.

overlays.label: Same-time slices
overlays.shortDescription: Shows equal-time fluid slices in section A and throat B.
whatToNotice: The same short time slice stretches farther in the faster throat while still representing the same flow rate.
overlays.whyItMatters: It reconnects the Bernoulli page to the earlier continuity picture.

graphs.label: Section speed vs throat area
graphs.description: Change throat area while keeping the entry state fixed. Read the continuity speed change first.

graphs.label: Pressure vs throat area
graphs.description: As the throat gets narrower, the throat pressure drops because more of the same total sits in the kinetic term there.

graphs.label: Pressure vs flow rate
graphs.description: Increase $Q$ with the same geometry. The faster throat usually means a larger pressure drop.

graphs.label: Pressure vs throat height
graphs.description: Raise the throat with the same speeds and areas. The throat pressure falls linearly because the height term grows with $\Delta y$.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Change one thing at a time so the arrows, gauges, energy bars, and graphs all tell the same pressure-speed-height story.

items.text: Make the throat smaller. The same $Q$ stays attached to both sections, the throat speed arrow grows, and the throat pressure gauge falls because more of the total is tied up in the kinetic term there.
Raise the throat while keeping the areas the same. The speed picture barely changes, but the throat gauge drops further because the height term now takes a larger share.
Open the pressure-flow-rate graph. As $Q$ rises, both sections move faster, but the faster throat loses more static pressure, so the pressure drop grows.

Use compare mode to keep the same entry pressure and flow rate, then widen only setup B's throat. Continuity explains why the throat speed drops in B, and Bernoulli explains why the throat pressure recovers there.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the pressure change before you test it. Keep continuity and Bernoulli in the same cause-and-effect chain.

choices.label: The throat speed rises and the throat static pressure drops.
The throat speed stays the same because the same fluid is moving.
The throat pressure rises because the fluid is squeezed.

items.explanation: Continuity makes the smaller throat faster. In Bernoulli, that larger speed leaves less of the same total as static pressure at the throat.
items.observationHint: The throat speed arrow lengthens while the throat pressure gauge and pressure-area graph move downward.

items.prompt: Starting from Level venturi, you raise the throat while keeping the same areas, entry pressure, and flow rate. What changes next?

choices.label: The throat pressure drops further even though the speed story barely changes.
The throat pressure rises because the throat is now higher.
Only the entry pressure changes.

items.explanation: With the same $Q$ and areas, continuity keeps the speeds almost unchanged. The higher throat still adds a larger $ho g y$ term, so the throat pressure drops.
items.observationHint: The pressure-height graph and energy bars show the height share growing while the speed arrows stay similar.

choices.label: The throat speed falls and the throat pressure recovers upward.
The throat speed rises because the pipe is larger.
The Bernoulli budget disappears because the geometry changed.

items.explanation: A wider throat lowers the throat speed for the same $Q$, so less of the total sits in the kinetic term and more can remain as static pressure.
items.observationHint: The throat speed arrow shortens and the throat pressure gauge rises.

items.title: Sound Waves and Longitudinal Motion
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

presets.label: Faster wave
presets.description: The disturbance moves faster while the parcel motion stays longitudinal, so the timing speeds up and the probe delay shortens.

presets.label: Shorter wavelength
presets.description: Compressions pack closer together, so the source frequency rises at the same wave speed.

presets.label: Strong compression
presets.description: The probe sits where neighboring parcels are tightly crowded at the current moment.

presets.label: Farther probe
presets.description: The same sound pattern reaches a parcel well downstream after a larger travel delay.

presets.label: One wavelength farther
presets.description: The probe is one full wavelength farther downstream, so the local phase repeats one full cycle later.

overlays.label: Motion directions
overlays.shortDescription: Shows back-and-forth parcel motion together with the right-moving disturbance.
whatToNotice: The parcel arrows reverse left and right, but the disturbance arrow keeps pointing right.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates local particle motion from wave propagation.

overlays.label: Compression / rarefaction
overlays.shortDescription: Labels one local compression and one local rarefaction on the ribbon.
whatToNotice: The ribbon marks crowded and spread-out regions of the medium, not just where one parcel happens to be.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties sound to density changes in the medium instead of only to a sinusoidal graph.

overlays.label: Energy-transfer cue
overlays.shortDescription: Adds a rightward arrow train above the stage to mark energy transfer through the medium.
whatToNotice: Energy moves with the right-moving disturbance even though no parcel travels all the way down the tube.
overlays.whyItMatters: It distinguishes energy transfer from bulk matter flow.

graphs.label: Source and probe particle motion
graphs.description: Compares source and probe parcel motion on the same time axis, so you can read the travel delay directly.

graphs.label: Probe shift and compression cue
graphs.description: Compares one parcel's displacement with the local compression cue, so you can see that strongest compression does not necessarily happen when that parcel is farthest from equilibrium.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one cue at a time and connect it to something visible on the stage or graph.

items.text: Notice that the parcel arrow is left-right while the disturbance arrow remains rightward. The parcels oscillate locally, but the wave pattern propagates through the medium.
items.tryThis: Keep the motion-direction overlay on and watch the probe parcel for one full cycle before touching any controls.
items.whyItMatters: This is the core physical idea of a longitudinal sound wave.

items.text: Notice that the compression cue is often strongest when the probe parcel is near its center position, not when it is farthest left or right.
items.tryThis: Pause on the probe-pressure graph, then compare the compression curve with the parcel-shift curve through one cycle.
items.whyItMatters: Compression depends on local spacing between neighboring parcels, not on one parcel's displacement alone.

items.text: Notice that shortening lambda packs compressions and rarefactions closer together and also makes the source cycle faster when the wave speed is unchanged.
items.tryThis: Lower the wavelength while keeping the wave speed fixed, then compare the pressure ribbon spacing with the source-probe timing graph.
items.whyItMatters: Wavelength is a spacing in the medium, but it also sets the timing through the wave relation.

items.text: Notice that increasing the wave speed shortens the probe delay, but the parcel motion is still back-and-forth rather than one-way drift.
items.tryThis: Raise the wave speed and compare the probe delay readout before and after.
items.whyItMatters: A faster disturbance means the pattern reaches the probe sooner, not that the matter stops oscillating locally.

items.text: Notice that larger particle amplitude makes the compression cue stronger, but it does not change the wave speed, wavelength, or travel delay by itself.
items.tryThis: Increase amplitude and compare what changed on the compression curve with what stayed fixed in the timing graph.
items.whyItMatters: Bigger parcel swings do not automatically mean faster propagation.

items.text: Notice that the time rail changes which parcel shift and compression state you are inspecting, but the same one-source sound wave still drives every view.
items.tryThis: Pause, step once, and compare what changed in the probe-pressure graph with what changed on the particle ribbon.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps the stage and graphs synchronized instead of inventing a separate preview-only state.

items.text: Notice which setup packs compressions closer together and which setup reaches the probe sooner. Those are related by the same wave relation, but they are not always the same change.
items.tryThis: Clone the current state, then shorten lambda in one setup and raise v_wave in the other before comparing the two benches.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the consequence before you test it. Each prompt changes one cause at a time so you can connect parcel motion, compression spacing, and timing.

items.prompt: Keep lambda fixed and make the sound wave move faster through the same medium model. What must happen to the source timing and the probe delay?

choices.label: The source frequency should increase and the probe delay should get shorter.
The source frequency should stay fixed because the particle motion is still longitudinal.
The probe delay should get longer because the disturbance is traveling farther each second.

items.explanation: With λ fixed, $v_{wave} = f\lambda$ means a larger wave speed requires a larger frequency. At the same probe position, the larger speed also makes $\Delta t = x / v_{wave}$ smaller.
items.observationHint: Watch the timing graph cycle faster while the same probe position is reached sooner.

items.prompt: Keep the wave speed fixed and shorten lambda. What should happen to the spacing of compressions and the source frequency?

choices.label: Compressions should pack closer together and the source frequency should rise.
Compressions should spread out because the parcels need more room to move.
Only the particle amplitude should change because the wave speed stayed fixed.

items.explanation: A shorter wavelength means more compressions fit into each meter. With wave speed fixed, the source must oscillate faster to produce that tighter spacing.
items.observationHint: Compare the pressure ribbon before and after the wavelength change, then read the source timing.
