items.prompt: Move the probe one full wavelength farther downstream without changing the wave itself. What should happen to the local phase relation at the probe?
items.scenarioLabel: Shift the probe by one wavelength

choices.label: The probe should repeat the same phase relation, but one full cycle later.
The probe should flip to the opposite phase because farther always means opposite.
The probe should stop oscillating because the disturbance has spread out.

items.explanation: Moving the probe one wavelength farther adds one full cycle of lag. The arrival time increases, but the local phase relation repeats after a whole cycle.
items.observationHint: Compare the source and probe traces before and after moving the probe one wavelength farther away.

items.title: Rational Functions / Asymptotes and Behavior
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

keyIdeas: The denominator x - h creates the true vertical asymptote at x = h, so that x-value is excluded from the domain.
The constant k sets the horizontal asymptote y = k, so the graph settles toward that level far to the left and far to the right.
The sign of a decides which side of y = k each branch occupies, and the magnitude of a controls how sharply the graph bends near x = h.
A removable hole adds another excluded x-value without creating blow-up there, so it must be read differently from a vertical asymptote.

correction: Near a vertical asymptote, the function values grow without bound as x approaches the forbidden value.
Near a removable hole, the nearby curve still approaches one finite height.
So a hole deletes a point from an otherwise finite curve, while a true asymptote marks a place where the graph blows up.

controls.description: Move the true vertical asymptote x = h left or right.
Move the horizontal asymptote y = k up or down.
Flip which side of y = k the branches occupy and change how sharply they bend near x = h.
Move the sample points closer to or farther from the true vertical asymptote.

controls.label: Show removable hole
controls.description: Add one removable hole without moving the true asymptotes.
Place the removable hole when the hole view is turned on.

presets.description: A plain shifted reciprocal with one vertical asymptote, one horizontal asymptote, and visible intercepts.

presets.label: Reflected branches
presets.description: The same asymptote layout, but the sign of a swaps which side of y = k each branch occupies.
The same reciprocal family, plus one finite missing point.

overlays.label: Asymptotes
overlays.shortDescription: Show the vertical and horizontal asymptote guides.
whatToNotice: Changing h moves x = h, and changing k moves y = k.
overlays.whyItMatters: These two guides give the fastest read of where the family breaks and where it levels off.

overlays.label: Near-asymptote probes
overlays.shortDescription: Show the left and right sample points at distance d from the vertical asymptote.
whatToNotice: As d shrinks, the y-values near x = h grow rapidly if there is a true vertical asymptote.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties asymptote language to nearby function values instead of only to an undefined x-value.

overlays.label: Intercept markers
overlays.shortDescription: Mark the x-intercept and y-intercept when they are actually defined.
whatToNotice: Intercepts can move even when the asymptotes stay fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: They give extra landmarks on the same family instead of replacing the asymptotes.

overlays.label: Removable hole marker
overlays.shortDescription: Show the missing point when the canceled-factor view is on.
whatToNotice: Near the hole, the curve still approaches one finite height.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates a removable missing point from blow-up at x = h.

graphs.label: Near-asymptote response
graphs.description: Shows the left-hand and right-hand values at distance d from x = h, so you can compare blow-up near the vertical asymptote with the long-run level y = k.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to keep forbidden x-values, long-run levels, and removable holes on one graph story.

items.text: Shrink the probe distance d and watch the y-values near x = h grow much faster. That is the visible difference between a true asymptote and an ordinary missing point.
Raise or lower k and watch the whole family move with y = k while the forbidden x-value stays fixed unless h changes too.
Turn on the removable hole and compare it with the true asymptote. The hole removes one finite point, but the asymptote layout and the long-run level stay the same.

predictionMode.title: Predict the graph behavior
predictionMode.intro: Decide what should move before you touch the controls.

items.prompt: If h increases while a and k stay fixed, what should move first on the graph?

choices.label: The vertical asymptote and both branches shift to the right.
Only the horizontal asymptote moves upward.
The branch orientation flips above and below y = k.

items.explanation: Changing h moves the forbidden x-value and the whole reciprocal family horizontally. The horizontal asymptote level and branch orientation stay the same until k or a changes.
items.observationHint: Keep the asymptote guides visible while you watch the family translate.

items.prompt: If a changes from positive to negative while h and k stay fixed, what is the most direct visual change?

choices.label: The two branches swap which side of y = k they occupy.
The vertical asymptote disappears.
The graph gains a removable hole automatically.

items.explanation: Changing the sign of a flips the branch orientation relative to the horizontal asymptote. The same x = h and y = k still anchor the family.
items.observationHint: Watch the probe markers relative to y = k when the branch sign flips.

items.prompt: Turning on the removable-hole view without changing h or k should do what?

choices.label: It removes one finite point without moving the true asymptotes.
It turns the hole into a second vertical asymptote.
It forces the horizontal asymptote to become y = 0.

items.explanation: A removable hole is a finite missing point on the same family. It adds a domain break, but the true vertical asymptote x = h and the horizontal asymptote y = k stay where they were.
items.observationHint: Keep the hole marker and asymptote guides visible together.

challengeMode.title: Separate the hole from the asymptote
challengeMode.intro: Use the graph as a domain-break bench. The goal is to keep a true asymptote and a removable hole clearly different while still reading branch orientation from the same family.

items.title: Keep the true break and the removable break separate
items.prompt: Build a reciprocal family where the true vertical asymptote sits near $x=-1$, the removable hole sits at a positive x-value, and the right branch stays below the horizontal asymptote.
items.successMessage: You separated the true asymptote from the removable hole while keeping the branch orientation consistent. The two excluded x-values now tell different graph stories for visible reasons.

setup.note: Start from the clean shifted reciprocal, then turn the hole on and move the family until the true asymptote and the removable hole behave differently on the same graph.

hints: The sign of a decides which side of the horizontal asymptote the right branch occupies.
A removable hole should still have one finite nearby height, so do not place it on the asymptote itself.

targets.label: Move the true vertical asymptote into the left-side band between $-1.1$ and $-0.9$.
Keep the horizontal asymptote above the x-axis, between $0.7$ and $0.95$.
Flip the branch scale negative, between $-2.1$ and $-1.5$, so the right branch stays below $y=k$.
Place the removable hole on positive x, between $1.15$ and $1.45$.

items.title: Continuity Equation
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

keyIdeas: In steady incompressible flow, the same volume per second passes every cross section, so $Q = A_A v_A = A_B v_B$.
A smaller area means the fluid must move faster there to keep the same $Q$.
A larger area means the same $Q$ can pass with a lower speed.
The stage and graphs should agree on the same story: area changes cause the speed change, not a change in how much fluid per second is flowing.
Continuity gives the speed-area link that Bernoulli later combines with pressure changes.

controls.description: Changes how much volume the pipe carries each second.
Adjusts the wider entry section.
Adjusts the middle section so it can narrow or widen relative to section A.

presets.label: Uniform pipe
presets.description: Both sections have the same area, so the speeds match.

presets.label: Tight throat
presets.description: A much smaller middle section forces a larger middle speed.

presets.label: Wide chamber
presets.description: A wider middle section slows the fluid down there.

presets.label: Wide entry
presets.description: A larger section A lowers the entry speed.

presets.label: Higher flow
presets.description: A larger flow rate raises both section speeds.

overlays.label: Area markers
overlays.shortDescription: Marks the two cross sections whose areas appear in the continuity equation.
whatToNotice: When section B is smaller than section A, section B must have the larger speed to keep the same $Q$.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the equation to the visible pipe geometry.

overlays.label: Speed arrows
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the speeds at sections A and B directly on the pipe.
whatToNotice: The smaller-area section gets the longer speed arrow when $Q$ stays fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It lets you see the speed change before reading the graph or calculation.

overlays.label: Flow-rate equality
overlays.shortDescription: Shows that the same $Q$ applies at both sections.
whatToNotice: Both sections keep the same $Q$ even when their areas and speeds are different.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates conservation of flow rate from changes in speed.

overlays.label: Same-time slices
overlays.shortDescription: Shows how far equal-time fluid slices extend in each section.
whatToNotice: Over the same short time, the faster section covers more length.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns volume flow rate into a visible same-time comparison.

graphs.label: Section speed vs entry area
graphs.description: Change section A only. Read how $v_A$ changes while section B and $Q$ stay fixed.

graphs.label: Section speed vs middle area
graphs.description: Change section B only. Read the inverse area-speed link in the middle section while section A and $Q$ stay fixed.

graphs.label: Section speed vs flow rate
graphs.description: Change $Q$ only. Both section speeds rise together because both must carry more volume each second.

graphs.label: Volume flow rate by section
graphs.description: The two lines stay matched because continuity says both sections carry the same steady incompressible flow rate.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Change one thing at a time so the pipe shape, arrows, readouts, and graphs all tell the same continuity story.

items.text: Make section B smaller. The matched $Q$ labels stay the same, but the section B speed arrow grows because the same flow must pass through less area.
Make section B wider than section A. The section B speed drops because the same $Q$ is spread across more area there.

Open the flow-balance graph. A flat matched line does not mean the pipe variables stopped mattering. It means the same volume per second is still passing both sections, while the speed changes show up elsewhere.
Use compare mode to keep the same $Q$ but make section B much smaller in one setup. That speed difference is the part continuity gives to Bernoulli later.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Choose the speed change first, then run the setup. Focus on which quantity stays fixed and which one must adjust.

choices.label: The middle speed rises while the flow rate through both sections stays the same.
The flow rate rises because the fluid is squeezed through the narrow section.
Both speeds stay the same because the same fluid is moving.

items.explanation: Continuity keeps the same $Q$ through the pipe. With less area at section B, the fluid there must move faster.
items.observationHint: Section B narrows, its speed arrow grows, and the flow-balance graph still keeps the two $Q$ lines matched.

choices.label: Both section speeds rise, while the faster section stays the one with the smaller area.
Only section B changes because it is the narrow section.
The speed ratio changes even though the areas are fixed.

items.explanation: With both areas fixed, each section speed is proportional to $Q$. The smaller-area section remains the faster one because the area ratio did not change.
items.observationHint: Both speed arrows grow, and the speed-flow-rate graph shifts both speeds upward together.

choices.label: The entry speed falls, but the middle speed stays the same because section B did not change.
The middle speed rises because section A got wider.
The flow rate must double because the entry area is larger.

items.explanation: Section A follows $v_A = Q/A_A$, so a larger entry area lowers the entry speed. Section B keeps the same speed because its area and $Q$ did not change.
items.observationHint: The section A arrow shortens while the section B arrow stays almost unchanged.

items.title: Dynamic Equilibrium / Le Chatelier's Principle
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

controls.label: Reactant amount
controls.description: Change the starting reactant amount to disturb the current balance.

controls.label: Product amount
controls.description: Change the starting product amount to disturb the current balance.
Shift the equilibrium target toward products or toward reactants.

presets.label: Reactant-heavy start
presets.description: Starts far from equilibrium on the reactant side, so the forward rate leads first.

presets.label: Product-heavy start
presets.description: Starts far from equilibrium on the product side, so the reverse rate leads first.

presets.label: Product-favored conditions
presets.description: Uses conditions that move the equilibrium target toward a more product-rich mixture.

presets.label: Reactant-favored conditions
presets.description: Uses conditions that move the equilibrium target back toward reactants.

overlays.label: Exchange pulses
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the forward and reverse exchange pulses visible.
whatToNotice: The exchange pulses can stay active even when the amount curves have almost flattened out.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows that equilibrium is dynamic rather than stopped.

overlays.label: Rate balance bars
overlays.shortDescription: Show the forward and reverse rates side by side.
whatToNotice: Near equilibrium, the two rate bars nearly match even though the reactant and product amounts do not have to be equal.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the definition of equilibrium to rate balance, not to equal amounts or a frozen system.

overlays.label: Equilibrium target
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the current mixture and the equilibrium target visible together.
whatToNotice: The system keeps shifting only while the current mixture still differs from the equilibrium target for those conditions.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes Le Chatelier shifts visible as motion toward a new equilibrium.

graphs.label: Reactants and products over time
graphs.description: Shows how the reactant and product amounts move toward equilibrium after a disturbance.

graphs.label: Forward and reverse rates over time
graphs.description: Shows the rates separate after a disturbance and then come back together near equilibrium.
Shows how changing the product-favor setting shifts the equilibrium mixture.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice about equilibrium
noticePrompts.intro: Read the rate bars, the current mix, and the equilibrium target together on the same bench.

items.text: The amount curves can flatten while both exchange pulses are still active. Equilibrium means equal forward and reverse rates, not a stopped reaction.
items.whyItMatters: This is the core idea of dynamic equilibrium: steady amounts can still come from ongoing forward and reverse change.

items.text: A disturbance matters when it makes the current mix differ from the equilibrium target. Watch the target band and the time graph together.
After a disturbance, the rates separate first, the mixture shifts next, and only later do the rates come back together.
items.tryThis: Apply one preset, then switch to the opposite favor setting and watch the rate graph and the concentration graph until a new balance is reached.

predictionMode.title: Predict the direction of the shift
predictionMode.intro: Before you test a change, ask how it moves the current mix relative to the equilibrium target for the new conditions.

items.prompt: Starting from the reactant-heavy case, you add even more reactants without changing the favor setting. What should happen first?
items.scenarioLabel: Add more reactants under the same conditions

choices.label: The forward rate should become larger first, so the system shifts toward products.
The reaction should stop because the mixture is too far from equilibrium.
The reverse rate should dominate immediately.

items.explanation: With more reactants under the same conditions, the forward reaction has the larger rate at first. The system therefore shifts toward products until the rates match again.
items.observationHint: Watch the forward rate pull ahead first, then watch the product amount rise toward the new equilibrium mix.

items.prompt: You change from product-favored conditions to reactant-favored conditions while keeping the same starting amounts. What should the equilibrium mixture do?
items.scenarioLabel: Change the conditions to favor reactants

choices.label: The equilibrium product share should decrease, and the reverse rate should lead during the shift.
The equilibrium product share should stay the same because the starting amounts did not change.
Both rates should instantly drop to zero.

items.explanation: Changing the conditions changes the equilibrium target. The system then shifts toward that new target, so the reverse rate can lead until the new balance is reached.
items.observationHint: Compare the equilibrium-share graph with the rate bars after the switch.

challengeMode.title: Challenge: disturb then rebalance
challengeMode.intro: Make a real disturbance, then give the system time to settle into a new equilibrium instead of treating equilibrium like an instant switch.

items.title: Create a shift, then let it rebalance
items.prompt: Start from a reactant-heavy mixture, choose clearly product-favored conditions, and wait until the forward and reverse rates nearly match again while the mixture stays product-rich.
items.successMessage: You created a genuine equilibrium shift and let the system settle. The mixture is product-favored, and the clearest equilibrium cue is that the forward and reverse rates are nearly equal again.

setup.note: Change the starting mix or the favor setting, then let the bench run long enough to approach the new equilibrium.

hints: Start from a reactant-heavy mixture, then choose conditions that place the equilibrium target on the product-rich side.
The challenge only counts once the system has had time to bring the forward and reverse rates close again.

targets.label: Set the product-favor setting between 1.28 and 1.4 so the new equilibrium clearly favors products.
Let the current product share settle between 58% and 70%.
Wait until the forward-reverse rate gap falls below 0.14 before judging the result.

items.title: Electromagnetic Waves
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

paragraphs: The probe-field graph compares the electric field and the display-scaled magnetic field at one probe on the same time axis, so their shared timing can be read directly.

The source-probe graph compares the source electric field with the downstream probe electric field. This makes the travel delay and phase lag visible for one wave without leaving the same simulation state.

controls.label: Electric-field amplitude
controls.description: Changes the electric-field height and the matching magnetic-field height without changing the timing relations.
Controls how quickly the whole field pattern travels to the right, changing both delay and frequency.
Controls the crest-to-crest spacing of the shared field pattern.
Moves the live measurement point along the propagation axis so the same wave is read later or earlier.

presets.label: Slower propagation
presets.description: The same electric amplitude and wavelength now move more slowly, so the probe waits longer and the magnetic amplitude grows in this model.

presets.label: Faster propagation
presets.description: The field pattern reaches the probe sooner and cycles faster while the magnetic amplitude shrinks relative to the same electric wave.

presets.label: Longer wavelength
presets.description: The same wave speed now spreads the field pattern farther apart in space, so the probe sits fewer cycles behind the source.

presets.label: One wavelength away
presets.description: The probe sits exactly one wavelength from the source, so it returns to the same phase one full cycle later.

overlays.label: Wavelength guide
overlays.shortDescription: Marks one full crest-to-crest spacing directly on the electric-field lane.
whatToNotice: Changing \lambda re-spaces both field lanes together because they are two views of the same traveling wave.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps wavelength as a visible spacing in space rather than a formula-only quantity.

overlays.label: Probe delay guide
overlays.shortDescription: Labels the source-to-probe distance, travel delay, and cycle lag together.
whatToNotice: Moving the probe changes when that location responds, not how the source itself oscillates.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates downstream delay from the local E/B relationship at one point.

overlays.label: Propagation triad
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the local E direction, B direction, and rightward propagation cue together.
whatToNotice: When the electric direction flips, the magnetic direction flips with it, so E 	imes B still points to the right.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the local field pair into a direct statement about propagation direction.

graphs.label: Probe electric and magnetic field
graphs.description: Tracks the electric field and the display-scaled magnetic field at the same probe, so their shared timing is easy to compare.

graphs.label: Source and probe electric field
graphs.description: Compares the source electric field with the downstream probe electric field on the same time axis, making travel delay and phase lag visible in one wave.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use one prompt at a time. Each one points to a relation the live stage and graphs already show in the current wave.

items.text: At one probe location, the electric and magnetic fields reverse together. The delay belongs to source versus probe, not to E versus B at the same point.
items.tryThis: Watch one probe marker through several cycles and compare the two traces on the probe-field graph.
items.whyItMatters: This is the main conceptual shift from treating electric and magnetic fields as separate timelines.

items.text: The source-probe graph shows the same electric oscillation appearing later downstream, not a different oscillation.
items.tryThis: Drag the probe right and watch the horizontal separation between the two electric traces widen.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps delay tied to distance through space instead of inventing a separate downstream mechanism.

items.text: Lowering the wave speed does three things at once here: it lowers frequency at fixed wavelength, increases source-to-probe delay, and makes the magnetic amplitude larger for the same electric wave.
items.tryThis: Move wave speed down while leaving wavelength alone, then compare the readout card with both graphs.
items.whyItMatters: It links propagation timing and local field pairing through one shared control.

items.tryThis: Clone the current setup, then slow only one setup and compare the readout card and source-probe separation.
items.whyItMatters: It isolates which differences come from propagation speed and which come from field size or wavelength.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Make the prediction before moving the control. Each scenario changes one wave property at a time so the live stage and graphs can confirm the consequence.

items.prompt: Keep electric amplitude and wavelength fixed, but make the wave travel more slowly. Which bundle of changes should happen in this model?

choices.label: The magnetic amplitude should grow, the source frequency should drop, and the probe delay should increase.
Only the travel delay should change because E and B amplitudes are fixed by the source.
The wavelength should shrink automatically so the source frequency can stay the same.

items.explanation: With \lambda fixed, lowering v lowers f because v = f \lambda. The source-to-probe delay x_p / v therefore increases, and the model's B = E / v rule makes the magnetic amplitude larger for the same electric field.
items.observationHint: Watch the source-probe gap widen and the magnetic trace grow relative to the electric trace.

items.prompt: Keep wave speed and probe position fixed, but increase wavelength. What should happen to the phase lag and the travel delay?

choices.label: The phase lag in cycles should decrease, while the travel delay stays the same.
Both the phase lag and the travel delay should increase because the pattern is more spread out.
Neither one should change because the probe is still at the same place.

items.explanation: Travel delay depends on x_p / v, so it stays fixed when x_p and v are fixed. But the cycle lag is x_p / \lambda, so a larger wavelength means fewer cycles of lag between the source and the same probe.
items.observationHint: Compare the cycle-lag readout before and after the wavelength change while the travel-delay value stays fixed.

items.prompt: Place the probe exactly one wavelength from the source. What should the source-probe phase relation become?
items.scenarioLabel: Place the probe one lambda away

choices.label: The probe should return to the same phase as the source, but one full cycle later.
The probe should flip to opposite phase because it is farther from the source.
The phase relation should become undefined because the probe is no longer near the source.

items.explanation: One wavelength of extra distance adds one full cycle of lag. The downstream point repeats the same phase, but only after one full-cycle delay.
items.observationHint: Watch the source and probe electric traces line back up with a full-period horizontal shift.

items.title: Vectors in 2D
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Set the horizontal component of vector A.
Set the vertical component of vector A.
Set the horizontal component of vector B.
Set the vertical component of vector B.

controls.label: Scalar on A
controls.description: Stretch, compress, or flip A before it combines with B.

controls.label: Subtract B
controls.description: Show subtraction as adding the opposite vector.

presets.label: Balanced sum
presets.description: A clean first-quadrant addition case.

presets.label: Difference
presets.description: A subtraction case where B is reversed before the combination.

presets.label: Flipped scale
presets.description: A negative scalar that flips A through the origin before the sum.

presets.label: Near cancel
presets.description: A case where the resultant lands close to the origin.

overlays.label: Component guides
overlays.shortDescription: Show the resultant's horizontal and vertical projections on the axes.
whatToNotice: The same resultant can be read as one diagonal arrow or as two perpendicular components.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the plane picture directly to the component rule.

overlays.label: Tip-to-tail view
overlays.shortDescription: Move the effective second vector to the tip of the scaled first vector.
whatToNotice: Addition and subtraction use the same construction once B is kept or reversed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes vector combination something you can see, not just a rule to memorize.

overlays.label: Scaled A
overlays.shortDescription: Show the scaled copy of A before it combines with B.
whatToNotice: A positive scalar keeps A's direction, while a negative scalar flips it through the origin.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates scalar multiplication from the later addition or subtraction step.

graphs.label: Result components vs scalar
graphs.description: Shows how the x- and y-components of the resultant change as the scalar on A varies.

graphs.label: Result magnitude vs scalar
graphs.description: Shows how the size of the resultant changes as the scaled first vector moves.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to keep the plane picture, the component readout, and the graphs describing the same vector event.

items.text: Drag A or B and compare the arrow on the plane with the ordered pair. They change together because they are the same vector in two forms.
Turn subtraction on and watch B reverse before it is moved tip-to-tail. The construction is still addition, but now you are adding the opposite of B.
Move the scalar through zero and watch sA flip through the origin. The magnitude graph never goes negative, because size and direction are different ideas.

predictionMode.title: Predict the change
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the plane and graphs should do before you change the operation or the scalar.

items.prompt: If the scalar on A changes from positive to negative while its magnitude stays above 1, what is the most direct visual effect?

choices.label: The scaled version of A flips through the origin and changes size.
Vector B is forced to reverse instead.
The resultant magnitude must become negative.

items.explanation: A negative scalar reverses A through the origin. Because the scalar's magnitude is still above 1, the scaled copy is also longer than A.
items.observationHint: Watch the scaled copy of A first, then see how the resultant and the graphs respond.

items.prompt: If subtract mode turns on while every component stays the same, what should happen to the second vector in the tip-to-tail picture?

choices.label: B should reverse direction so the picture becomes sA + (-B).
A should disappear because subtraction removes the first vector.
Only the magnitude graph changes while the plane stays the same.

items.explanation: Subtraction is addition with the opposite vector, so the same tip-to-tail rule still works after B is reversed.
items.observationHint: Watch B reverse, then watch the resultant rebuild from the same construction.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the plane like a vector lab, not a static diagram. The goal is to make cancellation visible while the individual vectors are still clearly nonzero.

items.title: Near-zero resultant
items.prompt: Adjust the vectors until the resultant lands very close to the origin, but keep the scaled first vector clearly nontrivial so the near-zero result comes from cancellation rather than from shrinking everything.
items.successMessage: You created a near-zero resultant by cancellation, not by making the vectors trivial. The plane, the component readout, and the magnitude now agree.

setup.note: Use the tip-to-tail view and the component guides together. One shows geometric cancellation, while the other shows which x- and y-components are balancing.

hints: A near-zero resultant needs both the x-components and y-components to nearly cancel.
Keep the scalar away from zero so the first vector still matters.

targets.label: Bring the resultant magnitude below $0.35$.
Keep the scaled first vector magnitude between $3.3$ and $3.9$.
Keep the scalar close to $1$ so the cancellation comes mostly from vector opposition, not from shrinking A away.

items.title: Derivative as Slope / Local Rate of Change
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Move the chosen point along the curve.
Set the horizontal spacing to the secant point used for the average rate.
Hide or show the secant comparison so you can compare average and local slope.

presets.label: Negative slope
presets.description: A point where the curve is decreasing and the tangent slope is negative.

presets.label: Almost flat
presets.description: A point near a turning point where the tangent is almost horizontal.

presets.label: Positive rise
presets.description: A point where the curve is increasing and the tangent slope is clearly positive.

overlays.shortDescription: Draw the tangent at the current point to show the instantaneous slope.
whatToNotice: At the chosen point, the tangent matches the curve's local direction.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows the derivative as a visible slope on the original curve.

overlays.label: Secant line
overlays.shortDescription: Draw the secant through the two sample points separated by the current Δx.
whatToNotice: A larger Δx usually makes this average slope differ more from the tangent slope.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the average-rate idea visible before the limit definition takes over.

overlays.label: Δy/Δx guide
overlays.shortDescription: Show the horizontal and vertical changes used in the difference quotient.
whatToNotice: The secant slope is the visible ratio Δy/Δx.
overlays.whyItMatters: It connects the drawn interval to the algebra of the difference quotient.

graphs.label: Derivative graph
graphs.description: Shows how the tangent slope changes across the curve: positive values mean the curve rises there, negative values mean it falls there, and values near zero mean a nearly flat tangent.

graphs.label: Secant slope vs delta x
graphs.description: Shows how the secant slope changes as Δx shrinks and how it approaches the tangent slope at the chosen point.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to connect the curve, the lines, and the slope graphs. Read the geometry first, then use the graph as a slope map.

items.text: Move the point and watch the tangent switch from falling to rising. The derivative crosses zero where the tangent becomes flat.
Shrink Δx and compare the secant slope with the tangent slope. The average rate approaches the local rate.
Read the derivative graph as a slope map: positive means rising, negative means falling, and zero means a flat tangent.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Predict what should happen to the slope before you move the point or change Δx.

items.prompt: If you move the point far enough to the right, what should eventually happen to the tangent slope?

choices.label: It should become positive because the curve eventually rises there.
It must stay negative because the curve started with a falling slope.
It should become zero everywhere on the right half.

items.explanation: Far enough to the right, the curve is increasing there, so the tangent slope becomes positive.
items.observationHint: Check the tangent on the curve and the matching point on the derivative graph at the same time.

items.prompt: If delta x shrinks while the point stays fixed, what should happen to the secant slope?

choices.label: It should move closer to the tangent slope.
It should always move farther away because the interval is smaller.
It should stay exactly the same because the point has not moved.

items.explanation: The derivative is the limit of the difference quotient, so shrinking delta x makes the secant slope approach the tangent slope.
items.observationHint: Keep the point fixed and watch the secant-slope readout move toward the tangent-slope readout as the interval tightens.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the point and Δx as a slope-comparison bench. The goal is to make the tangent and secant tell the same near-flat story for a geometric reason, not just to hit a number.

items.title: Match the near-flat slopes
items.prompt: Move to the right-hand turning point so the tangent is almost flat, then shrink Δx until the secant slope is almost flat there too.
items.successMessage: You found a point where both the local slope and the small-interval average slope are nearly zero. The derivative and difference quotient are now telling the same near-flat story.

setup.note: Use the secant graph and the Δy/Δx guide while you move toward the right-hand turning point.

hints: The right-hand turning point sits a little past $x=1.3$.
If the secant is still noticeably tilted, shrink Δx further.

targets.label: Move the active point into the right-hand flat-turn band between $1.25$ and $1.45$.
Shrink $\Delta x$ into a tight local interval between $0.15$ and $0.3$.
Bring the tangent slope into the near-zero band from $-0.08$ to $0.08$.
Keep the secant slope close too, between $-0.12$ and $0.12$.

items.title: Dot Product / Angle and Projection
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

variables.label: Signed projection of B onto A
Signed projection of B onto A

controls.description: Set the horizontal component of the reference vector A.
Set the vertical component of A.
Set the horizontal component of the probe vector B.
Set the vertical component of B.

presets.label: Acute alignment
presets.description: A clearly positive dot product with a forward projection along A.

presets.label: Orthogonal turn
presets.description: A clean right-angle case where the along-A part collapses.

presets.label: Negative projection
presets.description: An obtuse-angle case where the projection points against A.

presets.label: Nearly opposite
presets.description: A strong negative dot product with only a small perpendicular leftover.

overlays.label: Angle marker
overlays.shortDescription: Show the angle between A and B at the origin.
whatToNotice: The sign change happens when the angle crosses $90^\circ$, not when either vector becomes small.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties positive, zero, and negative dot products to geometry.

overlays.label: Projection guide
overlays.shortDescription: Show the projection of B onto A and the perpendicular leftover.
whatToNotice: The amber part is what contributes to the dot product. The dashed leftover does not.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the dot product into a visible decomposition instead of a hidden multiplication rule.

graphs.label: Dot product vs angle
graphs.description: With the current vector lengths fixed, shows how angle alone changes the dot product from positive to zero to negative.

graphs.label: Projection vs angle
graphs.description: With the current vector lengths fixed, shows how the signed along-A part of B changes as the angle moves from acute to obtuse.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the angle marker, the amber projection, and the sign reading on the same story.

items.text: An acute angle keeps a positive along-A part, so both the projection and the dot product are positive.
As the angle approaches $90^\circ$, the amber projection shrinks to zero even if $\vec{B}$ stays long.
Past $90^\circ$, the projection flips against A, so the dot product becomes negative.

predictionMode.title: Predict the sign
predictionMode.intro: Predict how the sign and projection will change before you rotate $\vec{B}$.

items.prompt: If B rotates toward ninety degrees from A while both vector lengths stay fixed, what must happen?

choices.label: The dot product and the signed projection both shrink toward zero.
The dot product stays positive because the vectors still have nonzero length.
The projection gets larger because the angle marker is larger.

items.explanation: Orthogonality removes the along-A part of B. Length alone does not keep the dot product positive.
items.observationHint: Watch the amber projection collapse while the angle marker approaches $90^\circ$.

items.prompt: If B rotates past ninety degrees while A stays fixed, which statement becomes correct?
items.scenarioLabel: Push B into an obtuse angle

choices.label: The projection flips against A and the dot product becomes negative.
Only the angle changes. The projection must stay in A's direction.
The dot product becomes undefined because the vectors are no longer aligned.

items.explanation: An obtuse angle means the along-A part of B points the opposite way, so both the signed projection and the dot product flip sign.
items.observationHint: The amber projection crosses to the opposite side of the origin before you read the negative value.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the angle marker, the amber projection, and the response graph together. The goal is to make zero alignment visible without shrinking either vector away.

items.title: Make the projection nearly vanish
items.prompt: Adjust $\vec{B}$ until the amber projection nearly collapses while both arrows stay clearly nonzero. Keep the angle marker and projection guide visible so the right-angle story stays geometric.
items.successMessage: You created the orthogonal case without hiding it in zero-length vectors. The angle, projection, and dot-product readout now agree on the same zero-alignment moment.

setup.note: Keep $\vec{A}$ fixed and rotate $\vec{B}$ until the along-A part disappears rather than shrinking either vector away.

hints: A right angle kills the along-A part without killing either vector.
Use the angle marker first, then confirm with the amber projection.

targets.label: Move the B x-component close to $-1$.
Move the B y-component close to $2$.

items.title: Optimization / Maxima, Minima, and Constraints
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

correction: On this bench, making the width larger always forces the height smaller because the perimeter stays fixed at 24 meters.
Area is width times height, so a longer base stops helping once the lost height becomes too costly.
That is why the objective curve eventually turns downward even while width is still increasing.

controls.description: Move the width while the fixed 24 meter perimeter forces the matching height.

presets.label: Too narrow
presets.description: A tall, narrow rectangle where making it wider still helps.

presets.label: Best square
presets.description: The square that reaches the peak area under the fixed perimeter.

presets.label: Too wide
presets.description: A wide rectangle that has already passed the peak.

overlays.label: Constraint band
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the fixed perimeter visible on the current rectangle.
whatToNotice: Every time width grows, height must shrink because the perimeter is staying fixed at 24 meters.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the optimization story honest by showing the tradeoff instead of hiding it behind symbols.

overlays.label: Best rectangle overlay
overlays.shortDescription: Show the square that reaches the peak area for this fixed perimeter.
whatToNotice: The best rectangle is the square, and the objective curve peaks at the same width where width and height match.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties the geometric picture directly to the maximum on the graph instead of making the best case feel magical.

overlays.label: Area guides
overlays.shortDescription: Show the current point on the objective graph together with width and height guides on the rectangle.
whatToNotice: The rectangle dimensions and the objective point are just two views of the same width choice.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the changing parameter, the candidate shape, and the area value on one compact bench.

graphs.label: Objective graph
graphs.description: Shows the constrained area A(w) for the 24 meter perimeter. Its highest point is the maximum possible area.
Shows whether making the rectangle wider would help or hurt area at the current width: positive helps, negative hurts, and zero marks the peak.

graphs.label: Constraint tradeoff
graphs.description: Shows the fixed-perimeter tradeoff h = 12 - w, so every extra meter of width costs one meter of height.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use the rectangle, the tradeoff line, and the area graph as one linked story.

items.text: This rectangle is still too narrow. A little more width gives back more area than the lost height removes.
Near the square, the area graph flattens. That near-zero slope is the sign that the best rectangle is here, not at one of the extreme widths.
Past the square, extra width hurts because the height is now dropping too fast.
The constraint line h = 12 - w shows the one-for-one tradeoff directly: every extra meter of width costs one meter of height.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Use the fixed-perimeter tradeoff first, then test what the objective curve should do.

items.prompt: From this narrow rectangle, what should happen if the width increases a little while the perimeter stays fixed?

choices.label: The area should increase.
The area should decrease immediately.
The area should stay almost unchanged because the perimeter is fixed.

items.explanation: On the narrow side of the peak, the area slope is still positive, so a little more width increases the objective.
items.observationHint: Watch the objective point and the local slope together as width grows from a narrow starting case.

items.prompt: Once the width is already past the square, what should happen if the width increases even more?

choices.label: The area should keep increasing because the base is getting longer.
The area should turn downward because too much height is being lost.
The area should lock at the same value because the perimeter is fixed.

items.explanation: Past the peak, the derivative is negative. Extra width now costs more height than it adds area.
items.observationHint: The objective curve is already on its falling side, and the best-square overlay makes that turning point visible.

challengeMode.title: Optimization challenge
challengeMode.intro: Use the rectangle, the objective graph, and the slope graph together. The goal is to reach the maximum because the tradeoff balances there, not just to guess 36.

items.title: Find the maximum-area rectangle
items.prompt: Move the width until the rectangle reaches the maximum area and the local area slope is essentially zero.
items.successMessage: You found the square maximum. The rectangle, the peak of A(w), and the zero of A'(w) all agree on the same width.

setup.note: Start from the narrow rectangle, then move toward the square while watching the height tradeoff and the objective curve flatten.

hints: The best rectangle is the square under this fixed perimeter.
The slope should settle near zero at the same width where the area graph reaches its peak.

targets.label: Bring the width into the square band between $5.85$ and $6.15$ meters.

items.title: Integral as Accumulation / Area
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Move the endpoint x that determines how much signed area has been accumulated from 0.

presets.label: Positive build
presets.description: A bound where the source is above the axis and the total is growing.

presets.label: Near cancel
presets.description: A bound where earlier positive area and later negative area nearly balance.

presets.label: Negative tail
presets.description: A bound where the source is below the axis and the total is being pulled down.

presets.label: Leftward build
presets.description: A negative bound so you can see signed area accumulate in the opposite direction.

overlays.label: Signed area shading
overlays.shortDescription: Shade the signed area collected from 0 to the current bound.
whatToNotice: Area above the axis adds to the total, while area below the axis subtracts from it.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the integral visible as accumulation, not just as notation.

overlays.label: Bound guide
overlays.shortDescription: Mark the current upper bound on the source graph.
whatToNotice: The source height at this x-value tells you how the running total is changing right now.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the current source value from the whole accumulated amount.

overlays.label: Accumulation point
overlays.shortDescription: Mark the matching point on the accumulation graph.
whatToNotice: The y-value is the running total, and the local slope there is set by the current source height.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the link A'(x) = f(x) visible on the graph.

graphs.label: Source function
graphs.description: Shows the source curve that supplies the signed area being accumulated.

graphs.label: Accumulation graph
graphs.description: Shows the running total built from the signed area between 0 and x.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use the same x-value on both graphs. One graph tells you what the next slice is doing now, and the other tells you how much has been collected so far.

items.text: While the source curve stays above the axis, moving the bound to the right adds positive slices and makes the running total rise.
Once the source drops below the axis, the accumulation graph can still sit above zero even while its local slope turns negative.
Compare f(x) with A(x) directly: one is the current height at the bound, and the other is the total signed area collected up to that bound.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Predict whether the running total should rise, flatten, or fall before you move the bound.

items.prompt: If the upper bound moves a little farther right while the source height stays positive, what should happen to the accumulated amount?
items.scenarioLabel: Move right in the positive region

choices.label: The accumulated amount should increase.
The accumulated amount must drop because the interval is longer.
The accumulated amount should stay fixed because the starting bound is still 0.

items.explanation: If the source height stays positive, each extra thin slice adds positive signed area, so the running total increases.
items.observationHint: Use the shaded area and the accumulation point as the same event shown on two graphs.

items.prompt: If the source height is negative at the current bound, what must be true about the accumulation graph there?

choices.label: Its local slope is negative there.
Its y-value must already be negative there.
It becomes flat because negative area cancels the whole graph.

items.explanation: Because A'(x) = f(x), a negative source height means the accumulation graph slopes downward at that x-value, even if A(x) is still above zero.
items.observationHint: Keep the graph's current height and its local slope separate.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the two graphs together. The goal is to keep local height and total accumulation separate, even when the signs tempt you to treat them as the same thing.

items.title: Negative height, positive total
items.prompt: Move the bound to a place where the source height is negative, but the running total is still positive.
items.successMessage: You found the key split between local rate and accumulated amount. The source is subtracting locally, but the earlier positive area still keeps the running total above zero.

setup.note: Watch the current height on the source graph and the total on the accumulation graph at the same x-value.

hints: Move past the right-hand zero of the source curve, but not so far that the earlier positive area has been fully cancelled.
The accumulation graph can stay above zero even while its local slope turns negative.

targets.label: Place the upper bound in the negative-tail window between $2.05$ and $2.35$.
Make the current source height clearly negative, between $-0.8$ and $-0.45$.
Keep the accumulated amount positive, between $0.8$ and $1.2$.

items.title: Graph Transformations
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Move the transformed graph left or right by changing where the chosen x-landmark lands.
Move the transformed graph up or down after the vertical scale has acted.
Stretch, compress, or reflect the graph across the x-axis.
Reverse the graph left-right across the y-axis before the horizontal shift acts.

overlays.label: Reference curve
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the base graph visible behind the transformed graph.
whatToNotice: The transformed graph is always being compared against the same original shape.
The y-axis reflection is easiest to read when the base curve stays on screen.
overlays.whyItMatters: A fixed reference graph makes the parameter effects readable instead of abstract.

overlays.label: Vertex markers
overlays.shortDescription: Mark the base vertex and the transformed vertex.
whatToNotice: The transformed vertex follows the inside move horizontally and the outside move vertically.
The vertex is the fastest landmark for checking whether a shift or reflection story is correct.

overlays.label: Shift guide
overlays.shortDescription: Show how the landmark moves horizontally and vertically from the base vertex.
whatToNotice: The horizontal placement sets the x-location first, then the vertical scale and shift set the final height.
A negative vertical scale can flip the height before the vertical shift is added.

graphs.label: Function graph
graphs.description: Compare the base and transformed curves on the same axes so landmark moves stay visible.

graphs.label: Vertex height vs vertical scale
graphs.description: Shows how changing the vertical scale changes the transformed vertex height for the current vertical shift.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts when the signs start to feel slippery. Each one points back to a visible landmark on the graph.

items.text: Change only the horizontal shift and watch the transformed vertex slide sideways while its height stays fixed.
When the vertical scale becomes negative, the graph flips across the x-axis before the vertical shift places it.
Turn on the y-axis reflection and compare the base vertex with the reflected inside landmark before the horizontal shift acts.

predictionMode.title: Predict the landmark move
predictionMode.intro: Commit to the landmark move before you apply the new transform.

items.prompt: If $h$ increases while everything else stays fixed, what happens first to the transformed vertex?

choices.label: It moves to the right while keeping the same height.
It moves upward because the inside sign changes.
It reflects across the x-axis.

items.explanation: Changing only $h$ moves the graph horizontally. The vertex height stays fixed until $a$ or $k$ changes.
items.observationHint: Track the transformed vertex marker rather than the sign in the formula by itself.

items.prompt: If $a$ changes from positive to negative with $|a| > 1$, what is the clearest visual result?

choices.label: The graph reflects across the x-axis and becomes steeper.
The graph shifts right but keeps the same orientation.
The graph reflects across the y-axis only.

items.explanation: A negative vertical scale reflects the graph across the x-axis, and a magnitude greater than 1 makes that reflected graph steeper.
items.observationHint: Watch the opening direction and the vertex height together.

items.prompt: If you turn on the y-axis reflection without changing any other control, what happens first?

choices.label: It reverses the left-right orientation of the base graph before the horizontal shift places it.
It automatically changes the vertical scale to negative.
It lifts the graph upward by one unit.

items.explanation: The inside input reverses left-right orientation. It does not change the vertical scale or vertical shift by itself.
items.observationHint: Use the reference curve and the current vertex markers together.

items.title: Buffers and Neutralization
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.

presets.label: Unbuffered acid pulse
presets.description: The same acid push with no buffer reserve, so the pH changes more sharply.
The same acid push with reserve available, so reserve falls before the pH shifts much.
A near-neutral mixture that still has buffer reserve available for later pushes.

overlays.label: Acid-base bars
overlays.shortDescription: Show the current acid-side and base-side bars beside the pH strip.
whatToNotice: The pH can stay near neutral even while the reserve bar shows that the buffer is absorbing a real push.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the pH reading to what is happening in the mixture instead of treating pH as a detached number.

overlays.label: pH strip
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the live pH marker visible while reserve is being used.
whatToNotice: A small pH change does not always mean a small chemical change. Check the reserve bar at the same time.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you read pH together with buffering rather than as a standalone label.

overlays.label: Buffer reserve bar
overlays.shortDescription: Show how much buffering capacity is still available.
whatToNotice: The reserve can drop a lot before the pH moves much.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the hidden action of the buffer visible.

overlays.label: Particle motion
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the mixture moving while the readouts update.
whatToNotice: A nearly steady pH does not mean the mixture has stopped reacting or mixing.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the bench feeling like a live chemical system rather than a frozen display.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice when reserve is working
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the pH strip and reserve bar visible, then change one control at a time.

items.text: In a buffered case, the reserve bar often falls before the pH strip moves far.
items.tryThis: Start from the buffered acid pulse, increase acid amount a little, and watch reserve drop before the pH changes dramatically.
items.whyItMatters: This is the clearest sign that the buffer is absorbing change rather than freezing the mixture.

items.text: Adding water can soften the pH shift, but it does not neutralize acid and base the way adding the opposite side does.
items.tryThis: Change water volume first, then add more base and compare which move actually changes the neutralized amount.
items.whyItMatters: It helps you separate dilution, neutralization, and buffering instead of treating them as the same idea.

predictionMode.title: Predict the first visible change
predictionMode.intro: Before you apply the change, decide whether reserve, pH, or both should respond first.

items.prompt: Starting from the buffered acid pulse, what should you notice first if you add a little more acid while reserve is still available?
items.scenarioLabel: Small acid increase with reserve still available

choices.label: The reserve drops noticeably while the pH moves only a little.
The pH swings strongly at once, but the reserve stays about the same.
Nothing changes because the buffer stops the chemistry.

items.explanation: As long as reserve remains, the buffer absorbs some of the added acid. Reserve therefore drops first, while pH changes less than it would in an unbuffered case.
items.observationHint: Watch the reserve bar, pH strip, and graphs together after the acid increase.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the reserve bar honestly. Keep pH near neutral without solving the problem by simply diluting everything.

items.title: Hold pH near neutral during an acid pulse
items.prompt: Starting from the unbuffered acid pulse, add enough buffer reserve to keep pH near neutral while leaving the acid push in place and without relying on lots of extra water.
items.successMessage: You used buffer reserve to resist the acid pulse while keeping the same chemical push. The reserve bar shows the buffer is doing work, not making the mixture magically unchanged.

setup.note: Keep the acid pulse in place. Solve the problem with buffer reserve, not by washing the mixture away with extra water.

targets.label: Keep acidamount between 6 and 6.4.
Keep baseamount between 3 and 3.8.
Keep watervolume between 1 and 1.5.
Keep bufferamount between 2.2 and 4.2.
Keep ph between 6.5 and 7.5.
Keep bufferremaining between 0.3 and 3.4.

items.title: Limits and Continuity / Approaching a Value
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

recommendedNext.reasonLabel: Use the same graph-first calculus language to catch a constrained turning point on one rectangle bench

controls.description: Switch between a continuous graph, a removable hole, a jump, and an asymptote-like blow-up.

controls.label: Distance to target
controls.description: Move the left-hand and right-hand sample points closer to or farther from the target at $x = 0$.

presets.description: Both sides and the actual point line up at the target.
Both sides approach one height, but the filled point is moved elsewhere.
The left-hand and right-hand sides settle toward different heights.
Both sides run hard toward opposite signs near the target.

overlays.label: Approach markers
overlays.shortDescription: Show the left-hand and right-hand sample points and where they sit on the graph.
whatToNotice: As $h$ shrinks, the two markers reveal whether the sides are moving toward one value, different values, or no finite value at all.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the idea of approach visible instead of leaving it as a definition to memorize.

overlays.label: Finite-limit guide
overlays.shortDescription: Show the shared finite limiting height when the case has one.
whatToNotice: The continuous and removable-hole cases can share the same finite limit guide.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates the limit from the separate question of whether the actual point matches it.

overlays.label: Actual point
overlays.shortDescription: Show the actual function value at $x = 0$, or show that no value is defined there.
whatToNotice: Continuity depends on this point matching the limiting value when a finite limit exists.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps $f(0)$ separate from the nearby approach behavior.

graphs.label: One-sided approach
graphs.description: Shows how the left-hand and right-hand values behave as the sample points move toward the target from each side.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use these prompts to keep three questions separate: what happens from the left, what happens from the right, and what happens exactly at the target.

items.text: Switch between the continuous and removable-hole cases. The nearby values can approach the same height in both cases, even though only one has the actual point at that height.
In the jump case, making $h$ smaller does not make the two sides agree. Each side approaches a different height.
In the blow-up case, the values do not settle to any finite height as $h$ shrinks. Their magnitudes keep growing instead.

predictionMode.title: Predict the limit story
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the graph should do before you switch cases or move the sample points inward.

items.prompt: If both sides head toward the same finite height but the actual point sits somewhere else, what type of behavior is that?

choices.label: A removable discontinuity.
A jump discontinuity.
Automatic continuity.

items.explanation: That is a removable discontinuity. The limit exists, but the function value at the target does not match it.
items.observationHint: Keep both the limit guide and the actual point visible so you can compare them directly.

items.prompt: If the left-hand and right-hand values approach different heights as $h$ shrinks, what happens to the two-sided limit?

choices.label: There is no single two-sided limit.
The two-sided limit must equal the average of the two heights.
The two-sided limit automatically equals the filled point.

items.explanation: A two-sided limit needs agreement from both sides. If the sides approach different heights, no single two-sided limit exists.
items.observationHint: Use a small $h$ and compare the two markers side by side.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the graph as a classification bench. The goal is to separate “the limit exists” from “the function is continuous.”

items.title: Limit exists, continuity fails
items.prompt: Switch to the case where the left-hand and right-hand values agree on one finite limit, but the function is still not continuous at $x = 0$.
items.successMessage: You found the removable-hole case. The limit exists, but the actual point does not match it.

setup.note: Start from the continuous case, then look for the smallest change that breaks continuity without breaking the limit.

hints: A jump is not the right case because the two sides stop agreeing.
You want the case where the limit guide stays the same but the actual point moves away.

targets.label: Select the removable-hole case by moving the behavior case to the band between $0.9$ and $1.1$.
Shrink the sample distance into the close-reading band from $0.1$ to $0.18$.

items.title: Unit Circle / Sine and Cosine from Rotation
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Set how quickly the point rotates around the circle. Faster rotation makes the angle graph steeper and the sine-cosine traces cycle faster.
Set the starting angle before time begins, which changes the starting point and the starting cosine and sine values.

presets.label: Reference turn
presets.description: A clean counterclockwise turn that shows the projection link clearly.

presets.label: Quadrant II start
presets.description: Start on the left-above side so cosine is negative while sine stays positive.
Start near the top of the circle so one projection is close to zero.

presets.label: Fast turn
presets.description: A quicker rotation makes the angle graph steeper and the traces cycle faster.

overlays.label: Projection guides
overlays.shortDescription: Drop perpendicular guides from the point to the x- and y-axes.
whatToNotice: The x-guide reads $\cos\theta$ and the y-guide reads $\sin\theta$.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the point, the coordinates, and the traces mean the same thing.

overlays.label: Angle marker
overlays.shortDescription: Show the current angle measured from the positive x-axis.
whatToNotice: The same angle sets the point on the circle and the markers on the graphs.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the geometry and the time graphs tied to one live angle.

overlays.label: Quadrant sign map
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the sign pattern for each quadrant visible.
whatToNotice: Left side means negative cosine, and below the x-axis means negative sine.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns sign changes into a geometry check instead of a memory trick.

overlays.label: Rotation trail
overlays.shortDescription: Show the arc swept from the positive x-axis to the current point.
whatToNotice: As the arc grows, the angle-history graph is tracking that same angle.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes time progression visible on the circle itself.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the circle, guides, and traces visible together so one moving point explains both graphs.

items.text: As the point moves left or right, the cosine trace changes with its x-coordinate because they are the same value.
Crossing the y-axis changes the sign of cosine first, and crossing the x-axis changes the sign of sine.

predictionMode.title: Predict the signs
predictionMode.intro: Predict the sign pattern before you change the starting angle, then check it on the circle and the traces.

items.prompt: If the point starts in Quadrant II, what signs should $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ have?

choices.label: Cosine should be negative while sine stays positive.
Both cosine and sine should be negative.
Cosine should stay positive while sine turns negative.

items.explanation: Quadrant II lies left of the y-axis but above the x-axis, so x is negative while y is positive.
items.observationHint: Watch the point begin on the upper-left arc: the x-projection is negative while the y-projection stays positive.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the live circle, the sign map, and the traces together. The goal is to catch a sign change from the geometry instead of from a memorized quadrant chart.

items.title: Catch the first sign flip
items.prompt: Start from the axis-crossing view, then move just into Quadrant II so cosine is negative while sine is still clearly positive. Keep the guides and sign map on so you can see exactly why the signs differ.
items.successMessage: You caught the first sign flip directly from the circle. The point location, sign map, and traces all agree on negative cosine and positive sine.

setup.note: Stay close to the top crossing so the x-coordinate has only just become negative.

hints: You only need to move a little past ninety degrees, not all the way to the middle of Quadrant II.
If cosine is still positive, the point has not crossed the y-axis yet.

targets.label: Set the starting angle between about $1.62$ and $1.78\,\mathrm{rad}$ so the point sits just into Quadrant II.

items.title: Inverse Trig / Angle from Ratio
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

controls.description: Moves the point closer to or farther from the origin along the same ray. At fixed angle, the ratio $y/x$ and the recovered direction stay the same.
Sets the direction of the point from the positive x-axis, which changes the quadrant and can change whether inverse tangent needs correction.

presets.label: First-quadrant ratio
presets.description: A safe case where the principal arctan output already matches the actual angle.

presets.label: Quadrant II warning
presets.description: A Quadrant II case where $\arctan(y/x)$ does not give the full direction by itself.

presets.label: Quadrant IV reference
presets.description: A Quadrant IV case with the same negative tangent sign as Quadrant II but a different actual direction.

overlays.label: Coordinate guides
overlays.shortDescription: Show the point's x- and y-coordinates on the axes.
whatToNotice: The ratio $y / x$ is built from these two coordinate values.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties inverse trig to an actual point on the plane instead of a bare calculator input.

overlays.label: Angle arc
overlays.shortDescription: Show the full angle from the positive x-axis to the current ray.
whatToNotice: Outside Quadrant I and Quadrant IV, the full angle can differ from the principal arctan output.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the actual direction visible when the ratio alone is ambiguous.

overlays.label: Radius sweep
overlays.shortDescription: Show all points at the current radius while the angle changes.
whatToNotice: Changing radius alone does not change direction, so the recovered angle stays fixed along one ray.

items.text: Changing radius stretches the point farther out or pulls it in along the same ray, so the direction and the recovered angle stay the same.
In Quadrant II, $y / x$ is negative, so $\arctan(y / x)$ returns a negative acute angle even though the actual point is above the x-axis on the left.

A positive acute angle in Quadrant I.
