items.title: Trig Identities from Unit-Circle Geometry
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Large translation packet. Translate in smaller chunks if using an external model.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

paragraphs: This bench keeps one rotating point, its horizontal and vertical projections, and the squared-projection graph visible together. That way, trig identities come from one piece of geometry instead of feeling like detached symbol rules.

On the unit circle, every point is exactly 1 unit from the origin, so $x^2 + y^2 = 1$ is always true. When you read the same point as $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$, that becomes $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$.

As the point moves through different quadrants, raw sine or cosine can change sign, but the squared sum does not change because the radius does not change. In the first quadrant, complementary angles swap the horizontal and vertical projections, so sine and cosine swap places too.

correction: The identities come from the geometry of one point on the unit circle.
If the point stays 1 unit from the origin, its horizontal and vertical projections must still satisfy $x^2 + y^2 = 1$, which becomes $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$.
A sign flip in sine or cosine does not break the identity, because the squared terms still come from the same fixed radius.

controls.description: Controls how quickly the point rotates around the unit circle. The identity stays true at every speed because the radius does not change.
Sets where the point starts before the live rotation begins, which changes the projection values without changing the unit-circle radius.

presets.description: A first-quadrant point near cosine 0.60 and sine 0.80 for a clean identity check.

presets.label: Complementary pair
presets.description: A first-quadrant angle where the horizontal and vertical projections are clearly different, so the complementary-angle swap is easier to notice.
A second-quadrant point where cosine is negative, sine is positive, and the squared sum still stays fixed.

overlays.label: Projection guides
overlays.shortDescription: Project the current point to the x- and y-axes so cosine and sine stay visible as lengths.
whatToNotice: The horizontal and vertical projections you read on the circle are the same values being squared on the identity graph.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the identity tied to visible geometry instead of detached notation.

overlays.label: Angle marker
overlays.shortDescription: Show the current angle from the positive x-axis.
whatToNotice: Changing $\theta$ moves the point and changes the projections, but it does not change the radius of the unit circle.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you see that the identity survives angle changes because the distance from the origin stays fixed.

overlays.label: Quadrant sign map
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the sign pattern for each quadrant visible while the point moves.
whatToNotice: Cosine or sine can change sign across quadrants, but the squared-sum line does not move.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates sign changes in the raw projections from the identity itself.

overlays.label: Rotation trail
overlays.shortDescription: Show the swept arc from the positive x-axis to the current point.
whatToNotice: The point keeps moving on the same radius-1 circle even while cosine and sine trade size.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the fixed distance from the origin visible.

graphs.label: Squared projections and their sum
graphs.description: Shows $\cos^2\theta$, $\sin^2\theta$, and their sum so you can see the two squared pieces trade size while the total stays at 1.

graphs.label: Cosine and sine traces
graphs.description: Shows the raw cosine and sine projections so the squared graph never loses touch with the original values.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the circle and the identity graph visible together. One moving point should explain both the raw projections and the squared sum.

items.text: The $\cos^2\theta$ and $\sin^2\theta$ traces trade size, but their sum stays at 1 because the point never leaves the unit circle.
Moving into Quadrant II flips the raw cosine sign first, but squaring removes that sign while keeping the same projection length in the identity.

predictionMode.title: Predict the identity
predictionMode.intro: Predict what happens to the identity before you move the point into a new quadrant.

items.prompt: If the point moves into Quadrant II while staying on the unit circle, what should happen to $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta$?

choices.label: It should stay equal to 1.
It should become less than 1 because cosine turns negative.
It should become greater than 1 because sine stays positive.

items.explanation: The point is still 1 unit from the origin, so the squared projections still add to 1. The sign change affects raw cosine, not the squared distance relation.
items.observationHint: Watch the raw projection traces and the squared-sum line together: the sign changes, but the line at 1 does not.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the live point, the projection guides, and the squared graph together. The goal is to build one angle that makes the identity visible, not just quote the formula.

items.title: 0.6-0.8 identity check
items.prompt: Start in Quadrant I and adjust the point until cosine is near 0.6 and sine is near 0.8. Keep the squared-projection graph open so you can check that the sum stays at 1 while you tune the angle.
items.successMessage: You built a clean 0.6-0.8 unit-circle case. The raw projections match the target, and the squared graph still locks their sum at 1.

setup.note: Aim for a first-quadrant point slightly steeper than 45 degrees.

hints: Stay in Quadrant I so both raw projections remain positive.
You want a point whose vertical projection is a bit larger than its horizontal projection.

targets.label: Set the starting angle between about 0.89 and 0.96 rad so cosine is near 0.6 and sine is near 0.8.

presets.label: Bubble on shuffled
presets.description: A useful baseline case where repeated scans, swaps, and rising cost are easy to follow.

whatToNotice: Watch whether the unsolved region shrinks from the left or the right as the run progresses.

overlays.label: Settled region
overlays.shortDescription: Mark the items the current algorithm already treats as fixed.
whatToNotice: Insertion usually grows a settled prefix, while bubble usually grows a settled suffix.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes each algorithm's local strategy visible on the list itself.

overlays.label: Pointer markers

overlays.whyItMatters: They reveal the decision process directly on the list instead of hiding it behind pseudocode.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice

items.text: With the same list size, a nearly sorted input can make insertion look efficient, while reverse order makes bubble do repeated sweeps and swaps.
All three algorithms reach the same sorted list, but the marker motion and counter lines reveal very different paths and costs.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Before switching setups, predict which cost pattern should stay lower and why.

items.prompt: If the list starts nearly sorted, which algorithm should usually produce the fewest writes on this bench?

choices.label: Insertion sort, because most items are already close to where they belong.
Bubble sort, because settling the right edge first should remove most of the work.
Selection sort, because fewer comparisons automatically means fewer writes.

items.explanation: Insertion sort can exploit short local fixes on a nearly sorted list, so it often reaches the same result with fewer writes.
items.observationHint: Open the nearly sorted insertion preset, then watch the write count rise slowly while the disorder line drops quickly.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Build a low-cost run for a reason, not by luck. Use the list and graph together.

items.title: Make insertion pay off
items.prompt: Create a run where insertion sort finishes a nearly sorted list with very few writes.
items.successMessage: You built the kind of case insertion sort handles well. Because the list began close to sorted, most items needed only short moves, so the write count stayed low.

targets.label: Keep algorithmindex between 2 and 2.
Keep patternindex between 1 and 1.
Keep completed between 1 and 1.
Keep writecount between 0 and 10.

items.title: Parametric Curves / Motion from Equations
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a parametric curve, a moving point, and controls for amplitudes, frequencies, and phase shift. The point moves through the same plane where the whole path is traced.
Keeping the full trace visible shows the geometric path, while the moving point and time trail show how that path is traversed over time.
One graph shows x(t) and y(t) together, so each time corresponds to one ordered pair on the plane.
A second graph shows the point's speed over time, which helps separate the shape of the path from how fast the point moves along it.

presets.description: A same-frequency ellipse that cleanly separates path shape from motion around it.

presets.label: Lissajous loop
presets.description: Different x and y frequencies create a loop that crosses itself.
The same frequency pair with a phase shift changes how the moving point lines up with the trace.

overlays.label: Full path
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the entire traced curve visible behind the moving point.
whatToNotice: The full trace shows the geometric path even when the point is currently somewhere else on it.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you separate the whole curve from the moment-by-moment position.

overlays.label: Time trail
overlays.shortDescription: Show the recent motion of the point along the curve.
whatToNotice: The trail can bunch up or spread out, showing that the point does not have to move at the same speed everywhere.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes traversal speed visible instead of hiding everything inside the shape alone.

overlays.label: Coordinate projections
overlays.shortDescription: Project the current point back to the x- and y-axes.
whatToNotice: The same point is being set by two time-dependent coordinate rules at once.
overlays.whyItMatters: It connects x(t) and y(t) to one visible point on the plane.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the full path, the moving point, and the time graphs visible together. The main job is to separate shape from motion.

items.text: Changing amplitudes changes the width and height of the traced path because it changes how far x and y can reach.
The point can speed up and slow down along the same geometric curve, so path shape and traversal speed are not the same idea.

predictionMode.title: Predict what changes
predictionMode.intro: Before you move a control, decide whether it should change the path, the motion along the path, or both.

items.prompt: If y amplitude increases while x amplitude stays fixed, what should happen to the traced path?

choices.label: The path should get taller without necessarily getting wider.
The path should always get wider.
The path should stay identical.

items.explanation: Increasing the y amplitude stretches the path vertically. It does not automatically make the path wider.
items.observationHint: Keep the full trace visible and compare the vertical reach before and after the change.

challengeMode.title: Shape versus motion checkpoint
challengeMode.intro: Build the shape first, then use the timing to catch the point in a specific part of the path.

items.title: Catch a fast point on a tall curve
items.prompt: Make the curve clearly taller than it is wide, then catch the point near the y-axis during a fast part of the motion.
items.successMessage: You separated shape from traversal successfully: the curve stayed tall while the point was caught near the y-axis during a fast part of the motion.

targets.label: Keep pathheight between 6.4 and 8.5.
Keep pathwidth between 2 and 5.4.
Keep horizontal position between -0.35 and 0.35.
Keep speed between 6.5 and 20.

items.title: Binary Search / Halving the Search Space
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.

presets.description: A gentle first case where the midpoint lands on or near the target and the marker logic is easy to read.
A larger case where the target sits near the far-right edge but the interval still shrinks quickly.

presets.label: Left-edge target
presets.description: A mirror case on the left edge, useful for checking that halving works in either direction.

overlays.label: Active interval
overlays.shortDescription: Highlight the positions that could still contain the target.
whatToNotice: After each midpoint check, one whole half disappears from the highlighted interval.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes halving visible on the list instead of leaving it as an abstract rule.

overlays.label: Low-mid-high markers
overlays.shortDescription: Show the current low, mid, and high positions on the ordered list.
whatToNotice: low and high mark the surviving interval, and mid is the one position you test next.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links each update of low or high to the visible search space.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the ordered list and one graph visible together, then follow what low, mid, and high do after each check.

items.text: The key move is not reading faster. It is using the midpoint to rule out half of the remaining positions at once.
The midpoint only tells you which half to keep because every value is already in sorted order.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Predict which half survives before you advance the search.

items.prompt: If the target value is larger than the current midpoint value, which side of the interval should remain active?

choices.label: Keep the right half, so low moves to just above mid.
Keep the left half, so high moves to just below mid.
Keep both halves, because one midpoint is not enough information.

items.explanation: In an ordered list, everything left of mid is too small when the midpoint value is already too small.
items.observationHint: Open the far-right target setup and watch low jump past mid while the interval window keeps only the right half.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Build a case where halving, not luck, explains the small number of checks.

items.title: Find an edge target fast
items.prompt: Create a large ordered list with the target near the far-right edge, but still find it in five checks or fewer.
items.successMessage: You built the right kind of scene. Even with the target near the edge, binary search stayed efficient because each failed midpoint cut the remaining interval roughly in half.

targets.label: Keep arraysize between 18 and 20.
Keep targetindex between 15 and 19.
Keep found between 1 and 1.
Keep comparisons between 0 and 5.

items.title: Concentration and Dilution
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a beaker with dissolved particles, a concentration readout, and controls for solute amount and solvent volume.
The overlays help the learner compare how much solute is present with how much liquid it is spread through.
One graph shows how concentration changes with solvent volume, and a second shows how concentration changes with solute amount.
Comparing the two graphs helps separate dilution from simply adding more solute.

presets.description: Lots of solute packed into a smaller solvent volume.

presets.label: Diluted same solute
presets.description: The same solute amount spread through more liquid, so the concentration is lower.

presets.label: More solute, same volume
presets.description: A higher concentration caused by adding more solute to the same liquid volume.

overlays.label: Density cue
overlays.shortDescription: Show how crowded the same solute looks in different volumes.
whatToNotice: When the solute amount stays the same, adding more solvent makes the particles look more spread out.
overlays.whyItMatters: It connects the concentration number to how crowded the dissolved particles really are.

overlays.label: Amount-volume guide
overlays.shortDescription: Keep solute amount and solvent volume visible side by side.
whatToNotice: Adding solvent and adding solute can both change concentration, but they change different parts of the mixture.
overlays.whyItMatters: It helps you see why dilution means changing volume, not changing the amount of solute.

overlays.label: Particle motion
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the particle drift visible inside the beaker.
whatToNotice: The particles are still moving even when the concentration readout is steady.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the beaker feeling like a live mixture rather than a static picture.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice in the beaker
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the beaker, the amount-volume guide, and a concentration graph visible together.

items.text: Increase solvent volume while keeping solute amount fixed. The concentration should fall because the same particles are spread through more liquid.
Increase solute amount while keeping solvent volume fixed. The concentration rises because there is more dissolved material in the same amount of liquid.

predictionMode.title: Predict before you dilute
predictionMode.intro: Predict what the concentration will do before you add solvent or solute.

items.prompt: If solvent volume increases while solute amount stays fixed, what should happen to the concentration?

choices.label: The concentration should drop because the same solute is now spread through more liquid.
The concentration should rise because the beaker is fuller.
Nothing should change.

items.explanation: Dilution lowers concentration because the same solute is spread through a larger volume of liquid.
items.observationHint: Watch the concentration readout fall while the particle view looks more spread out.

challengeMode.title: Challenge: dilute without losing solute
challengeMode.intro: Use dilution honestly: lower concentration by adding solvent, not by quietly changing to a different solute amount.

items.prompt: Start from a crowded beaker and make it clearly less concentrated while keeping the solute amount close to the starting value.
items.successMessage: You diluted the mixture without switching to a different solute case. The concentration fell because the same dissolved material occupied more liquid.

targets.label: Keep soluteamount between 10.5 and 11.5.
Keep solventvolume between 1.8 and 2.6.
Keep concentration between 3.6 and 5.6.

items.title: Kepler's Third Law and Orbital Periods
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.

controls.description: Changes the central mass and therefore the gravity that sets the circular speed.
Sets the dashed circular target whose speed and period are being compared.
1.00 means the live speed matches the circular speed. Away from 1.00, the path stops being the circular orbit whose period the law predicts.

whatToNotice: Kepler's law here is about staying on this dashed circular path, not about paths that peel away from it.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the chosen circular year separate from cases that are too slow or too fast to remain circular.

whatToNotice: The same radius setting that makes the circle wider also changes the allowed circular speed and the period.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the geometry of the larger-versus-smaller orbit visible while you compare timing consequences.

whatToNotice: Wider circular orbits have shorter velocity vectors because the allowed circular speed is lower.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the slower-part of the longer-year story visible instead of hiding it in a formula only.

whatToNotice: This same inward gravity is what makes wider circular orbits slower and heavier-source circular orbits faster.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the timing law tied to the actual force picture instead of making period feel detached from gravity.

whatToNotice: At the circular match, the trail keeps tracing one stable circle. Away from that match, the path is no longer the circular orbit whose period Kepler's law describes.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows whether you are still looking at the circular case or have already left it.

items.title: Pitch, Frequency, and Loudness / Intensity
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.

whatToNotice: Pitch and loudness are both being read from the same sound wave.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the sound model honest while you focus on pitch and loudness.

whatToNotice: Higher frequency packs the compression pattern closer together when the medium speed is fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It ties pitch to the real spacing and timing of the sound wave.

whatToNotice: Larger amplitude increases the energy-transfer cue even when pitch stays fixed.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps loudness tied to stronger driving rather than to faster cycling.

items.title: Simple Harmonic Motion
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.

paragraphs: Simple harmonic motion is motion in which the acceleration always points back toward equilibrium and grows with displacement. Move the mass to one side and the restoring acceleration points toward the center, trying to pull it back.
The mass does not stop at equilibrium because its speed is greatest there. It passes through the center, overshoots, and the same restoring rule slows it down, reverses it, and repeats the cycle.

The stage and graphs show the same instant. At a turning point, displacement is largest, velocity is zero, and the restoring acceleration has its largest magnitude. At an equilibrium crossing, displacement and acceleration are zero while speed is largest.

Use the controls one idea at a time. Amplitude sets the turning points and the energy scale, Angular frequency changes how quickly the cycle repeats, and Phase changes only the starting point in the cycle.

items.title: Acid-Base / pH Intuition
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one container of solution, a pH strip, bars for acid and base influence, and sliders for acid amount, base amount, and water volume.
Changing a slider updates the current pH and the visible balance of the mixture.
One graph shows pH against acid amount, and the other shows pH against base amount.
Use them to compare how each control pushes the mixture away from or toward neutral.

whatToNotice: When the acid bar is larger, the pH marker should sit lower; when the base bar is larger, it should sit higher.
Adding water can reduce the size of the mismatch without changing which side is larger.

targets.label: Keep ph between 6.6 and 7.4.
Keep acidshare between 0.45 and 0.55.
Keep baseshare between 0.45 and 0.55.

items.title: Buoyancy and Archimedes' Principle
notes: Missing fields currently inherit from optimized English at runtime.
Preserve placeholders, LaTeX, and protected tokens exactly in the exported fields.

keyIdeas: Archimedes' principle says the buoyant force equals the weight of the displaced fluid, so displaced volume is the key quantity to track.
While the block is only partly submerged, pushing it deeper increases displaced volume and therefore increases the buoyant force.
A freely floating block settles where weight and buoyant force balance, so the required submerged fraction is the density ratio $\rho_{obj}/\rho_f$.
If $\rho_{obj} > \rho_f$, even full submersion cannot provide enough buoyant force, so the block sinks.
Once the whole block is submerged in one uniform fluid, moving it deeper raises both the top and bottom pressures together but does not change their difference, so the buoyant force stays the same.
